Reduced crop yields in some regions. Increased disease spread. Mass human migration from affected areas.
What is an ecosystem and how do its components interact?

A community of living organisms (biotic: plants, animals, decomposers) interacting with non-living elements (abiotic: soil, water, temperature, light). Energy flows through food chains from producers to consumers. Nutrients are recycled by decomposers. Change to one component ripples through the whole system.
What is a food chain and what do the arrows represent?

A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another. Arrow direction = direction of energy transfer (e.g. grass → rabbit → fox). Producers (plants) form the base. Primary consumers eat producers. Secondary consumers eat primary consumers. Energy is lost at each stage as heat and waste.
What is the nutrient cycle and how does it work?

Nutrients are taken up from the soil by producers. Passed through consumers in the food chain. Returned to soil by decomposers breaking down dead organic matter. Key inputs: rainfall (leaches nutrients in), weathering. Key outputs: runoff, burning. The cycle is not perfect — deforestation breaks it.
What is the difference between biotic and abiotic components?

Biotic: living parts of an ecosystem — producers (plants), consumers (animals), decomposers (fungi, bacteria). Abiotic: non-living — temperature, rainfall, soil type, light levels, aspect. Both must be balanced for an ecosystem to function. Changing one component affects all others.
What are the global biomes and their distribution?

Tropical rainforest (5°N–5°S, high rainfall year-round). Savanna (tropical grassland, seasonal rainfall). Hot desert (20-30° N/S, less than 250mm/yr rain). Temperate grassland. Deciduous/temperate forest. Boreal (taiga) forest. Tundra (Arctic fringe, permafrost). Distribution determined by temperature and rainfall (climate).
What is urbanisation and what causes it?

The increase in the proportion of people living in urban areas. Caused by rural-to-urban migration (push: lack of jobs/services in rural areas; pull: jobs/education/healthcare in cities) and natural population increase.
What is a squatter settlement and what are its characteristics?

Illegal self-built housing on land people don't own, on city outskirts in LICs/NEEs. Lack clean water, sewage, electricity, healthcare. High density, poor housing quality, high unemployment. Also called favelas (Brazil) or shanty towns.
How can squatter settlements be improved?

Site and service schemes (government provides land, residents build). Self-help schemes (residents improve homes with support). Local authority improvements (utilities, paved roads). Redevelopment — though this may displace residents.
What are the social, economic and environmental challenges of rapid urban growth in LIC cities?

Social: overcrowding, crime, poor healthcare/education. Economic: informal economy, unemployment, inequality. Environmental: air and water pollution, waste disposal issues, traffic congestion, lack of green space.
What urban transport problems exist and how can they be managed?

Problems: traffic congestion, air pollution, long commutes. Solutions: bus rapid transit (BRT), metro/light rail, park and ride, congestion charging, cycling infrastructure, integrated transport systems.
London's population is {{c1::9.7 million}} (2023) and is set to become a megacity by {{c2::2030}}. Air pollution causes {{c3::4,000}} premature deaths per year.
In deprived areas like {{c1::Newham}}, recycling rates are as low as {{c2::14%}} compared to {{c3::32%}} for the rest of London.
What strategies make London more sustainable?

Crossrail (1.5 million more people within 45 mins of centre). 100+ electric buses. 9 super-cycle highways (£770m). Boris Bikes. Congestion charge. Queen Elizabeth Olympic Park regeneration. Brownfield development.

The {{c1::Queen Elizabeth Olympic Park}} (Stratford, East London) was built for the {{c2::2012}} London Olympics. It regenerated one of London's most deprived areas — {{c3::Newham}}, which had {{c4::30%}} unemployment before the games.
What were the social and economic benefits of the Olympic Park regeneration?

20,000 new homes planned. 8,000 permanent jobs created. New facilities: Aquatics Centre, Velodrome, London Stadium — all open to public. New schools and healthcare facilities. Stratford International station improved transport links. Westfield Shopping Centre opened — largest urban shopping centre in EU.
What are the criticisms of the Olympic Park regeneration?

Original residents and businesses displaced during construction. House prices rose, pricing out local people (gentrification). Many new homes unaffordable for existing community. Some promised affordable housing targets not met. Benefits mainly attracted wealthier newcomers rather than improving lives of existing residents.

The Olympic Park is an example of {{c1::brownfield}} regeneration — built on a former {{c2::industrial}} site. It is part of the wider {{c3::Thames Gateway}} regeneration project, one of Europe's largest urban renewal schemes.

Newham (deprived east London) vs Kensington (wealthy west London): life expectancy {{c1::75}} vs {{c2::83}}. GCSE passes {{c3::22%}} lower in Newham. {{c4::26%}} of Newham homes earn under £15,000. Unemployment in Newham is {{c5::9.4%}}.

London Docklands regeneration began in {{c1::1981}}. Cost: {{c2::£1.86 billion}} public money + {{c3::£7.7 billion}} private investment. Created {{c4::85,000}} jobs and {{c5::24,000}} new homes. {{c6::Canary Wharf}} was built employing 100,000 in international banking.
What are greenfield vs brownfield sites and the trade-offs?

Greenfield: undeveloped (farmland, forests) — cheaper, more space, easier to build. Loses biodiversity and agricultural land. Brownfield: previously built-on land — more expensive (demolition/decontamination needed), limited space, but better for sustainability and closer to existing infrastructure.
What is the Demographic Transition Model (DTM)?

Shows how birth and death rates change as a country develops. Stage 1: high BR, high DR. Stage 2: falling DR (healthcare improves). Stage 3: falling BR (education/contraception). Stage 4: low BR, low DR. Stage 5: BR may fall below DR.
What is HDI and what are its limitations?

Human Development Index measures development using life expectancy, education and income (GNI per capita). Limitations: ignores inequality within countries, doesn't measure happiness, freedom, gender equality or environmental quality.
What are the causes of uneven development?

Physical: landlocked, poor soil, extreme climate. Economic: debt, unfair trade terms, lack of investment. Historical: colonialism — resources extracted, economies distorted, infrastructure built for export not local development.
What is a TNC and what are its advantages and disadvantages for LICs?

Transnational corporation — operates in multiple countries (e.g. Shell, Nike). Advantages: jobs, investment, technology transfer, infrastructure. Disadvantages: profits sent to HIC HQ, low wages, poor conditions, environmental damage.
What are the development indicators and their limitations?

GNI per capita (income, ignores inequality). Infant mortality per 1,000 live births. Life expectancy. People per doctor. Literacy rates. Access to safe water. HDI (composite — life expectancy, education, income). Limitations: country averages hide internal inequality. Don't measure happiness, freedom, gender equality or environmental quality.

DTM Stage examples: Stage 1 (high BR/DR) = {{c1::remote tribes}}. Stage 2 (falling DR) = {{c2::Kenya}} 1950s. Stage 3 (falling BR) = {{c3::India}}. Stage 4 (low BR/DR) = {{c4::UK/USA}}. Stage 5 (BR below DR, natural decrease) = {{c5::Japan/Germany}}.
What are the causes of uneven development?

Physical: landlocked countries, poor soils, extreme climates. Economic: unfair trade terms (LICs export raw materials cheaply, import expensive goods). Historical: colonialism — resources extracted, economies distorted for colonial benefit, borders drawn disrupting communities. Political instability discourages foreign investment.
What are the impacts of economic development in Nigeria?
Positive: oil revenue grew economy, expanding middle class, improved infrastructure. Negative: oil pollution in Niger Delta, inequality, corruption, over-dependence on oil, environmental destruction.

Nigeria is classified as an {{c1::NEE}} (Newly Emerging Economy). It gained independence from {{c2::Britain}} in {{c3::1960}}. Its main export is {{c4::oil}}. Main trading partner is now {{c5::China}}, having shifted from the UK.
What is Nigeria's global and regional importance?

Most populous country in Africa (~220 million). Largest economy in Africa by GDP. Contains 80% of West Africa's oil reserves. Member of OPEC. Growing middle class and consumer market. Lagos is West Africa's financial hub. Political instability and corruption remain challenges.

Shell's role in Nigeria: operates in the {{c1::Niger Delta}} and contributes to the economy through {{c2::oil revenues}}. However oil spills have polluted {{c3::farmland and rivers}}. Profits largely return to {{c4::Shell's HQ}} in the Netherlands. Local communities receive {{c5::little}} benefit.
What are the environmental and human impacts of Nigeria's economic development?

Environmental: oil spills in Niger Delta, deforestation, air/water pollution from industrial growth. Human: growing middle class (improving quality of life), but 50%+ live below poverty line. Healthcare and education improving in cities. Rural-urban inequality widening. Youth unemployment high — contributes to crime and instability.
What is the north-south divide in the UK and how is it being addressed?
Economic output and wealth concentrated in London and South East. North, Midlands and Wales lag behind. Solutions: Northern Powerhouse, HS2, Local Enterprise Partnerships, freeports.
How has the UK's employment structure changed?

Primary sector (farming, mining) declined sharply. Secondary (manufacturing) declined after deindustrialisation. Tertiary (services) and quaternary (research/tech) grew significantly and now dominate.
Crossrail added {{c1::1.5 million}} people within {{c2::45 minutes}} of central London. The government spent {{c3::£770 million}} on super-cycle highways.
Why has the UK moved towards a post-industrial economy?

Deindustrialisation: manufacturing cheaper abroad (globalisation). Government policies encouraged service sector growth. Growth of IT and digital economy (Silicon Roundabout in Shoreditch). Science parks (Cambridge — links with universities, research). Business parks (Cobalt Business Park, Newcastle — out-of-town, cheap land, good transport links).

Nissan Sunderland: employs {{c1::7,000}} directly and {{c2::28,000}} in associated industries. Produces {{c3::500,000}} cars per year. Contributes approximately {{c4::3%}} of UK GDP. Example of how manufacturing can still support the UK economy, but vulnerable to trade deal changes.
What are enterprise zones and how do they address the north-south divide?

Areas where businesses pay reduced taxes and face fewer planning restrictions to attract investment. Examples in north/Midlands aim to create jobs and stimulate local economies. Other strategies: Northern Powerhouse, HS2, Local Enterprise Partnerships, Assisted Areas, Freeports in northern cities.
What are the UK's links with the wider world?

Trade: exports financial services, manufactured goods, creative industries. EU: largest trading partner (post-Brexit changes terms). Commonwealth: historical, cultural and economic ties with 56 nations. Cultural: English language, BBC, arts. Transport: major international air hub (Heathrow), shipping. Electronic communication: global financial centre.
What is food security and what threatens it?

When all people have access to sufficient, safe and nutritious food. Threatened by: population growth, climate change, water scarcity, extreme weather, conflict, biofuels displacing food crops, food waste and unequal distribution.
What is water insecurity and what causes it?

Unreliable access to safe water. Caused by: physical water scarcity (dry climate), economic scarcity (lack of infrastructure), pollution, population growth, climate change altering rainfall, over-extraction of groundwater.
What is energy insecurity and what are its consequences?

Unreliable or inadequate access to energy. Consequences: economic (businesses can't operate), social (no heating/healthcare), political (conflicts over resources), environmental (illegal deforestation for biomass).
What are the advantages and disadvantages of renewable energy?

Advantages: no CO₂ emissions, sustainable, reduces energy insecurity. Disadvantages: intermittent (wind/solar unreliable), high upfront costs, visual impact, some habitat damage (e.g. hydroelectric dams flooding valleys).
What is the difference between large-scale and small-scale water management?

Large-scale: dams, reservoirs, water transfer schemes. Small-scale/appropriate tech: rainwater harvesting, drip irrigation, well digging, greywater recycling — more accessible for LICs and rural communities.
What is the UK's water supply problem and how is it managed?

South-east has a water deficit (high population, low rainfall). North/Wales have a surplus. Solutions: water transfer schemes (e.g. Kielder Reservoir, Northumberland — largest artificial lake in UK, can transfer water south). Grey water recycling, water meters, fixing leaky pipes (25% lost to leaks in UK).
How can food supply be made more sustainable?

Organic farming (no artificial pesticides). Permaculture (mimics natural ecosystems). Irrigation management (drip irrigation reduces waste). GM crops (higher yields, pest resistance). Reducing food waste. Buying local (fewer food miles). Fairtrade — better returns to farmers in LICs. Urban agriculture. Reducing meat consumption.

Global food insecurity is concentrated in {{c1::Sub-Saharan Africa}} and {{c2::South Asia}}. The UK has high food security but growing demand for {{c3::high-value}} food exports from LICs and seasonal food year-round. This increases {{c4::food miles}} and carbon footprints.
What is agribusiness and how does it contrast with organic farming?

Agribusiness: large-scale commercial farming using machinery, chemicals, fertilisers and GM crops — maximises yield and profit. Organic: no artificial chemicals, smaller scale, more sustainable, higher welfare — but more expensive, lower yields. UK trend towards agribusiness increases food availability but concerns over biodiversity loss and soil health.

Chambamontera is a remote community in {{c1::Peru}} in the {{c2::Andes Mountains}}, more than {{c3::two hours}} from the closest town of {{c4::Jaén}}. Nearly half the population lives on just {{c5::US$2}} a day.

The Chambamontera micro-hydro scheme was built with help from the charity {{c1::Practical Action}}. Total cost: {{c2::US$51,000}}, funded by the {{c3::government}}, {{c4::Japan}}, and the local community. Each family paid an average of {{c5::US$750}}.
Why was Chambamontera suited to a micro-hydro scheme?

High rainfall, steep terrain and fast-flowing rivers made water power ideal. The challenging geography (steep slopes up to 1,700m altitude, roads closed in winter) made a traditional electricity grid economically impractical.
What are the social benefits of the Chambamontera micro-hydro scheme?

Students can study after dark (improved school facilities). Healthcare improved through refrigeration of medicines. Safer nighttime mobility via street lights. Reduced rural-to-urban migration — local population has grown. Less reliance on kerosene reduces fire risk.
What are the economic and environmental benefits of the Chambamontera micro-hydro scheme?

Coffee processing machinery can now be used (business growth). Renewable energy with minimal maintenance costs. Negligible environmental footprint. Less wood used for heating — reduces deforestation and soil erosion. Flood risk reduced by regulating river flow. Scheme lifespan of at least 25 years.
Why is Chambamontera an example of appropriate/small-scale technology?

It uses local workforce and materials. Low maintenance and running costs. Affordable for an LIC community (with credit options). Self-sufficient — the community manages it themselves. Sustainable, renewable energy source suited to the local environment. Contrasts with large-scale HIC solutions like dams or nuclear power.
What are the social opportunities created by rapid urbanisation in Lagos?

Life expectancy in Lagos is 70 vs 55 for Nigeria overall. Only 152/1,000 under-5s die in Lagos vs 201/1,000 nationally. Over 150 schools in Lagos. 94.3% of children receive a primary education. 600,000 people migrate to Lagos each year seeking these opportunities.
What are the economic opportunities created by rapid urbanisation in Lagos?

80% of Nigeria's international trade passes through Lagos. Nissan opened an assembly factory in 2010 employing 2,500 people. 40% of people work in the informal sector. Lagos produces 25 megawatts of energy from organic waste biofuel. Eko Atlantic project creates thousands of construction jobs.
What are the challenges of water, sanitation and energy in Lagos?

Only 1 in 10 residents has access to clean piped water. Street vendors sell bottled water (informal sector). Average resident has electricity for only 3 hours a day. In Makoko, 15 families share 1 toilet. 88% of sewage goes into open sewers/rivers. Diseases like cholera, diarrhoea and dysentery are common.
What are the environmental and crime challenges in Lagos?

Only 40% of Lagos waste is collected. Olusosun Dump processes 3,000 tonnes of rubbish by hand daily — burning copper wires releases carcinogens. Air pollution is 5× recommended limits. Road fatalities are 3× the EU rate. Gangs ('Area Boys') extort money. 29% of young men are unemployed.
What is Makoko and what are the problems there?

Informal settlement of around 1 million people built on stilts over Lagos Lagoon. People move around by canoe. No formal sanitation — waste goes directly into the water. High crime rates. Very poor housing quality. Lagos Lagoon is heavily polluted by waste and oil. Extreme overcrowding.
What was the Makoko Floating School and why was it significant?

Built for $6,500 using recycled materials. 100 students. 250 barrels collected rainwater reducing cholera risk. Solar panels provided electricity. Lower floor used for repairing fishing nets improving food security. Rising design meant it could float with sea level rise. Built by locals — self-sustaining model. Destroyed by storm in 2016 — not long-term.

Tunisia tourism: {{c1::6.2 million}} tourists visited in 2013. GDP rose from {{c2::$0.9 billion}} (1962) to {{c3::$47.6 billion}} (2014). Tourism supported {{c4::370,000}} jobs in 2009. Life expectancy rose from {{c5::42}} (1960s) to {{c6::75}}. Literacy from {{c7::66%}} (1995) to {{c8::79%}}.
How has tourism reduced the development gap in Tunisia?

Tourists spend in hotels, restaurants, shops — multiplier effect creates formal jobs. Government taxes tourist income to fund schools, hospitals, infrastructure. Foreign investment encouraged. Girls' education now compulsory (families can afford it). BUT: environmental problems (raw sewage from hotels), terrorist attack 2015 crippled income temporarily, international companies take profits abroad.
What are the advantages and disadvantages of extracting fossil fuels (Trans-Alaska Pipeline)?

Advantages: $2.2bn from mineral extraction, 79,000 jobs from oil/fishing, energy security for USA. Disadvantages: Exxon Valdez oil spill (1989) — 1,000 miles of coastline damaged. Pipeline disrupts caribou migration. Risk of permafrost melting destabilising infrastructure. Habitat damage. Long-term sustainability questioned.
What are the advantages and disadvantages of fracking?

Advantages: increases domestic energy supply, cheaper gas prices, creates jobs, reduces energy insecurity. Disadvantages: water contamination risk, methane leaks (potent GHG), causes minor earthquakes (UK fracking suspended after tremors), landscape damage, public opposition. UK government placed moratorium on fracking in 2019.

Sheringham's physical enquiry question (learn word for word): '{{c1::How effective are the groynes at Sheringham?}}' Human enquiry question (learn word for word): '{{c2::To what extent does tourism impact Sheringham?}}'
Why was Sheringham a suitable fieldwork location?

Close to school (reliable — can return). Known coastal defences to measure and compare with undefended areas (valid). Tourist destination for human data. Secondary data on Wroxham Crag erosion available. Accessible beach for safety.
What are the four key fieldwork quality words?

Validity — conclusions answer the enquiry question. Precision — how detailed/specific the data is. Reliability — results are repeatable and consistent. Accuracy — equipment used correctly and findings reflect reality.
What are the three sampling strategies?

Systematic — regular intervals (e.g. every 2m down the groyne). Random — removes unconscious bias (e.g. random number generator). Stratified — divides into sub-groups then samples randomly within each.
How was groyne effectiveness measured at Sheringham?
A tape measure placed at the top of the groyne, measuring down to the top of sediment every 2m. Shows how longshore drift builds sediment on one side. Repeated on multiple groynes for reliability.
How was longshore drift speed measured at Sheringham?
10m distance marked along the beach. Orange thrown into sea and timed over 10m. Speed = distance ÷ time (m/s). Repeated three times and averaged to improve reliability.
How was tourism impact measured at Sheringham?

Retail diversity survey on the High Street — recording type of each shop (tourist vs local). Distance from each shop to sea wall measured by trundle wheel. Environmental Quality Survey (EQS) at midpoint of High Street.
What are the limitations of Sheringham data collection?

Footsteps inaccurate for distance (step length varies). Tape measure on groyne may not be held straight. Orange drift gives point-in-time data only (tidal conditions vary). Day's weather affects wave type and LSD speed.
How do you answer 'Explain why the chosen location was suitable for data collection'? [2 marks]

State the location. Link to the enquiry question. Explain how it allows valid/reliable/accurate data. E.g. 'Sheringham was close to school meaning we could return to collect more groyne sediment data, making results more reliable.'
How do you answer 'Suggest one reason why risk assessment was important'? [2 marks]

Name a specific risk. Give magnitude and likelihood. State the control measure. E.g. 'Wet shingle could cause slips (magnitude 3, likelihood 4) so we wore suitable footwear, reducing the risk of injury.'

Sheringham key statistics — learn all: Rock type: {{c1::Wroxham Crag}}. Erosion rate without defences: {{c2::2.5 m/year}}. Number of groynes: {{c3::19}} ({{c4::4}} made of rock, rest wood). Sea wall length: {{c5::1300 m}}. 1995 maintenance cost: {{c6::£3.4 million}}. Current erosion since defences: {{c7::0 m}}. 2013 storm damage cost: {{c8::£804,000}}.

Sheringham key statistics continued: Tourism value to Norfolk coastline: {{c1::£3 billion}}+. Happisburgh retreat 2004–2007: {{c2::20 m}}. Compensation offered to Happisburgh residents: {{c3::40%}} of house value. LSD speed (unprotected beach): {{c4::0.55 m/s}}. LSD speed (protected — between groynes): {{c5::0.17 m/s}}. Simpson's Diversity Index for High Street: {{c6::0.80}}.
Why was coastal management needed at Sheringham?

Sheringham is built on Wroxham Crag — a soft, easily eroded rock that erodes at 2.5 m per year if undefended.
Sheringham relies heavily on tourism (worth £3bn+ to the Norfolk coastline) — the beach must remain accessible and attractive.
Without defences, cliff retreat would threaten the High Street businesses, homes and infrastructure.
Happisburgh (further along the coast, with no defences until recently) shows what happens when protection is not provided — retreating 20 m in just 3 years (2004–2007).
What coastal defences are in place at Sheringham and what does each do?

19 groynes (4 rock, rest wood): trap sediment moved by longshore drift → builds up the beach → acts as a buffer absorbing wave energy.
Sea wall (1300 m long, angled/curved): hard barrier blocking waves. Curved face reflects wave energy. Cost £3.4m to maintain in 1995.
Rip-rap/rock armour: large boulders in front of sea wall cause waves to break early, reducing energy hitting the wall. Small gaps create crab habitats.
Gabions: rocks in wire cages on top of sea wall — prevents cliff collapse behind.
Beach nourishment: pebbles added to widen beach. Beach reprofiling done every few years (LSD piles it against groynes).
How effective have Sheringham's defences been? What problems remain?

Effective: erosion has been reduced to 0 m since defences built (compared to 2.5 m/year undefended). Groynes have built a larger beach → more tourism. Investment has protected millions of pounds of property and businesses.
Problems: In December 2013 a storm surge caused £804,000 of damage to the defences. Defences are so expensive that not everywhere can be protected — Happisburgh received nothing for years. Building defences in summer disrupts tourist season. Climate change and rising sea levels mean future storms will be more frequent/severe. Seasonal employment means some businesses only profit in summer.
What is longshore drift and how do groynes affect it?

Longshore drift: waves approach the shore at an angle (the angle of prevailing winds). Swash carries sediment up the beach at that angle. Backwash carries it straight back down (gravity). Net movement = sediment transported along the coast.
Groynes interrupt LSD: they act as barriers perpendicular to the shore, trapping sediment on the updrift side — building the beach. Sediment cannot pass the groyne → less sediment reaches downdrift areas (sediment starvation).
At Sheringham: LSD travels from west to east (evidenced by sediment piled on the west side of each groyne). Our orange experiment showed LSD velocity was 0.17 m/s (protected) vs 0.55 m/s (unprotected) — showing groynes do slow it.
What is a constructive wave and what is a destructive wave?

Constructive waves: 7–10 waves per minute. Long wavelength, low height. Strong swash, weak backwash → deposit sediment, build beach.
Destructive waves: 10–15 waves per minute. Short wavelength, high height. Weak swash, strong backwash → erode and remove sediment.
At Sheringham: wave count used to determine type. Counted over 1 minute, repeated 3 times for reliability. Destructive waves are more common at Sheringham — hence the need for groynes to trap sediment the waves would otherwise remove.
Describe the human data collection method at Sheringham in full.

Retail diversity land use survey:
Starting at the sea front, walked along the right-hand pavement towards the coach park. For each shop passed: recorded type of shop (in one of predefined categories: food, café, souvenir, charity etc.) and measured distance from sea wall (using footsteps).
Crossed road at car park end, repeated back towards the sea (opposite pavement).
EQS (Environmental Quality Survey): completed at 3 points (sea wall end, middle, car park end). Scored categories (litter, traffic noise, building quality etc.) on a scale to get a total score for each location.
Sampling type: systematic (every shop recorded) + stratified (chose High Street specifically as most relevant to tourism).
Describe the physical data collection methods at Sheringham in full.

1. Sediment height measurement (groyne survey):
Place tape measure at top of groyne. Measure distance down to sediment surface every 2 m along the groyne. Repeat on both sides (east and west). Short distance = more sediment. Shows which side LSD is depositing on.
2. Wave count:
Count waves breaking per minute. Repeat x3 for average. Destructive = 10–15/min, constructive = 7–10/min.
3. LSD orange experiment:
Mark 10 m along beach. Throw orange into sea. Time how long for it to travel 10 m (3 readings, averaged). Speed = 10m ÷ time. Done in protected AND unprotected areas for comparison.
What were the actual results of the Sheringham physical enquiry?

LSD direction: sediment piled on west side of groynes → LSD moving west to east. (Orange also confirmed east-to-west movement on the day — an anomaly: Sheringham sits at a drift divide where direction changes seasonally.)
LSD speed: unprotected beach = 0.55 m/s; protected (between groynes) = 0.17 m/s. Groynes reduced LSD speed by ~69%.
Wave type: destructive waves (>10 per minute).
Sediment height: west side of groynes consistently had shorter measurement (more sediment piled up) than east side — confirms west-to-east LSD.
What were the actual results of the Sheringham human enquiry?

Retail diversity land use (pie chart): showed a variety of shop types, but 'other' was the largest category — suggesting 9 predefined categories were not precise enough.
Simpson's Diversity Index: D = 0.80 (scale 0–1; 1 = complete diversity, 0 = all same). 0.80 = fairly diverse — Sheringham not overly reliant on tourism, though tourist-related shops are present.
Dispersion graph: tourist shops (cafés, souvenirs, arcades) clustered near the sea wall (beach end) and near the car park — targeting tourist footfall at both entry points.
EQS: environmental quality tended to be better away from the beach — sea wall area showed more litter/wear from tourism.
What data presentation methods were used for the human enquiry and why?

Pie chart: shows proportion of each shop type as % of total → easy to see if tourism dominates. Disadvantage: doesn't show location of shops along High Street.
Dispersion graph (distance from sea wall on y-axis, shop type on x-axis): shows clustering of types by location → reveals which types are near beach (tourist) vs car park. Better than pie chart for showing spatial patterns.
Simpson's Diversity Index (D = 0.80): statistical test → mathematically proves how diverse (not just visual impression). Allows objective comparison.
Radar graph (EQS): shows score for each quality category at each location simultaneously → easy to spot which categories are low at each site. Bar graph of total EQS allows direct comparison between sites.
What data presentation methods were used for the physical enquiry and why?

Bar graph (LSD rate): simple, clear comparison of LSD speed in protected vs unprotected areas → immediately shows whether groynes slow LSD. Disadvantage: doesn't show spatial location of measurements.
Located bar graphs (bar graphs overlaid on a map/satellite image): improvement over plain bar graphs — shows each groyne's position relative to town. Explains anomalies: groynes closer to town may have been affected by beach reprofiling or greater human disturbance. Missing bars (where water covered sediment) are explained by location.
Why located bar graphs are better: distance from Sheringham town centre explains the trends — less management = more extreme natural patterns further out.
What are the limitations of the human data collection methods at Sheringham?

Footsteps for distance: inaccurate — step length varies between people and over time → data imprecise, limits reliability of dispersion graph.
Only 9 predefined shop categories: 'other' was the largest category → data not precise enough → reliability of SDI questioned → conclusions may not be valid.
Only surveyed the High Street: biased — tourist shops most likely concentrated on High Street (route from car park to beach). Local shops on other streets missed → results may not represent all of Sheringham → limits validity.
One day in summer: temporal limitation — shops may change (open/close) in winter. EQS subjective — different people score differently → reduces reliability.
What are the limitations of the physical data collection methods at Sheringham?

Tape measure on groyne: may not be held perfectly straight → distance to sediment recorded as longer than actual → inaccurate data → limits reliability.
Orange experiment: if not thrown straight into sea, distance travelled ≠ 10m → speed calculation inaccurate. Only 2 locations measured on one day → limited reliability.
Temporal limitation: LSD rate and direction changes with seasons, tidal conditions, prevailing winds → one day's data is a snapshot, not representative.
Beach reprofiling: human management of sediment distribution means observed sediment heights may not reflect purely natural LSD patterns → limits validity of conclusions.
Drift divide anomaly: Sheringham sits at a drift divide — LSD direction reverses seasonally, making it hard to draw clear conclusions about groyne effectiveness.
[2-mark model] Explain why it was a suitable topic for a geographical enquiry. (Physical: groynes)

"Groynes are used to protect coastal towns against erosion. Without defences, cliff retreat will occur due to hydraulic action and abrasion — Sheringham's Wroxham Crag erodes at 2.5 m/year. It is important that Sheringham is effectively protected, especially as climate change will increase storm frequency."
Structure: State the geographical theory (LSD + coastal erosion) → link to why it matters (erosion threat, Wroxham Crag, businesses, tourism).
[2-mark model] Explain why it was a suitable topic for a geographical enquiry. (Human: tourism)

"Businesses along the Norfolk coast are worth £3 billion from tourism. Sheringham therefore has to cater for both locals and tourists — it is important to understand its reliance on tourism because the businesses and employment will be heavily affected by seasonality (peak vs low season)."
Structure: State the geographical theory (tourism economy, multiplier effect) → link to why it matters (seasonal employment, reliance, economic vulnerability).
[2-mark model] Explain why the chosen location was suitable for data collection.

"Sheringham was close to school, meaning we had time to collect more land use data on the High Street. This made the results more reliable as we had a larger data set."
OR (physical): "Sheringham was close and accessible meaning we had more time to collect sediment height data at multiple groynes, making our results more reliable."
Formula: Name the location → explain HOW it enabled better data collection → link to a quality word (reliable/accurate/valid/precise).
[2-mark model] Suggest one reason why risk assessment was important when planning your enquiry.

"One risk was slipping on wet shingle when walking across the beach. Without a risk assessment this risk could have been overlooked. By identifying it, we required everyone to wear trainers with ankle support, reducing the likelihood of injury."
Formula: Name specific risk → consequence if not addressed → control measure taken.
[3-mark model] Justify one primary data collection method used in your physical geography enquiry.

"We measured the distance from the top of the groyne down to the sediment surface every 2m using a tape measure. We collected data in this way because we wanted to see how longshore drift was depositing sediment differently on each side of the groyne, telling us whether the groynes were trapping sediment effectively. Measuring every 2m (systematic sampling) gave us a reliable dataset representing the full length of the groyne without being too time-consuming."
Formula: Describe the method → state WHY it was collected → link to enquiry question → mention sampling strategy → link to quality word.
[3-mark model] Justify one primary data collection method used in your human geography enquiry.

"We collected a tally of different shop types (9 categories) and recorded their distance from the sea wall using footsteps. We collected data in this way because we wanted to see how important tourism was to the local economy in Sheringham. By counting each category we could calculate Simpson's Diversity Index (D = 0.80), showing mathematically how diverse the High Street was. Systematic sampling (every shop) gave us a reliable and complete dataset."
Formula: Describe the method → state WHY → link to question → mention statistical analysis → link to quality word.
[6-mark model] Explain how one data presentation technique helped you interpret your data. (Human)

"We used dispersion graphs to display the diversity of shops along Sheringham High Street. First way it helped: it made it easy to identify clustering — tourist shops (cafés, arcades, souvenirs) were clustered near the sea wall end, targeting tourists walking to the beach. This helped us conclude that tourism significantly shapes the character of the High Street nearest the sea. Second way it helped: the dispersion graph also made it easy to identify anomalies (shops that didn't fit the pattern) which we could investigate further. This meant our interpretation of the data was more nuanced. However, the dispersion graph was difficult to read with 12 categories — colour-coding would have improved it."
Structure: 2 ways it helped (each with evidence from results) + 1 limitation.
[6-mark model] Assess the effectiveness of your data collection method in your physical enquiry.

"One way the method was effective: measuring sediment height every 2m along multiple groynes gave us a large dataset. This meant we could compare patterns across 3 groynes and identify whether trends were consistent, making our results more reliable. Another way it was effective: measuring both sides of each groyne meant we could directly compare east vs west, clearly showing the direction of longshore drift from the sediment height differences. However, one limitation: the tape measure may not have been held perfectly straight. A 'wonky' tape would record a larger distance to the sediment than was actually there, making the data inaccurate and potentially distorting our conclusions about which side had more sediment."
Structure: 2 ways effective + 1 limitation with impact on conclusions.
[9-mark structure] How do you structure a 9-mark fieldwork question?

AQA pattern for 9-mark Sheringham questions: All variants ask about reliability of conclusions, extent results met aims, or improvements.
Paragraph 1 — Methods limitation: what was wrong with data collection → how it affected the data → impact on conclusions.
Paragraph 2 — Results limitation: what the data showed → what the conclusion was → why the conclusion may not be valid (temporal/spatial limitation).
Paragraph 3 — Overall extent + improvement: 'To some/great/limited extent' → specific improvement → how it would improve validity/reliability → back to enquiry question.
Key connectives: "This means that..." / "As a result..." / "This is significant because..." / "However, this conclusion may not be valid because..." / "To improve this, we could..."
[9-mark model] To what extent did the results meet the original aims of the physical enquiry?

The original aim was to investigate how effective groynes are at Sheringham.
Results that DID meet the aim: LSD speed was 0.17 m/s between groynes vs 0.55 m/s on unprotected beach — showing groynes do slow LSD. Sediment height was consistently higher on the west (updrift) side of groynes — confirming they trap sediment.
Limitation 1: data collected on one day only → seasonal variation in wave energy/LSD direction not captured → conclusions are a snapshot, not reliable long-term evidence.
Limitation 2: Sheringham sits at a drift divide — LSD direction reversed on the day — making it harder to conclude whether this is typical groyne effectiveness or anomalous.
Conclusion: "To some extent, our results met the aim — we evidenced that groynes reduce LSD speed and trap sediment. However, the temporal limitation means we cannot fully assess long-term effectiveness without repeated measurements over different seasons."
What is Simpson's Diversity Index and how do you interpret the Sheringham result?

Formula: D = 1 − (Σn(n-1)) ÷ (N(N-1)). D ranges from 0 to 1. D = 0 means all shops are the same type (no diversity). D = 1 means complete diversity (all different).
Sheringham result: D = 0.80
Interpretation: fairly diverse — Sheringham is not overly reliant on tourism. There are tourist-related businesses (cafés, souvenirs, arcades) but also shops for locals (supermarkets, hairdressers, solicitors). The economy provides some year-round employment.
Limitation: the 9 predefined categories were too broad — 'other' was the largest category, meaning many shops couldn't be accurately classified → reduces precision and therefore reliability of the D value.
What is primary and secondary data? Give examples from Sheringham fieldwork.

Primary data: collected directly by you during the investigation.
Sheringham examples: shop tally (land use survey), EQS scores, footstep distances, sediment height measurements, wave count, LSD orange experiment timings.
Secondary data: collected or published by someone else before your investigation.
Sheringham examples: Wroxham Crag erosion rate (2.5 m/year) from published geological records, Google Street View for pre-visit risk assessment, government statistics on tourism (£3bn for Norfolk), historical records of Happisburgh erosion.
What is the difference between systematic, random and stratified sampling? Link each to Sheringham.

Systematic: data collected at regular, fixed intervals. Sheringham examples: every shop on High Street recorded; sediment height every 2m down the groyne; LSD measurements every 2m. Why used: gives good coverage, easy to replicate, reliable.
Random: no bias — any location/individual equally likely. Sheringham: not extensively used. Could apply to selecting which groynes to measure randomly.
Stratified: population divided into groups, sample taken from each. Sheringham: chose the High Street specifically as the representative area for tourism (stratified by relevance); chose 3 groynes from the available 19. Why used: ensures the most relevant sub-groups are included → improves validity.
What is the exam structure for AQA Paper 3 Section C (fieldwork)?

Every past exam has had: 1 × 2-mark question + 1 × 3-mark question + 1 × 6-mark question + 1 × 9-mark question (from model answers booklet).
Common 2-mark questions: Why suitable topic? Why suitable location? Why was risk assessment important?
Common 3-mark questions: Justify use of maps/field sketches. Justify one data collection method.
Common 6-mark questions: Explain how a presentation technique helped interpret data. Assess effectiveness of data collection method(s).
Common 9-mark questions: To what extent did results meet aims? How could the enquiry be improved? To what extent were conclusions reliable?
AO1 (knowledge) + AO2 (application) + AO3 (evaluation) all tested. 9-mark questions require all three.
What improvements could be made to the Sheringham fieldwork?

Methods improvements: Use a trundle wheel (not footsteps) for shop distance → more accurate/precise. Use a spirit level with the groyne tape measure → prevents 'wonky' measurements. Use callipers to measure pebble volume (Eureka can method) rather than longest axis — accounts for shape.
Results improvements: Measure in multiple seasons → captures seasonal LSD variation → more reliable conclusions. Measure along more than 2 locations for orange test → more representative of whole beach.
Conclusion improvements: Interview customers to verify if tourist shops used by locals or tourists (rather than assuming). Survey more streets (not just High Street) → more valid picture of Sheringham's retail diversity as a whole.

Average UK household water use increased by {{c1::70%}} since 1985. The average person uses {{c2::140}} litres per day. Government target: {{c3::122}} litres/day by 2038 and {{c4::110}} litres/day by 2050.
UK demand for water is predicted to increase by {{c1::4 billion}} litres per day by {{c2::2050}}. The {{c3::South East}} accounts for {{c4::50%}} of the projected demand increase.
The proposed Lincolnshire reservoir would hold {{c1::55 million}} cubic metres and supply {{c2::166 million}} litres per day to {{c3::500,000}} homes, bringing {{c4::£2.6 billion}} investment.
In the UK, approximately {{c1::3 billion}} litres of water are lost daily to leakage — about {{c2::20%}} of supply. The Thames desalination plant produces {{c3::140-150 million}} litres per day.
What are the arguments for the Lincolnshire reservoir?

Addresses growing water demand in one of UK's driest areas. 175,000 new homes planned locally. Recreational facilities (sailing, cycling). Creates wetland habitats. £2.6 billion investment. Stores winter river water for summer use.
What are the arguments against the Lincolnshire reservoir?

50+ families lose homes. Farmers lose land and livelihoods. Construction traffic disrupts narrow rural roads. Uncertainty causes mental health stress. Critics argue leakage reduction and conservation could meet demand instead.
What is effective rainfall?

The rainfall remaining available for water supply after evaporation and plant uptake. Ranges from over 2,500mm/year in NW England to under 200mm in parts of eastern England — creating a supply-demand mismatch.
What are the causes of water pollution in the UK?
Untreated sewage discharge. Agricultural slurry and fertiliser runoff. Illegal dumping. Leaking sewers. In 2021 there were 325 category 1 and 2 incidents — a 24% increase from the previous year.
What does AO1, AO2 and AO3 mean in Geography?

AO1: Knowledge — know facts, definitions, case studies. AO2: Application — use knowledge to explain processes and situations. AO3: Analysis and Evaluation — make judgements, assess significance, compare effectiveness, use evidence to support conclusions.
How do you approach a 9-mark Geography question?

BUG the question (Box command word, Underline key terms, Glance at marks). Plan 3 paragraphs: Idea + Fact/Evidence + 'As a result...' + 'This is significant because...'. Use case study statistics. Address the specific aspect asked.
What are the key command words in Geography?

Describe: say what you see (no explanation). Explain: give reasons why. Suggest: informed idea using knowledge. Assess/Evaluate: judgement using evidence. To what extent: weigh both sides and conclude. Compare: similarities and differences.
How do you describe a graph or map in a Geography exam?

State the overall trend/pattern. Give specific evidence (quote data, reference locations). Identify anomalies if obvious. Use both axes. Reference the figure explicitly ('Figure 2 shows...'). Don't just repeat the trend without data.
How should you use figures/data in extended Geography answers?

Quote specific data directly ('Figure 2 shows the South East accounts for 50% of the projected demand increase'). Use it to support your argument, not just describe it. Combine figure evidence with case study knowledge for higher marks.
How do you give a six-figure grid reference?

Read eastings (left to right) first — two-digit square number then estimate tenths into the square. Then northings (bottom to top) — same process. E.g. a point 3/10 into square 52 east and 7/10 into square 34 north = 523 347.
What is the difference between mean, median and mode?
Mean: add all values, divide by count — can be skewed by extremes. Median: middle value when ordered — better for skewed data. Mode: most frequent value. Range: highest minus lowest.
Speed of longshore drift = {{c1::distance}} ÷ {{c2::time}}, expressed in {{c3::metres per second}}. Take the {{c4::mean}} of three readings to improve reliability.
What geographical skills are tested across all three papers?

OS map skills (grid references, scale, compass). Graph interpretation (bar, line, pie, scatter). Photograph analysis. Statistics (mean, median, mode, percentage change). Describing distributions and patterns. Evaluating data quality.
What were the key features of Anglo-Saxon England before 1066?

Towns defended by royal burhs (fortified mud walls). Women had legal rights — could own land, claim fines for harassment, and divorce. Anglo-Saxons were known for engraving and metalwork (e.g. Alfred Jewel, Fuller Brooch). The Church used English not Latin; Archbishop Stigand practised pluralism (held multiple posts) — the Pope was unhappy.

Anglo-Saxon England in January 1066: King {{c1::Edward the Confessor}} died on {{c2::5 January}} with no direct heir. On {{c3::6 January}}, {{c4::Harold Godwinson}} was crowned king by the Witan. Three other claimants: {{c5::Harald Hardrada}} (Norway), {{c6::Edgar the Atheling}} (blood-relative), and {{c7::William Duke of Normandy}}.
The Battle of {{c1::Fulford}} took place on {{c2::20 September 1066}}. {{c3::Harald Hardrada}} and {{c4::Tostig}} (Harold's brother) defeated English earls {{c5::Edwin and Morcar}}.

The Battle of {{c1::Stamford Bridge}} took place on {{c2::25 September 1066}}. Harold Godwinson marched north and defeated {{c3::Harald Hardrada}}. Tostig was killed. Harold {{c4::spared}} Hardrada's son {{c5::Olaf}}.
How did William win the Battle of Hastings (14 October 1066)?

Harold's army was exhausted after marching south from Stamford Bridge. William used the feigned retreat trick — Normans pretended to flee, drawing English off the hill. Normans had stirrups, which allowed knights to fight effectively on horseback. Harold was killed (arrow in the eye or cut down by knights — shown in the Bayeux Tapestry). The English shield wall broke after hours of fighting.

After Hastings, William waited two weeks for the English to {{c1::surrender}}. Instead, the Witan elected {{c2::Edgar the Atheling}} as king. William decided to take control by force. He was crowned at {{c3::Westminster}} on {{c4::25 December 1066}}.
What happened during January–March 1067 (establishing control)?

William wanted to calm the situation. He took land from English lords but let some buy it back. He built castles across East Anglia and gave them to loyal followers: William FitzOsbern in Norwich, Odo (Bishop of Bayeux) in the south-east at Dover. Then William returned to Normandy, taking Edwin, Morcar and Edgar with him as insurance.
August 1067: {{c1::Edric the Wild}} of Mercia raided Herefordshire. He had lost his land in Shropshire to Normans. He allied with the {{c2::Welsh}}. William {{c3::did not leave Normandy}}.

1068: {{c1::Gytha}} (Harold's mother) began plotting with the {{c2::Irish}}. William marched to {{c3::Exeter}} in January. The townspeople promised to behave, then broke their word — William {{c4::gouged out hostages' eyes}}. May 1068: {{c5::Matilda}} was crowned queen.

The Harrying of the North took place in {{c1::winter 1069–70}}. William marched north and used a {{c2::scorched earth}} policy — burning crops, livestock and villages. Sixteen years later, the Domesday Book recorded these areas as still {{c3::wasteland}}. It caused widespread {{c4::starvation}}.

The rebellion of {{c1::Ely}} (1070–71) was led by {{c2::Hereward the Wake}}, who joined with {{c3::Danes}} who had attacked Ely in June 1070. William crushed the rebellion in {{c4::1071}}. This was the last major English resistance to Norman rule.
How did the Normans change castles in England?

Built many more — especially motte-and-bailey (wooden keep on a raised mound, surrounded by a bailey). Later replaced with stone. Key castles: Tower of London (started 1075–79), Castle Acre, Old Sarum. Castles served as military bases and symbols of Norman power and control.

The {{c1::Domesday Book}} was compiled in {{c2::1086}} on William's orders. It was a survey of all land, owners and values in England. By this date, only {{c3::5%}} of land was still owned by Englishmen. It showed William how much {{c4::tax}} he could collect.

New Norman laws: {{c1::Murdrum Fine}} — if a Norman was killed and the killer not found, the whole {{c2::local community}} was fined. {{c3::Forest Law}} — William claimed all forests as royal hunting land; poaching meant death. {{c4::Trial by combat}} replaced Anglo-Saxon oaths.

William replaced Archbishop {{c1::Stigand}} with {{c2::Lanfranc}}, a much stricter Norman archbishop. The Pope {{c3::approved}} the Conquest. William began rebuilding cathedrals — {{c4::Winchester, York, Canterbury, Ely}}. By 1087, only {{c5::10%}} of people lived in towns.
What were the main rebellions against William after 1066 and who led them?

Edric the Wild (Mercia, 1067). Gytha's rebellion (Exeter, 1068). Northern rebellions (1068–69) — Edwin, Morcar, Edgar the Atheling. Danish-backed revolt and Harrying of the North (1069–70). Hereward the Wake at Ely (1070–71). All were eventually crushed, with the North suffering most.
Who were the key individuals in the Norman Conquest and what roles did they play?

Harold Godwinson: English king, killed at Hastings. Harald Hardrada: Viking claimant, killed at Stamford Bridge. Tostig: Harold's traitorous brother, killed at Stamford Bridge. William of Normandy: conqueror, became king. Edgar the Atheling: blood-claim but young, eventually submitted. Hereward the Wake: last rebel at Ely. Lanfranc: new Norman Archbishop, stricter than Stigand.

The Bayeux Tapestry shows Harold's death as an {{c1::arrow in the eye}} or being {{c2::cut down by Norman knights}}. It was made by the {{c3::Normans}} and so presents a {{c4::Norman perspective}} on the Conquest.
What were the positive and negative changes brought by the Norman Conquest?

Positive: beautiful stone churches and cathedrals built; Domesday Book created a clear record of land ownership; more organised feudal system. Negative: English lost their land and language; Harrying caused mass starvation in the North; harsh laws like Murdrum Fine and Forest Law; English culture (language, engraving) suppressed.

William's coronation ({{c1::25 December 1066}}): the crowd outside Westminster cheered for William. Norman guards outside mistook the noise for a rebellion and {{c2::set fire to London houses}}. Inside, the ceremony continued in chaos. This shows the tension and {{c3::fear}} that underpinned Norman rule from the very beginning.

Norman castle placements 1067: William gave {{c1::William FitzOsbern}} control of {{c2::Norwich}} in East Anglia. {{c3::Odo of Bayeux}} (William's half-brother, Bishop of Bayeux) controlled the south-east, based at {{c4::Dover}}. These were William's two most trusted lieutenants.

Spring 1069: {{c1::Robert of Comins}} was made new Earl of Northumbria with over {{c2::500}} Norman soldiers. Rebels {{c3::killed Robert in Durham}}. Edgar the Atheling attacked {{c4::York}} again. This time William did not build castles — he attacked. He left {{c5::William FitzOsbern}} in charge of a new castle at York.

William's 3-point plan (winter 1069): 1. Defeat the {{c1::English rebels}}. 2. Defeat the {{c2::Danes}}. 3. The {{c3::Harrying of the North}} — destroy crops, livestock and villages so rebels could never rise again. The Harrying killed approximately {{c4::100,000}} people and left land recorded as {{c5::wasteland}} in Domesday Book 16 years later.
What were the key features of a motte-and-bailey castle?

Motte: raised mound of earth (sometimes layered with stone/turf) with a wooden keep on top. Bailey: large walled enclosure around the base where soldiers, animals and villagers lived. Palisade: wooden fence wall around the bailey. Moat: deep ditch (sometimes water-filled) around the outside. Drawbridge: only entrance to the bailey — could be raised if attacked. Later replaced by stone (e.g. Tower of London started 1075–79).
Who were the main chroniclers of the Norman Conquest and why are they useful/limited as sources?

William of Poitiers: William's personal chaplain and biographer — detailed but extremely pro-Norman. Orderic Vitalis: wrote decades later, includes English perspective — more balanced but less contemporary. Bayeux Tapestry: visual Norman account — narrative but made by the victors. All sources have Norman bias; no surviving English account exists from 1066 itself.
What are the OCR Norman Conquest exam question types and their mark allocations?

3-mark question: identify and explain one feature of an interpretation. 5-mark question: propose a further line of enquiry (must include a second-order concept). 12-mark question: compare two interpretations — what do they say? Why do they differ? 20-mark essay: 4 paragraphs + introduction + conclusion, agree/disagree structure.
What are the four periods covered in People's Health and roughly what years do they cover?

Medieval (1250–1500), Early Modern (1500–1750), Industrial (1750–1900), Modern (1900–present). The theme is continuity and change in living conditions and government responses to disease across these periods.
What were medieval towns like for ordinary people? (housing, water, waste, food)

Housing: cramped, thatched, shared with animals (hovels). Smoke stayed inside; trades like tanning done indoors. Water: water sellers with leather sacks (went stagnant); conduits funded by monasteries; streams. Waste: cesspits emptied by gongfermers twice a year; rakers cleared streets; middens (waste heaps). Food: pottage (stew kept for days); rotten meat sold at markets; fresh fish/veg in the countryside.

Medieval water: in towns, water sellers used {{c1::leather sacks}} in which water quickly went {{c2::stagnant}}. Conduits (lead pipes) were often paid for by {{c3::monasteries}}. Most people collected water from {{c4::streams or rivers}}.

Medieval waste: {{c1::gongfermers}} emptied cesspits, usually only {{c2::twice a year}}. {{c3::Rakers}} swept the streets, but left them if too narrow. {{c4::Middens}} were waste heaps outside homes. Cesspits often {{c5::overflowed}} if not watertight.

The Black Death arrived in England in {{c1::1348}} and eventually killed about {{c2::one third}} of Europe's population. There were three forms: {{c3::bubonic}} (swellings/buboes), {{c4::septicaemic}} (blood), and {{c5::pneumonic}} (lungs).
What did medieval people believe caused the Black Death?
Punishment from God for sin. Jews had poisoned the water. Miasma (bad-smelling air). Volcano fumes. The movement of planets. The four humours being out of balance.
How did individuals and the Church respond to the Black Death?

Flagellants walked barefoot through streets whipping themselves to beg God's forgiveness. People tied live toads or chickens to their buboes. Others balanced the four humours by bloodletting. Rich people fled to the countryside to escape miasma. Sweet-smelling herbs were carried; candles were burned.
How did the government respond to the Black Death?

There was no national government — the king and Church acted locally. Edward I ordered York to clean its streets (1301). Edward III ordered London to clear up (1349). No centralised laws were passed. Local towns made their own rules. These were largely ineffective.

Medieval improvements: in 1329 the {{c1::butcher's guild of Winchester}} was set up to check meat quality. In 1415 the Mayor of London ordered {{c2::public latrines}} to be rebuilt. In 1385 a {{c3::warden}} was hired to check the Thames was clean. These were {{c4::local}} not national responses.
How did living conditions change (or not) in the Early Modern period (1500–1750)?

Some improvement: stone houses began replacing thatch for the wealthy; piped water arrived for the rich (1600s); flushing closet invented 1596. But for the poor: water sellers still common (now using barrels); cesspits still overflowed; main diet still pottage. Little changed for the majority.

In the Early Modern period, {{c1::Henry VIII}} was the first monarch to issue national plague orders, requiring {{c2::40 days' isolation}} (1518). In 1578, {{c3::Elizabeth I}} introduced {{c4::17 Plague Orders}}, including burning victims' clothing, killing stray animals, and holding funerals after dusk. In 1604, James I added {{c5::hanging and whipping}} as punishments for ignoring plague orders.
What was the Gin Craze and why was it a public health problem?

In the early 1700s, cheap gin flooded London. By the 1720s: crime increased, families fell into poverty, death rates rose (alcohol-related), babies were harmed by mothers drinking while pregnant. Hundreds of thousands drank gin as an escape from hardship.

The {{c1::1729, 1736 and 1743}} Gin Acts introduced taxes and licences for gin sellers but were {{c2::largely ineffective}} — the government did not enforce them and illegal gin shops flourished. The {{c3::1751}} Gin Act was much tougher: only people who paid rent and taxes could sell gin; illegal sellers faced {{c4::imprisonment and whipping}}, and gin drinking was hugely reduced.
What were the key features of Industrial-era living conditions (1750–1900)?

Housing: back-to-back houses; up to 8 people per room; lodging houses; cellars. Water: shared pumps serving up to 200 people; water drawn from polluted canals. Waste: shared privies over cesspools; 'night soil men' collected waste. Food: widespread adulteration (sawdust in bread, chalk in milk, copper in butter).

Cholera first arrived in Britain in {{c1::1831–32}}, killing {{c2::32,000}} people. A second epidemic in {{c3::1877}} killed {{c4::12,000}}. Symptoms were {{c5::fever, diarrhoea and vomiting}}. Most believed it was spread by {{c6::miasma}} (bad air).

In 1842, {{c1::Edwin Chadwick}} published a report linking {{c2::poverty and dirt}} to disease. This led to the {{c3::first Public Health Act in 1848}}, which set up a Central Board of Health. Chadwick argued that {{c4::clean water and sewers}} would reduce disease and poverty.

In 1854, {{c1::John Snow}} investigated a cholera outbreak in {{c2::Soho, London}}. He mapped deaths and found they centred around the {{c3::Broad Street water pump}}. He persuaded the council to {{c4::remove the pump handle}}, ending the outbreak. He proved cholera was spread through {{c5::contaminated water}}, not miasma.

The {{c1::Great Stink}} of {{c2::1858}} made the smell from the River Thames so unbearable that Parliament could not sit. This forced the government to fund {{c3::Joseph Bazalgette}}'s sewer system — over {{c4::1,300 miles}} of sewers were built, cleaning up the Thames and reducing cholera.

The {{c1::1875 Public Health Act}} (under Disraeli) made it {{c2::compulsory}} for local authorities to provide clean water, sewers, and housing inspections. Unlike the {{c3::1848}} Act, which was voluntary, the 1875 Act had real {{c4::enforcement powers}}.

{{c1::Louis Pasteur}} developed germ theory in {{c2::1861}}, proving that {{c3::germs (microbes)}} — not miasma or bad air — caused disease. This was a turning point: governments could now target the {{c4::actual cause}} of disease rather than its symptoms.
What key improvements to public health were made in the industrial period (1750–1900)?

1842: Chadwick's report on poverty and disease. 1848: first (voluntary) Public Health Act. 1854: John Snow identifies cholera waterborne source. 1858: Great Stink → Bazalgette's sewers. 1861: Pasteur's germ theory. 1867: working class men get the vote. 1875: second (compulsory) Public Health Act. 1860: Pure Food Act.

The Spanish Flu of {{c1::1918–19}} killed over {{c2::50 million}} people worldwide and over {{c3::228,000}} in the UK. Symptoms began as a common cold then became {{c4::pneumonia}}. The national government response was {{c5::limited}} — key action came from individuals like {{c6::Dr Niven}} in Manchester.

Dr {{c1::Niven}} in Manchester: published detailed advice in {{c2::newspapers and cinemas}}; kept {{c3::detailed case records}} to track patterns; urged councils to {{c4::shut schools and theatres}}; praised NHS workers. Only {{c5::2,000}} died in Manchester — far fewer than London or Glasgow.
What was the 'Homes for Heroes' initiative and who introduced it?

After WW1, Lloyd George's government promised to build council houses ('Homes for Heroes') for returning soldiers. Back-to-back housing was banned in 1909 and removed by 1919. However, Margaret Thatcher sold off council houses in the 1980s — they passed to private landlords who often failed to maintain hygiene standards.
What were the five phases of society's response to AIDS in Britain?

1. Growing awareness (1970s–1983): deaths reported, Terrence Higgins Trust set up. 2. Growing alarm (1984–85): fear, firemen stopped giving mouth-to-mouth, schools withdrew pupils. 3. Growing understanding (1986–87): clean needle programmes, Princess Diana shook hands with AIDS patients. 4. Growing acceptance (1988–95): Freddie Mercury concert raised £20 million. 5. Growing complacency (1996+): anti-retrovirals led people to believe AIDS was curable.

In {{c1::1987}}, {{c2::Princess Diana}} visited an AIDS clinic and shook hands with a patient, proving AIDS was {{c3::not spread by touch}}. This broke the social stigma and created widespread publicity. In {{c4::1992}}, a tribute concert for Freddie Mercury raised {{c5::£20 million}} for AIDS research.

In {{c1::1996}}, scientists developed {{c2::anti-retroviral}} drugs that slowed the development of AIDS in HIV-positive patients. As a result, the government {{c3::relaxed its campaigns}}, leading to {{c4::growing complacency}}. By 2009, over {{c5::100,000}} people in Britain had HIV.
What does RARE stand for in the context of Nazi Germany's impact on the German people? (Also used in People's Health for 20th century analysis)
Rationing, Area bombing, Refugees, Employment. A framework for analysing the impact of Total War (WW2) on Germany — or used by teachers to structure analysis of modern public health impacts.
How did monasteries contribute to medieval public health?

Infirmaries cared for the sick (Benedictine rule: 'care for the sick stands before all'). Herb/physick gardens grew medicinal plants. Monasteries built near running fresh water — provided drinking and washing water for the sick. Latrines built so waste discharged into rivers. Even without running water, cathedrals built pipes to supply fresh water. Monks believed caring for the sick brought them closer to God. Limited: only helped those who could reach a monastery; no national system.

Leeds local board of health ({{c1::1831}}): set up in response to cholera. Employed surgeon {{c2::Dr Robert Baker}} to investigate. He {{c3::mapped}} the outbreak and found cases concentrated in {{c4::dirty areas}}. Baker believed in {{c5::miasma}} (incorrect), but his report was important evidence linking {{c6::dirt and disease}}. The board also published advice on {{c7::posters and newspapers}} and opened a {{c8::cholera hospital}}.
How can you classify the different responses to cholera in the 1830s?

Three belief categories: (1) Punishment from God — prayer, church attendance, fasting. (2) Miasma — cleaning streets, burning barrels of tar, adding chloride of lime to sewers to mask smells. (3) Contagion — quarantine (stopping people moving from town centres to suburbs), cholera hospitals to isolate victims. Most responses were based on miasma theory — incorrect, but accidentally useful (cleaning streets did reduce disease). John Snow's 1854 waterborne proof was not widely accepted for years.

{{c1::Laissez-faire}}: the political belief that the government should {{c2::not interfere}} in people's everyday lives. This was the main reason the 1848 Public Health Act was {{c3::voluntary}} — the government did not feel it had the right to force local councils to act. It also explains why the {{c4::1751 Gin Act}} was tougher than earlier acts — eventually public opinion shifted enough to force action. Laissez-faire slowed health reform throughout the {{c5::Industrial period}}.
How do you structure the People's Health 18-mark essay (individuals vs government)?

Typical question: 'The actions of great individuals were the most important factor in improving Public Health.' Structure: Para 1 (agree — individual, e.g. John Snow). Para 2 (agree — individual, e.g. Bazalgette/Chadwick). Para 3 (disagree — government, e.g. 1875 Public Health Act compulsory). Para 4 (disagree — events forcing change, e.g. Great Stink, cholera epidemics). Conclusion: both individuals AND government were needed — individuals provided the evidence; government provided the enforcement. Link to laissez-faire as the brake on progress.
What are the key second-order concepts used in OCR History People's Health essays?

Cause, Consequence, Change, Continuity, Significance, Diversity (Typicality). Always name which concept you are using — e.g. 'this shows continuity because...' or 'a consequence of this was...'. The 9-mark summary question requires at least two different second-order concepts.
What key events between 1918 and 1933 helped Hitler rise to power?

1918: Armistice — 'Stab in the Back' myth. 1919: Treaty of Versailles — punishments humiliated Germany. 1919: Hitler joins DAP (German Workers' Party). 1920: 25 Point Programme. 1923: Hyperinflation crisis. 1923: Munich Putsch — failed coup, Hitler jailed. 1925: Mein Kampf published. 1929: Wall Street Crash → Great Depression → 6 million unemployed. January 1933: Hitler appointed Chancellor.

Hitler became {{c1::Chancellor}} of Germany in {{c2::January 1933}}. The Reichstag (parliament building) was set on fire on {{c3::27 February 1933}} — the Nazis blamed the {{c4::Communists}}. This was used to justify banning communists and calling new elections on {{c5::5 March 1933}}.

The {{c1::Enabling Act}} was passed on {{c2::24 March 1933}}. It gave Hitler the power to {{c3::pass laws without consulting the Reichstag}} for four years. This made him a dictator. Trade unions were banned on {{c4::2 May 1933}}. All political parties were banned in {{c5::July 1933}}.

The Night of the Long Knives took place on {{c1::29–30 June 1934}}. Hitler had the leaders of the {{c2::SA (Sturmabteilung)}} killed, including {{c3::Ernst Röhm}}. This removed a rival power base and reassured the {{c4::army}}. After this, Hitler was seen as above the law.

President Hindenburg died on {{c1::2 August 1934}}. Hitler immediately combined the roles of {{c2::Chancellor and President}}, calling himself {{c3::der Führer}} (the Leader). The army swore an oath of personal loyalty {{c4::to Hitler}} (not Germany) on {{c5::August 1934}}.
How did the Nazis control Germany through fear?

SS (Schutzstaffel): Hitler's personal bodyguard and elite force — ran concentration camps. SD (Sicherheitsdienst): Nazi intelligence service. Gestapo: secret state police — used informers and torture; 26% of cases came from public denunciations. People's Court: dealt with political crimes; unfair trials. Concentration camps: first opened March 1933; held political opponents.

The Gestapo had very few officers — they relied on the public to {{c1::inform on their neighbours}}. In Düsseldorf, {{c2::26%}} of cases came from the {{c3::general public}}. Despite Nazi propaganda suggesting agents were {{c4::everywhere}}, the reality was that Germans {{c5::informed on each other}} voluntarily.
How did the Nazis control Germany through propaganda?

Goebbels ran the Ministry of Propaganda. Controlled: all radio stations (taken over 1934), newspapers, cinema, art, music, theatre, school curriculum. Rallies like Nuremberg used spectacle and emotion. Public loudspeakers broadcast Hitler's speeches in workplaces, shops and pubs. Key message: national pride, anti-Semitism, racial purity.
Who opposed the Nazis between 1933 and 1939, and how effective were they?

Social Democrats and Communists: forced underground; had limited impact. Christians for/against: Concordat 1933 initially silenced Catholics; 1937 Papal Encyclical 'With Burning Anxiety' opposed the Nazis. Swing Kids: rejected Nazi music for jazz; minor youth rebellion. Edelweiss Pirates: youth groups who fought Hitler Youth and sang anti-Nazi songs — not organised enough to threaten the regime.
What did the Nazis want from German workers, and what was the reality?

Wanted: full employment, loyalty, productivity. Did: Strength Through Joy (cheap holidays, leisure), Beauty of Labour (improve workplaces), abolish trade unions. Results: unemployment fell from 6 million (1933) to near zero (1938). BUT: workers had no rights, could not strike, wages were controlled. Standard of living for workers was mixed.

Nazi policies towards women: marriage loans of up to {{c1::1,000 Reichsmarks}} offered to Aryan couples — reduced by {{c2::a quarter}} for each child born. The {{c3::Honour Cross of the German Mother}} was awarded: {{c4::bronze}} for 4–5 children, {{c5::silver}} for 6–7, {{c6::gold}} for 8+.

Nazi women policies: the number of marriages {{c1::increased}} from 516,000 (1932) to 772,000 (1939). But women in employment {{c2::also increased}} — in agriculture from 4.6 million to {{c3::4.9 million}}; in industry from 2.7 to {{c4::3.3 million}}. Female university enrolment was capped at {{c5::10%}}. The Nazis' aims for women were {{c6::largely unsuccessful}}.
How did the Nazis control young people through education and youth groups?

Schools: all teachers had to join the National Socialist Teachers League (1933); curriculum pushed Nazi ideology in every subject (e.g. maths problems about racial 'impurity'). Youth groups: Hitler Youth (boys 10–18) and League of German Maidens (BDM, girls) — cheap holidays, hikes, sports, Nazi songs. Membership was compulsory from 1936. Some children informed on parents to the Gestapo.

The {{c1::Nuremberg Laws}} were passed in {{c2::1935}}. They stripped German Jews of {{c3::citizenship}} and banned marriage or sexual relations between Jews and {{c4::Aryans}}.{{c5::Kristallnacht}} (Night of Broken Glass) occurred on {{c6::9–10 November 1938}}: Jewish businesses, synagogues and homes were attacked; around {{c7::100}} Jews were killed and {{c8::30,000}} arrested.
What were the stages of Nazi persecution of Jews leading to the Final Solution?

1933–38: boycotts, civil service sackings, Nuremberg Laws. 1938: Kristallnacht. First solution (1938–39): forced emigration. Second solution (1939–41): concentration in ghettos (e.g. Warsaw). 1941: Einsatzgruppen (mobile killing units) followed the army into the Soviet Union. 1942: Wannsee Conference planned the Final Solution. Death camps set up in Eastern Europe (e.g. Auschwitz, Treblinka).
How did World War II affect Germany in its early stages (1939–42)?

Initially positive: Germany gained food, raw materials from conquered territory. 2/5 Germans actually ate better due to rationing. Luxury goods imported (mostly to senior Nazis). But: coal shortages in winter 1940; rationing queues; children evacuated; up to 4 air raids a week.

{{c1::Albert Speer}} was put in charge of the German war economy in {{c2::1942}}. He reorganised production and increased output. {{c3::Total War}} was declared in {{c4::February 1943}} after the German defeat at {{c5::Stalingrad}}, requiring all resources to be focused on the war effort.

Use the RARE framework to analyse the impact of WW2 on Germans: {{c1::R}}ationing (diets became bread, potato, preserves by 1945), {{c2::A}}rea bombing (500,000 Berliners homeless by Nov 1943; 400,000 Germans killed in raids 1943–45), {{c3::R}}efugees (11 million ethnic Germans expelled by 1945), {{c4::E}}mployment (7.6 million forced foreign workers; elderly and Hitler Youth conscripted 1944).
Who opposed the Nazis during World War II and how effective were they?

White Rose (Munich University, 1942–43): Hans and Sophie Scholl distributed anti-Nazi leaflets; arrested and executed February 1943. July Bomb Plot (1944): senior military officers tried to assassinate Hitler; failed; 5,000 executed. Rosenstrasse (February 1943): German women protested for release of Jewish husbands; succeeded. Cardinal Galen (1941): publicly condemned Nazi euthanasia programme. Passive resistance: absenteeism, spreading rumours, listening to BBC.
How did the Nazis treat occupied Poland and the Netherlands differently?

Poland: first country occupied (1939); 500,000 Germans moved in, Poles moved out; Hans Frank ruled by terror; 1.9 million Poles killed; 3 of 3.5 million Polish Jews murdered; extermination camps built. Netherlands (occupied May 1940): Dutch seen as Aryan, so no mass murder; no German resettlement; but Dutch Jews still deported 1943; 500,000 Dutch workers sent to Germany as forced labour.
What were the OCR Nazi Germany exam question types and mark allocations?

7-mark source question: analyse usefulness of one source (content, origin, purpose). 15-mark question: analyse two sources and one interpretation. 18-mark essay: 4 paragraphs + one-sentence intro + conclusion. Each paragraph starts 'I agree/disagree because…'. Always include specific facts and link back to the question.

Key Nazi Germany chronology: 1918 {{c1::Armistice}}; 1919 {{c2::Treaty of Versailles}}; 1923 {{c3::Munich Putsch}}; Jan 1933 {{c4::Chancellor}}; Feb 1933 {{c5::Reichstag Fire}}; March 1933 {{c6::Enabling Act}}; June 1934 {{c7::Night of the Long Knives}}; Aug 1934 {{c8::Hindenburg dies → Führer}}; 1935 {{c9::Nuremberg Laws}}; Nov 1938 {{c10::Kristallnacht}}; Sept 1939 {{c11::invasion of Poland}}; 1942 {{c12::Wannsee Conference}}; July 1944 {{c13::Bomb Plot}}; April 1945 {{c14::Hitler's suicide}}.
How do you classify Hitler's steps to dictatorship as legal or violent?

Legal steps: Reichstag Fire (used legally to ban Communists and call elections); Enabling Act (passed by the Reichstag — legal but forced); ban on all other political parties July 1933; Hindenburg's death merged roles legally. Violent steps: SA intimidation during 1933 elections; Night of the Long Knives (political murder); SS/Gestapo used terror. Key argument: Hitler used a mixture of both — the legal veneer made it harder for opponents to resist, and harder for foreign powers to intervene.
How do you answer the Nazi Germany 7-mark source question (Content, Origin, Purpose)?

Structure: (1) Content — what does the source actually say/show? Quote it directly and explain what it tells us. (2) Origin — who made it, when, what type of source? Consider what this means for its reliability. (3) Purpose — why was it made? What was it trying to do? (e.g. to persuade, to inform, to justify). Example: Source A (German newspaper 1934 ordering loudspeakers for Hitler's speech) → Content: shows propaganda reach into workplaces. Origin: official Nazi newspaper, 1934. Purpose: to maximise exposure to Hitler's message and create impression of total support.
How do you answer the Nazi Germany 15-mark question (two sources + interpretation)?

Structure: Three paragraphs — each like a mini 7-mark answer. Para 1: What does Source A suggest? Use content/origin/purpose. Para 2: What does Source B suggest? Same structure. Para 3: What does the interpretation suggest? How does it compare to the sources — does it agree or disagree? Always use specific quotes from the sources. The sources and interpretation are usually on the same theme — identify what they agree/disagree on.

Example 7-mark source: Source A is a {{c1::German newspaper announcement (March 1934)}} ordering that loudspeakers be placed in {{c2::factories, shops, pubs and blocks of flats}} so people could hear Hitler's speech. Content shows: propaganda was spread into {{c3::all areas of everyday life}}. Origin: an official {{c4::Nazi}} source, so likely exaggerates compliance. Purpose: to {{c5::maximise the audience}} for Hitler's speeches and reinforce the impression of {{c6::total national unity}}.
What is the structure of Making of America and what period does it cover?

The Making of America, 1789–1900. Covers: American expansion 1789–1838 (Constitution, slavery, Indian removal); The West 1839–60 (Plains Indians, migration, gold rush); Civil War and Reconstruction 1861–77; Settlement and conflict on the Plains 1861–77; American cultures 1877–1900 (changing lives of Plains Indians and Black Americans).
