Peripeteia (Ancient Greek): the turning point or unexpected reversal of circumstances — in Macbeth, the moment he kills Duncan. After this point, his tragic decline is inevitable. Hubris: excessive pride that leads to one's downfall — Macbeth's belief in the witches' prophecies leads him to think he is invincible ('none of woman born shall harm Macbeth'), which is his fatal mistake.
What did King James I write and how does this inform Macbeth?

James I wrote books on kingship whilst King of Scotland — 'Basilikon Doron' (Royal Gift) and 'The True Law of Free Monarchies'. These outlined his beliefs: the king is God's representative, regicide is the gravest sin. Shakespeare appeals directly to these in Macbeth — the qualities Malcolm lists ('the king-becoming graces') echo James's own writing. Malcolm testing Macduff's loyalty mirrors James's advice that a king should be cunning, not naively trusting like Duncan.
What is trochaic tetrameter and why do the witches speak in it?

Trochaic tetrameter = four feet of trochees: DAdum/DAdum/DAdum/DAdum (8 syllables). The witches speak in INCOMPLETE trochaic tetrameter — 7 syllables instead of 8. It is the opposite of iambic pentameter, marking the witches as 'unnatural' and 'other'. Shakespeare uses it to suggest they belong to a different order of existence — the supernatural world rather than the human one.

The witches speak in {{c1::incomplete trochaic tetrameter}} — {{c2::7}} syllables instead of {{c3::8}}. This is the {{c4::opposite}} of iambic pentameter, signifying they are {{c5::outside normal human order}}. When Macbeth uses a rhyming couplet at the end of a scene (e.g. 'fires/desires'), it signals his {{c6::alignment with the supernatural}}.

Macbeth's arc in one phrase: at the start he is called '{{c1::brave Macbeth}}' and 'valiant cousin'; by the end he is '{{c2::dead butcher}}' and '{{c3::untitled tyrant bloody-sceptred}}'. Tracking this language across the play shows Shakespeare's argument: {{c4::ambition without morality}} leads to total destruction.
Why are soliloquies significant in Macbeth specifically?

Soliloquies give the audience privileged access to Macbeth's true thoughts — we see the gap between his public behaviour and private mind. His first aside ('why do I yield to that suggestion') shows murderous thoughts BEFORE Lady Macbeth even reads the letter — proving he is not simply 'manipulated' by her but already capable of evil. This is crucial: the witches and Lady Macbeth expose his flaw but don't create it.
How does setting function in Macbeth?

The heath opening establishes a dark, gothic tone — suggesting free will is contested and nature will restore order despite human interference. Castles signal the high status of characters against their low-status actions (dramatic irony). The battlefield setting at both opening and close provides cyclical structure — reminding audiences of Macbeth's true identity as a celebrated soldier. The play begins and ends with war, but the first is righteous and the second corrective.
Why is the dagger in Macbeth theatrically significant?

It is deliberately ambiguous — Shakespeare calls it a 'dagger of the mind' (a hallucination) but its handle points 'toward my hand', tempting action. The command word 'Come' transfers responsibility for the murder to the dagger itself. Directors must decide whether to show it or not — if real, the supernatural world is literally present; if imagined, Macbeth's guilt is already manifesting. Either interpretation enriches the themes.
Trace how appearance vs reality develops across Macbeth from start to end.

Start: witches 'Fair is foul' — world established as untrustworthy. Duncan 'look like women' but have beards. Middle: dagger — 'false creation' yet points toward Macbeth's hand. Neptune's ocean won't wash away guilt. Banquo's ghost — real to Macbeth, invisible to others. End: Birnam Wood coming to Dunsinane — soldiers carry branches. Lady Macbeth sees blood on hands that isn't there. Macduff 'not of woman born' — the prophecy was literally true but meaningfully false.

At the very start, the witches open the play: '{{c1::When shall we three meet again? / In thunder, lightning, or in rain?}}' — the chaotic weather immediately establishes {{c2::disorder and the supernatural}}. They speak in {{c3::trochaic tetrameter}} — the reversed, incomplete metre marks them as '{{c4::other}}', outside human order.

At the start, the witches open: '{{c1::Fair is foul, and foul is fair}} / Hover through the fog and filthy air.' — the {{c2::oxymoron}} establishes the theme of {{c3::appearance vs reality}} before we even meet Macbeth.
At the start, Macbeth's first words echo the witches: '{{c1::So foul and fair a day I have not seen}}' — immediately linking him to the {{c2::supernatural}} and suggesting a pre-existing connection.

At the start, in his soliloquy before the murder, Macbeth: '{{c1::I have no spur / To prick the sides of my intent, but only / Vaulting ambition, which o'erleaps itself / And falls on th'other}}' — the extended metaphor of horse-riding shows ambition as {{c2::ungovernable}} and self-destructive.

At the start, in an aside after the witches' prophecy, Macbeth: '{{c1::Stars, hide your fires; / Let not light see my black and deep desires}}' — the imperative to the stars shows he is {{c2::aware}} his desires are evil; he is not deceived by the witches — he {{c3::chooses}} to act on them.

At the start, after meeting the witches, Macbeth: '{{c1::If chance will have me king, why, chance may crown me / Without my stir}}' — he believes fate will do the work; he has not yet decided to {{c2::act}}. This makes Lady Macbeth's influence more significant.

At the start, Macbeth's first {{c1::aside}} — 'why do I yield to that suggestion / whose horrid image doth {{c2::unfix my hair}} / And make my seated heart {{c3::knock at my ribs}}, against the use of nature?' — his body {{c4::physically revolts}} against the thought of murder, showing he is not naturally evil.

At the start, in his soliloquy before the murder, Macbeth acknowledges: 'Besides, this Duncan / Hath borne his faculties so {{c1::meek}}, hath been / So {{c2::clear}} in his great office' — even Macbeth acknowledges Duncan's {{c3::virtue}}, making the murder more morally indefensible.

At the start, in his soliloquy before the murder, Macbeth acknowledges: 'His virtues who plead like {{c1::angels, trumpet-tongued}}, against the {{c2::deep damnation}} of his taking off' — the angelic imagery shows even Macbeth knows this is {{c3::morally indefensible}}. He commits the murder {{c4::fully aware}} of its evil.

At the start, Macbeth resolves not to kill Duncan: '{{c1::We will proceed no further in this business}}' — the monosyllabic line creates certainty. After Lady Macbeth's persuasion: '{{c2::False face must hide what the false heart doth know}}' — he completely reverses.

At the start, after committing to the murder, Macbeth: '{{c1::Away, and mock the time with fairest show / False face must hide what the false heart doth know}}' — the alliteration of 'f' and the antithesis of false/fair foregrounds the play's central theme of {{c2::appearance vs reality}}.

Just before killing Duncan, Macbeth hallucinates: '{{c1::Is this a dagger which I see before me}} / The handle toward my hand? Come, let me clutch thee.' — the rhetorical question and command word '{{c2::Come}}' almost {{c3::transfers blame}} for the murder to the dagger itself.

After killing Duncan: 'Will all great {{c1::Neptune's ocean}} wash this blood / Clean from my hand? No, this my hand will rather / The {{c2::multitudinous seas incarnadine}} / Making the green one red.' — the Latinate verb uses the root 'carn' ({{c3::flesh}}), showing guilt cannot be washed away.

After Duncan's murder: '{{c1::Amen stuck in my throat}}' — Macbeth cannot express {{c2::holy goodness}} after regicide. The transgressive act has made him {{c3::unable to pray}} — he has severed his connection to God, showing he lacks every quality of a {{c4::good Jacobean king}}.
What does Macduff say when he discovers Duncan's body and why is it significant?

In the middle, when Macduff discovers Duncan's body: 'O horror, horror, horror! Tongue nor heart / Cannot conceive nor name thee!' — the epizeuxis of 'horror' shows stupefaction. The sense that the most terrifying things 'transcend language' links to the witches who perform 'a deed without a name'.

Macduff discovers Duncan: '{{c1::O horror! Horror! Horror! / Most sacrilegious murder hath broke ope / the Lord's anointed temple}}' — the {{c2::epizeuxis}} of 'horror' shows stupefaction; naming Duncan 'the Lord's anointed temple' frames regicide as {{c3::desecration of a church}} — Jacobean audiences would have been appalled.

In the middle, Banquo suspects Macbeth: 'Thou hast it now: king, Cawdor, Glamis, all, / As the weird women promised, and I fear, / {{c1::Thou play'dst most foully for't}}' — 'foully' echoes the witches' motif of '{{c2::foul}}' running through the play.

In the middle, after becoming queen, Lady Macbeth: '{{c1::Nought's had, all's spent}} / Where our desire is got without content' — the oxymoron '{{c2::doubtful joy}}' shows that achieving ambition has brought only emptiness.

In the middle, at the banquet, Macbeth addresses Banquo's ghost: '{{c1::Thou canst not say I did it}}: never shake / Thy gory locks at me!' — the pronoun 'I' shows Macbeth believes he is not guilty as he used hired killers, revealing his {{c2::moral cowardice}}.

In the middle, as Macbeth seeks the witches out, the Second Witch announces: 'By the pricking of my thumbs, / {{c1::Something wicked this way comes}}' — Macbeth was once summoned BY the witches; now he seeks THEM out, showing he has become the {{c2::villain}} of the play.

In the middle, after ordering Banquo's murder, Macbeth: '{{c1::O, full of scorpions is my mind, dear wife!}}' — the metaphor of scorpions (painful, venomous) conveys the {{c2::agony}} of his guilty conscience after Banquo's murder.

In the middle, Macbeth: 'a {{c1::fruitless crown}}, and put a {{c2::barren sceptre}} in my grip' — the plant and fertility motifs show Macbeth has no heir; his power is {{c3::impermanent and sterile}}. He has murdered for a crown that will die {{c4::with him}}.

Macbeth distances himself from Lady Macbeth: 'be {{c1::innocent of the knowledge}}, dearest {{c2::chuck}}' — 'chuck' is {{c3::patronising}}, a stark contrast to 'dearest partner of greatness' at the start. He no longer relies on her; their {{c4::partnership has collapsed}}.

At the end, Macbeth: '{{c1::those in command move only in command, nothing in love}}' — his men obey him only from {{c2::fear}}, not loyalty. Ambition has destroyed every relationship he had; he rules in {{c3::total isolation}}.

At the end, on hearing of Lady Macbeth's death, Macbeth: '{{c1::Tomorrow, and tomorrow, and tomorrow}} / Creeps in this petty pace from day to day' — the triple epizeuxis mimics the unbearable repetition of time. Life is now '{{c2::a tale told by an idiot, full of sound and fury, signifying nothing}}'.

Macbeth's nihilism: 'Life's but a {{c1::walking shadow}}, a poor player / it is a {{c2::tale told by an idiot}}, full of sound and fury, {{c3::signifying nothing}}' — life has become meaningless; the theatrical metaphor suggests existence itself is {{c4::performance without substance}}.

At the end, Macbeth realises: '{{c1::juggling fiends}} / That palter with us in a double sense' — the word means {{c2::deceiving}}; 'palter' means equivocating. Macbeth finally understands the witches' {{c3::equivocation}} — they told half-truths to engineer his {{c4::downfall}}.

Malcolm's closing speech: 'this {{c1::dead butcher}} and his {{c2::fiend-like queen}}' — natural order is restored. The word '{{c3::crown'd}}' at Scone signals the rightful King is back, completing the play's cyclical structure.

At the start, Lady Macbeth reads Macbeth's letter: 'yet do I fear thy nature; / It is too full o' the {{c1::milk of human kindness}}' — 'milk' implies {{c2::weakness and femininity}}, but also that she must {{c3::nurture}} him into murder.

At the start, Lady Macbeth reads his letter and reflects: '{{c1::Art not without ambition, but without / The illness should attend it}}' — she identifies that Macbeth has ambition but lacks the {{c2::moral corruption}} needed to act on it; she sees ruthlessness as a {{c3::necessity}}.

At the start, Lady Macbeth invokes spirits: 'Come you spirits / That tend on mortal thoughts, {{c1::unsex me here}} / And fill me from the crown to the toe top-full / Of {{c2::direst cruelty}}' — she must reject her {{c3::gender}} to commit murder.

At the start, Lady Macbeth: '{{c1::Come to my woman's breasts / And take my milk for gall}}' — she asks for her maternal instinct (milk) to be replaced by {{c2::poison (gall)}}; motherhood and {{c3::femininity}} are presented as obstacles to power.

At the start, Lady Macbeth: '{{c1::Pall thee in the dunnest smoke of hell}} / That my keen knife see not the wound it makes' — she calls for {{c2::literal darkness}} to conceal the murder from heaven. 'Dunnest' (darkest) shows she {{c3::actively summons evil}} rather than passively accepting it.

At the start, Lady Macbeth advises Macbeth: 'look like the {{c1::innocent flower}} / But be the {{c2::serpent under't}}' — the biblical {{c3::serpent}} imagery links her to Eve in the Garden of Eden, tempting Macbeth to sin.

At the start, Lady Macbeth: '{{c1::I would, while it was smiling in my face}}, have {{c2::plucked my nipple from his boneless gums}} / And {{c3::dashed the brains out}}' — her most shocking line: she would kill her own infant for ambition. This {{c4::inverts all maternal instinct}} and shows her moral corruption is total.

At the start, after Lady Macbeth persuades him, Macbeth: '{{c1::Bring forth men-children only / For thy undaunted mettle should compose / Nothing but males}}' — the irony is that he {{c2::praises}} her masculine qualities while dismissing feminine ones, yet later she is {{c3::destroyed}} by the guilt he suppresses.

Lady Macbeth: '{{c1::Was the hope drunk / Wherein you dressed yourself?}} Hath it slept since? / And wakes it now to look so {{c2::green and pale}}?' — she uses the metaphor of drunkenness to mock his {{c3::cowardice}}; the phrase has connotations of {{c4::illness and weakness}}, reducing the celebrated warrior to a {{c5::sickly, timid child}}.

Lady Macbeth: '{{c1::When you durst do it, then you were a man}}; / And, to be more than what you were, you would / Be so much more the man.' — she weaponises {{c2::masculinity}} against Macbeth, equating courage with manhood. Shakespeare later has Macbeth use this exact technique on the hired murderers, showing how her manipulation has {{c3::infected his language and methods}}.

Lady Macbeth: '{{c1::A little water clears us of this deed}} / How easy is it then!' — her confidence that guilt can be washed away is completely undone by her sleepwalking. By the end she cries '{{c2::all the perfumes of Arabia will not sweeten this little hand}}'. Shakespeare uses this structural reversal to argue that {{c3::guilt is inescapable}} — the certainty that made her strong becomes the thing that {{c4::destroys her}}.

At the end, Lady Macbeth sleepwalks: '{{c1::Out, damned spot!}} Out, I say! — Hell is murky!' — the earlier claim 'a little water clears us of this deed' is completely undone. Her language falls out of {{c2::iambic pentameter}} — even her speech is now disordered.

Lady Macbeth's sleepwalking speech does NOT follow {{c1::iambic pentameter}} — her language has {{c2::broken down}}. This structural choice reflects her {{c3::loss of control}} over even basic functions like speech. Earlier she spoke in perfect verse; now she {{c4::cannot}}.

At the end, sleepwalking, Lady Macbeth: '{{c1::The Thane of Fife had a wife: where is she now?}}' — she has internalised the guilt of {{c2::Lady Macduff's murder}}, showing guilt for crimes she did not directly commit, proving she is not the {{c3::unfeeling monster}} she claimed to be.

At the end, Lady Macbeth: '{{c1::All the perfumes of Arabia will not sweeten this little hand}}' — the hyperbole shows guilt is {{c2::overwhelming and permanent}}. She earlier said 'a little water clears us of this deed' — the {{c3::irony}} is complete. Her hand is 'little', yet the guilt is {{c4::infinite}}.

At the end, Lady Macbeth: '{{c1::What is done cannot be undone}}' and '{{c2::Hell is murky}}' — resignation and dread. The confident planner who said 'a little water' now fears {{c3::hell}}. The monosyllabic simplicity creates a sense of {{c4::childlike terror}} — she is broken.

Lady Macbeth (blood motif): '{{c1::Who would have thought the old man to have had so much blood in him?}}' — 'old man' is deliberately {{c2::dehumanising}}: she cannot name Duncan because naming him forces her to acknowledge his {{c3::humanity}}. The image has consumed her unconscious so completely that it replays involuntarily in {{c4::sleep}}.

At the start, after the witches' prophecy, Banquo warns Macbeth: 'What! Can the {{c1::devil speak true}}?' — Banquo immediately suspects the witches. He later calls them '{{c2::instruments of darkness}}' who 'win us with honest trifles to betray us in {{c3::deepest consequence}}' — Banquo is the {{c4::antithesis}} of Macbeth: tempted but choosing to resist.

Banquo: '{{c1::If you can look into the seeds of time, / And say which grain will grow and which will not}}' — he uses the same {{c2::plant imagery}} as the witches but with {{c3::scepticism}}. He challenges them rather than being '{{c4::rapt withal}}' like Macbeth.

Banquo: '{{c1::Or have we eaten on the insane root / That takes the reason prisoner?}}' — he immediately questions whether the witches are {{c2::hallucination}} not reality, showing his {{c3::rational mind}}. He is Macbeth's {{c4::foil}} — same prophecy, opposite response.

Banquo before sleeping: '{{c1::Restrain in me the cursed thoughts that nature / Gives way to in repose}}' — he prays for protection from {{c2::dark temptation}}. Unlike Macbeth he {{c3::actively resists}} evil. '{{c4::Keep my bosom franchised and allegiance clear}}' — loyalty to Duncan is his priority.

Banquo's dying words: '{{c1::Fly, good Fleance, fly, fly, fly!}}' — the triple repetition and imperative show {{c2::desperate urgency}}. Banquo sacrifices himself so Fleance can escape, fulfilling the witches' prophecy that '{{c3::thou shalt get kings}}' — his final act is one of {{c4::selfless loyalty}}.

Banquo: '{{c1::I must become a borrower of the night / For a dark hour or twain}}' — the night imagery aligns him momentarily with {{c2::darkness}}, but his reason for being out is {{c3::innocent}}. Shakespeare uses this to create dramatic irony — Macbeth sees Banquo as a {{c4::threat}} when he is simply a loyal friend.

Duncan: '{{c1::There's no art / To find the mind's construction in the face}}' — said of the treacherous Thane of Cawdor, immediately before Macbeth enters; the dramatic irony is that Duncan makes the {{c2::same mistake twice}}.

Duncan: '{{c1::What he hath lost noble Macbeth hath won}}' — the {{c2::lexical field of winning and losing}} hints that fortunes may change suddenly. The {{c3::repetition/antithesis}} creates a sense of inevitability — linking to {{c4::fate}}.

Duncan: '{{c1::I have begun to plant thee}}, and will labour to make thee full of growing' — the plant metaphor presents Duncan as a {{c2::nurturing, natural king}}. Macbeth will later refuse to grow naturally, instead {{c3::usurping}} — he becomes the '{{c4::weed}}' Malcolm must drown.

Duncan rewards Macbeth: '{{c1::I have begun to plant thee, and will labour / To make thee full of growing}}' — the gardening metaphor presents Duncan as a {{c2::nurturing}} king. Macbeth will later corrupt this imagery, becoming the '{{c3::weeds}}'.

Macbeth describes Duncan after killing him: 'His silver skin {{c1::laced with his golden blood}} / and his gashed stabs looked like a {{c2::breach in nature}}' — the precious metals ('silver', 'golden') show Duncan's {{c3::regal sanctity}}; the phrase confirms regicide is a {{c4::cosmic violation}}, not just a political crime.

Macduff on Duncan: '{{c1::most sainted King}}' — the superlative and religious adjective show Duncan's legacy outlasts his death. His {{c2::goodness}} is confirmed by those who served him, creating a {{c3::stark contrast}} with Macbeth who is called '{{c4::dead butcher}}'.

Duncan is simultaneously Macbeth's {{c1::cousin}}, {{c2::king}}, and {{c3::guest}}. Shakespeare makes his murder a triple violation: killing his host broke the sacred laws of {{c4::hospitality}}; regicide violated the {{c5::Divine Right of Kings}}; killing kin broke bonds of {{c6::blood}}. Even Macbeth acknowledges this — 'his virtues / Plead like {{c7::angels, trumpet-tongued}}, against the deep damnation of his taking-off.'

Macbeth: introduced as 'brave Macbeth' who 'unseamed him from the {{c1::nave to the chaps}}' and 'smoked with {{c2::bloody execution}}' — he is celebrated for the same {{c3::violence}} that will later destroy him. The irony: his {{c4::heroism and villainy}} use identical methods.

Macduff learning his family were killed: '{{c1::O hell-kite! All? What, all my pretty chickens / And their dam at one fell swoop?}}' — the domestic tenderness of 'pretty chickens' contrasts with the violent 'hell-kite'; this is the moment Macbeth {{c2::crosses from political tyranny to killing the entirely innocent}}, losing all audience sympathy.

Macduff on Scotland: '{{c1::Bleed, bleed, poor country! / Great tyranny, lay thou thy basis sure, for goodness dare not check thee}}' — Scotland itself is {{c2::bleeding}} (illness/blood motif); tyranny is so entrenched that {{c3::virtue is powerless against it}}. Macduff is the {{c4::moral voice}} of the play.

Macduff confronts Macbeth: '{{c1::Turn, hell-hound, turn!}}' — the compound noun 'hell-hound' reduces Macbeth to a {{c2::monstrous animal}}. He then reveals: '{{c3::Macduff was from his mother's womb / Untimely ripped}}' — born by caesarean, technically '{{c4::not of woman born}}' — the witches' equivocation is exposed.

Macduff: '{{c1::Behold where stands / The usurper's cursed head}}' — natural order is restored; Malcolm is crowned. Macbeth is now labelled '{{c2::usurper}}' — his rule was never legitimate. Compared to '{{c3::brave Macbeth}}' at the start, the trajectory confirms the {{c4::tragic arc}} is complete.

Macduff: '{{c1::I could play the woman with mine eyes / And braggart with my tongue}}' — but he chooses action instead: '{{c2::I must also feel it as a man}}'. Shakespeare shows true masculinity includes {{c3::grief}} — contrasting with Macbeth and Lady Macbeth's perversion of 'manliness' through {{c4::violence and dominance}}.

At the end, in England, Malcolm lists the '{{c1::king-becoming graces}}' — justice, verity, temperance, stableness, bounty, perseverance, mercy, lowliness, devotion, patience, courage, fortitude. This speech appeals directly to {{c2::James I}}, who believed kingship was a moral duty. Duncan embodied {{c3::all}} of these qualities; Macbeth has {{c4::none}} by the end.

Malcolm tests Macduff's loyalty: Malcolm falsely claims to be '{{c1::voluptuous, avaricious}}' and deceitful to see if Macduff will still support him. When Macduff refuses — '{{c2::fit to govern? No, not to live}}' — Malcolm reveals the test. This echoes James I's advice that a king must be {{c3::cunning, not naively trusting}} like Duncan.

Donalbain: '{{c1::There's daggers in men's smiles: the near in blood, the nearer bloody}}' — 'near in blood' means {{c2::close relatives}}; he suspects those closest to the throne are most dangerous. He flees to {{c3::Ireland}} while Malcolm goes to {{c4::England}}. Their flight creates suspicion that they killed Duncan, which Macbeth exploits — they become collateral victims of {{c5::regicide}} despite being entirely innocent.
How does the bird motif develop across Macbeth?

At the very start: Macbeth is implicitly the eagle — Duncan asks if Macbeth was dismayed and the Captain replies 'Yes, as sparrows eagles' (the rebels feared Macbeth as sparrows fear eagles). He begins as the apex predator of the natural hierarchy. Then: the raven (Lady Macbeth — death omen); the martlet (Duncan's fatal naivety entering his death-chamber). During the murder: 'the owl that shriek'd, the fatal bellman' — nature's witness. Just after: a 'mousing owl' kills a 'falcon towering in her pride of place' — the mousing owl hunts low to the ground, at night, skulking; this mirrors Macbeth's cowardly nocturnal murder of a sleeping king rather than the open-battle heroism of his eagle days. At the end: Macduff calls him a 'hell-kite' — the eagle has become a monster. Eagle to mousing owl to hell-kite is Macbeth's entire moral fall told through birds.

At the very start, Duncan asks if Macbeth was dismayed in battle; the Captain replies: 'Yes, as {{c1::sparrows eagles}}, or the hare the lion' — Macbeth is implicitly the {{c2::eagle}}, the apex predator of the natural hierarchy. By the end Macduff calls him a '{{c3::hell-kite}}' — the {{c4::eagle has become a monster}}. Shakespeare traces Macbeth's entire moral fall through {{c5::bird imagery}}.

At the start, on hearing Duncan is coming, Lady Macbeth: 'The {{c1::raven}} himself is hoarse / That croaks the {{c2::fatal entrance}} of Duncan / Under {{c3::my}} battlements' — the raven is a bird of {{c4::death and ill omen}}; the possessive pronoun '{{c3::my}}' shows Lady Macbeth has already claimed ownership of {{c5::Duncan's murder}}.

At the start, Duncan praises Inverness: 'the {{c1::temple-haunting martlet}}...approve / By his loved mansionry that the heavens' breath / Smells wooingly here' — the martlet nests only in {{c2::safe, peaceful places}}, marking Duncan's {{c3::fatal naivety}}. Shakespeare uses {{c4::dramatic irony}}: the most innocent bird nests here; {{c5::murder}} waits inside.

During the murder, Lady Macbeth hears '{{c1::the owl that shriek'd, the fatal bellman / Which gives the stern'st good-night}}' — the owl functions as nature's own {{c2::witness}} to the crime. A bellman rang a bell at {{c3::executions}} — the owl announces the moment of {{c4::Duncan's death}}.
What is the significance of the 'mousing owl' killing the falcon in Act 2 Scene 4?

Just after the murder, the Old Man reports: 'A falcon, towering in her pride of place, / Was by a mousing owl hawked at and killed.' The falcon is a day creature associated with royalty; 'pride of place' is a falconry term for the apex of flight — Duncan at the pinnacle. The mousing owl hunts low to the ground, at night, skulking after mice — its method mirrors Macbeth's cowardly nocturnal regicide: not open battle (as the eagle would fight) but a hidden murder of a sleeping king. The disruption to the Great Chain of Being is made visible in nature.

Just after the murder, the Old Man reports: 'A falcon, {{c1::towering in her pride of place}}, / Was by a {{c2::mousing owl}} hawked at and killed' — '{{c1::pride of place}}' is a falconry term for the apex of a falcon's flight, placing Duncan at the {{c3::pinnacle}} of the natural hierarchy. The mousing owl hunts {{c4::low to the ground, at night}} — its method mirrors Macbeth's {{c5::cowardly nocturnal regicide}}: not open battle but a {{c6::skulking murder of a sleeping king}}. The disruption to the {{c7::Great Chain of Being}} is complete.

Bird motif structural irony: at the start Macbeth is implicitly the {{c1::eagle}} ('as sparrows eagles') — noble, apex, fights in {{c2::daylight}}. By Duncan's murder he has become the {{c3::mousing owl}} — nocturnal, low, {{c4::skulking}}. By the end Macduff calls him a '{{c5::hell-kite}}' — he has descended from {{c6::apex predator to monster}}. Shakespeare uses bird imagery to chart the entire {{c7::moral collapse}}.
How does the motif of blood develop through Macbeth?

Blood tracks the progression of guilt. After Duncan's murder Macbeth asks if Neptune's ocean can wash it away — it cannot. Lady Macbeth, who claimed 'a little water clears us', ends up unable to clean a 'damned spot'. 'Who would have thought the old man to have had so much blood in him' — the irony is complete. Blood literally and metaphorically stains them to their deaths.
How does the motif of clothing work in Macbeth?

Titles and power are described as garments — 'Why do you dress me in borrowed robes?' (Act 1). By Act 5: 'Now how he feels his title / Hang loose upon him, like a giant's robe / Upon a dwarfish thief' — Macbeth cannot fill the role of King. Power he has stolen will never truly fit him.
How does the motif of plants develop in Macbeth?

Duncan says 'I have begun to plant thee' (growth through loyalty). Lady Macbeth says 'look like the innocent flower but be the serpent under't' (corruption of nature). Macbeth fears 'the seed of Banquo kings!' By Act 5 he is 'a yellow leaf' — past his time, losing vitality. Malcolm will 'dew the sovereign flower and drown the weeds' — cyclical restoration.

Plant motif — start: Duncan '{{c1::plants}}' Macbeth naturally; Macbeth refuses to grow naturally and {{c2::usurps}} instead. Lady Macbeth: 'look like the {{c3::innocent flower / but be the serpent under't}}' — Macbeth is a {{c4::flower}} (weak, showy) hiding a serpent (evil). The plant imagery is immediately {{c5::corrupted}}.

Plant motif — middle: '{{c1::a fruitless crown}} and put a {{c2::barren sceptre}} in my grip' — Macbeth has no heir; his power cannot {{c3::grow or continue}}. 'To make them kings, the {{c4::seed of Banquo}} kings!' — Banquo's line WILL grow naturally; Macbeth's {{c5::cannot}}.

Plant motif — end: 'I have lived long enough: my way of life / Is fallen into the {{c1::sear, the yellow leaf}}' — 'yellow leaf' connotes {{c2::decay, lost potential, past one's time}}. Malcolm will '{{c3::dew the sovereign flower and drown the weeds}}' — nature is {{c4::restored}}; Macbeth is the weed.
How does the motif of sleep function in Macbeth?

After killing Duncan, Macbeth hears 'Macbeth shall sleep no more' — sleep represents innocence and peace which he has permanently destroyed. By Act 5, Lady Macbeth sleepwalks — sleep has been corrupted into torment. Banquo asks to 'restrain me in cursed thoughts' before sleeping — even he fears what his mind might do.
How does the illness motif function in Macbeth?

Scotland is described as diseased under Macbeth's rule: 'It weeps, it bleeds; and each new day a gash is added to her wounds'. Lady Macbeth's doctor says 'More need she the divine than the physician' — her illness is spiritual not physical. Malcolm as the 'antidote' restores health to Scotland at the end.
How does the plant motif develop through Macbeth?

Start: Duncan 'plants' Macbeth ('I have begun to plant thee') — natural, nurturing growth. Banquo speaks of 'seeds of time' — questioning which grain will grow. Lady Macbeth uses 'innocent flower / serpent under't' — corrupting the plant imagery. Middle: Macbeth has a 'fruitless crown / barren sceptre' — his rule cannot grow naturally. End: Macbeth is a 'yellow leaf' — past his time, decaying. Malcolm will 'dew the sovereign flower and drown the weeds' — cyclical restoration of natural order.

Illness motif — middle: '{{c1::O full of scorpions is my mind, dear wife!}}' — Macbeth's guilt manifests as painful, venomous creatures in his mind. Scotland also suffers: '{{c2::It weeps, it bleeds; and each new day a gash is added to her wounds}}' — the country shares its ruler's {{c3::sickness}}.

Illness motif — end: Doctor on Lady Macbeth: '{{c1::Unnatural deeds / Do breed unnatural troubles; infected minds}}' and '{{c2::her eyes are open, but their sense is shut}}' — guilt has become {{c3::literal illness}}. Malcolm is the '{{c4::antidote}}' who restores Scotland to health at the close of the play.
What is the timing and mark breakdown for Paper 1 Section A (Macbeth)?

34 marks total: AO1 (12 marks), AO2 (12 marks), AO3 (6 marks), plus 4 marks for close reading of the extract. One extended essay responding to both the given extract and the text as a whole. Suggested timing: 50–55 minutes including 10 minutes reading and planning.

Paper 1 Section A (Macbeth): {{c1::34}} marks total. Spend {{c2::50–55}} minutes including {{c3::10}} minutes reading and planning. AO1 = {{c4::12}} marks (ideas/argument), AO2 = {{c5::12}} marks (language/structure/form), AO3 = {{c6::6}} marks (context). You must write about both the {{c7::extract}} and the {{c8::text as a whole}}.
How does the mark-a-minute rule apply to the Macbeth question?

A mark a minute is typically a good guide. Section A is 34 marks — so aim for roughly 34 minutes of writing, leaving 10 minutes for reading and planning (total: ~44 minutes, fitting within the 50–55 minute window). If you are still writing after 55 minutes, move on to Jekyll regardless — an incomplete Jekyll answer loses more marks than a slightly shorter Macbeth one.
How does Lord of the Flies respond to The Coral Island?

The Coral Island (1858, R.M. Ballantyne) shows three boys marooned on an island who remain civilised and heroic. Golding was 'unsatisfied with how it espoused myths' and deliberately inverts it — in LOTF the boys are not saved by their inherent goodness but destroyed by their inherent evil. Golding believed man was 'diseased and fallen', not perfectible.
What are the key allegorical layers in Lord of the Flies?

Religious allegory: island = Garden of Eden, scar = Fall of Man, Simon = Christ figure, Castle Rock = Purgatory, sea = heaven. War allegory: Piggy = the Jews (victimised, vulnerable), Jack = Nazi leader (manipulates, brutalises). Philosophical: Freud (id/ego/superego), Darwin (regression), Maslow (hierarchy of needs). Political: Ralph = democracy, Jack = totalitarianism.
How is Simon presented in Lord of the Flies?

Simon is a Christ-figure — he retreats alone into nature, has a visionary encounter with evil (the LOTF), and is killed before he can share the truth. He alone understands 'the beast' is within the boys themselves. His death in Chapter 9 is the moral turning point of the novel — the boys have killed true goodness. His body is carried out to sea 'with a brightness' around it — a halo image.
What are the key events and their chapter names in Lord of the Flies?

Ch1: The Sound of the Shell (arrival, Ralph as chief). Ch4: Painted Faces (Jack paints face, ship missed). Ch8: Gift for the Darkness (Jack leaves, LOTF created). Ch9: A View to a Death (Simon killed). Ch10: The Shell and the Glasses (Piggy's glasses stolen). Ch11: Castle Rock (Piggy killed, conch destroyed). Ch12: Cry of the Hunters (Ralph hunted, naval officer arrives).
What does the naval officer's arrival at the end represent?

The officer expects to find boys playing 'a jolly good show' like The Coral Island — naively assuming civilisation. His presence forces the boys back into childhood. He turns away weeping, not wanting to see what they have become. Golding ends on the officer 'resting his eyes on the trim cruiser in the distance' — the wider war adults are fighting, suggesting the boys' violence is merely a microcosm of adult civilisation's own savagery.
What is the biographical context of Golding and how does it shape LOTF?

Golding served as a naval officer in WWII — he participated in the Normandy D-Day landings and hunted U-boats. He witnessed firsthand what ordinary men were capable of under pressure. This destroyed any idealistic view of human nature he had held. He said: 'I began to see what people were capable of doing.' LOTF is his direct response — an argument that evil is not external but innate in all humans, including the civilised English boys society held up as ideals.

Golding was a {{c1::naval officer}} in WWII who participated in the {{c2::D-Day landings}}. His war experience convinced him that {{c3::evil is innate}} in all humans — not a product of poverty or environment. LOTF is his argument against the {{c4::comfortable myth}} that civilised people are inherently good.
How does Golding's response to The Coral Island function as AO3?

The Coral Island (Ballantyne, Victorian era) showed British boys as naturally civilised, heroic and morally superior — a reflection of imperial ideology. Golding deliberately inverts this: same names (Ralph, Jack), same island, but the outcome is savagery. AO3: Golding is dismantling the Victorian/imperial idea of British moral superiority. He argues that civilisation is not a natural state but a fragile construct — remove the rules and any human will revert.

Golding inverts {{c1::The Coral Island}} (Ballantyne) — a {{c2::Victorian}} novel that showed British boys as naturally {{c3::civilised and heroic}}. This reflected {{c4::imperial ideology}}. Golding uses the same names ({{c5::Ralph and Jack}}) to make the contrast deliberate — civilisation is not natural but {{c6::fragile}}.
How does the post-WWII context shape the meaning of LOTF?

Written in the early post-WWII era when the Holocaust and atomic bomb had shattered faith in human progress. The civilised, educated Europeans had committed genocide. AO3: Golding's boys are a microcosm of this — educated, middle-class English boys from the 'best' society committing murder. The naval officer at the end, representing adult 'civilisation', is himself part of a war. Golding's message: there is no safe civilisation to return to. The adults are doing exactly what the boys did, just with bigger weapons.

Written in the {{c1::post-WWII}} era after the {{c2::Holocaust}} proved that educated, 'civilised' Europeans could commit {{c3::genocide}}. The naval officer arrives to 'rescue' the boys — but he represents adult {{c4::warfare}}, not safety. Golding's message: there is no {{c5::civilised world}} to return to.
How does Freudian psychology function as AO3 in LOTF?

Golding's characterisation maps onto Freud's model of the psyche: Ralph = ego (rational, seeks balance between impulse and society). Piggy = superego (conscience, reason, rules). Jack = id (pure instinct, desire, pleasure principle). AO3: The novel shows what happens when the id (Jack) overwhelms the ego (Ralph) and destroys the superego (Piggy). This is Golding's argument that without social structures, instinct will always win.

Freudian allegory in LOTF: Ralph = {{c1::ego}} (rational balance). Piggy = {{c2::superego}} (conscience and reason). Jack = {{c3::id}} (pure instinct and desire). When Jack destroys Piggy, the {{c4::id}} has overwhelmed {{c5::conscience}} — Golding argues this is what happens without {{c6::social structures}}.
How does the religious allegory in LOTF function as AO3?

The island begins as a Garden of Eden — paradise, no adults, freedom. The 'scar' left by the plane crash represents the Fall of Man — humans have already corrupted this paradise upon arrival. Simon is a Christ figure — he alone understands the truth, retreats alone, and is killed before he can share it. The Lord of the Flies (Beelzebub/Devil) suggests evil is not something that arrives but something that was already there. AO3: Golding was a Christian — he believed in original sin, that humans are born fallen.

Religious allegory: the island = {{c1::Garden of Eden}}. The plane crash 'scar' = {{c2::the Fall}}. Simon = {{c3::Christ figure}} (alone understands truth, killed before he can share it). Lord of the Flies = {{c4::Beelzebub (the Devil)}}. Golding was a {{c5::Christian}} who believed in {{c6::original sin}} — evil is innate, not learned.

Golding described The Coral Island as having '{{c1::rotted to compost}}' in his mind — a metaphor showing how Ballantyne's {{c2::optimistic Victorian adventure story}} decomposed into the {{c3::darker, more realistic}} themes of LOTF. The organic metaphor suggests the transformation was {{c4::natural and inevitable}}.
How does Golding's 'rotted to compost' quote function as AO3?

Golding said The Coral Island had 'rotted to compost' in his mind. This tells us his engagement with Ballantyne was not conscious subversion but a deep, gradual transformation of the same raw material. Just as compost is organic matter broken down and rebuilt into something new, Golding took the same island, same boys, same names — and rebuilt them into the opposite argument. It also implies decay is natural — Golding sees the collapse of civilisation as inevitable, not exceptional.

Golding: '{{c1::Man produces evil as a bee produces honey}}' — the simile presents evil as {{c2::natural and effortless}}, not exceptional. Just as a bee cannot help producing honey, humans {{c3::cannot help}} producing evil. This is Golding's central argument.
How does Golding's 'man produces evil as a bee produces honey' function as AO3?

The simile presents evil as natural, instinctive and unavoidable — not a product of bad upbringing, poverty or circumstance. A bee doesn't choose to make honey; it simply does. Golding is arguing that humans don't choose evil — it is their default state when civilisation's rules are removed. This directly challenges The Coral Island's assumption that educated British boys would naturally behave well. In the novel, Jack doesn't become evil — he simply stops suppressing what was always there.

Golding believed '{{c1::the perfectibility of man}}' is unachievable and that man is a '{{c2::diseased and fallen being}}'. As a '{{c3::fabulist}}', he sees the corruption of mankind and wishes to {{c4::educate}} us about the depths of evil we are capable of in the right situations.
How does Golding present Ralph's relationship with Piggy as significant?

Piggy consistently feeds Ralph's ideas — 'I knew that!' Ralph insists, when Piggy reminds him about the smoke signal. Golding shows Ralph cannot lead without Piggy's intellect. The syntactic parallelism of Piggy's 'I'll come too' mirrors Ralph's 'I'll bring 'em back' — showing Piggy's eager loyalty. Ralph's belated recognition ('the true, wise friend called Piggy') at the end is Golding's indictment: society destroys its most rational voices then mourns them too late.
How does Jack fail to recognise Simon's death as murder?

After Simon's death, Jack claims 'the beast might try to come in again…he came disguised. He may come again.' Jack has so fully committed to the fiction of the beast that he cannot process Simon's death as real. This wilful blindness protects his authority — acknowledging Simon's murder would expose the savagery of his tribe. Golding shows how power depends on maintaining collective delusion.
How does Golding use the sea to treat Piggy and Simon similarly?

'The sea breathed again in a long slow sigh, the body of Piggy had gone.' Like Simon, Piggy's body is taken by the sea. The sea in the religious allegory represents heaven — both Simon (Christ figure) and Piggy (intellect/reason) are received by it. Golding suggests that true goodness and true wisdom are both destroyed by the boys, and both transcend the island's savagery in death.
How does Simon function as a Christ figure in LOTF?

Simon retreats alone into the forest (Christ in the wilderness). He has a vision/revelation about the true nature of the beast — the Lord of the Flies tells him 'Fancy thinking the Beast was something you could hunt and kill!' He rushes back to share the truth with the others. He is killed before he can do so, by those he sought to save, at night, in a frenzy — mistaken for the monster he was coming to disprove. His body is carried to the sea (heaven) with a brightness around it. He is the novel's moral martyr.
What does each key symbol represent in LOTF according to your teacher?

Ralph = order and democracy (civilising impact). Piggy = pragmatic intellect and logic. Jack = savagery, authoritarian/totalitarian rule. Simon = sensitivity, spirituality, wisdom. Roger = cruelty, sadism (antithesis of Simon). Conch = civilisation and democracy. Piggy's glasses = science and technology. Fire = hope of salvation. The Beast = human nature (desire to be savage). The scar = mankind's destructive influence on paradise. Lord of the Flies (pig's head) = physical manifestation of the beast. Adults = civilisation and social order.
What is the war allegory in LOTF?

Piggy = the Jews — victimised, vulnerable, marginalised, ultimately killed. Jack = Nazi leader — manipulates through fear, bullies, rules through force. The island's descent mirrors Europe's under fascism: rational voices suppressed, mob mentality takes over, the vulnerable are sacrificed.

Castle Rock = {{c1::Purgatory}} (a place of suffering for sinners). The Sea = {{c2::Heaven}} (receives Simon and Piggy). The island = {{c3::Garden of Eden}}. The scar = {{c4::the Fall of Man}} (how man destroys paradise). Simon = {{c5::Christ}}. Lord of the Flies = {{c6::Beelzebub/the Devil}}.
How does fire develop symbolically?

Fire has dual symbolism. Controlled = hope, civilisation, rescue (Ralph's signal fire). Destructive = savagery (Jack lets fire die to hunt; boys' fire on Castle Rock destroys). The final irony: Jack sets the whole island ablaze to hunt Ralph, but the smoke brings the naval officer. Destruction accidentally achieves rescue. Golding suggests civilisation's collapse may paradoxically be what saves us.
How does the beast develop as a motif in Lord of the Flies?

At the start: a littlun reports 'a beastie, a snake-thing' — Ralph dismisses it; the beast is merely a child's nightmare. Middle: fear multiplies — the beast from the water, then the dead parachutist (mistaken for a living monster by Sam and Eric), giving the beast a tangible physical form. Jack exploits this fear to seize control, making hunting the beast the justification for his tribe's savagery. Simon alone understands: 'maybe it's only us'. In the middle, the Lord of the Flies confirms it: 'Fancy thinking the Beast was something you could hunt and kill! You knew, didn't you? I'm part of you?' Simon is killed before he can share the truth — the boys destroy the very knowledge that would have saved them. At the end: Ralph weeps for 'the darkness of man's heart' — acknowledging the beast is real, internal, and universal. Golding's argument: the beast cannot be hunted because it lives inside every human.

At the start, a littlun reports: 'He says there's a {{c1::beastie, a snake-thing}}, ever so big' — Ralph {{c2::dismisses}} it. The beast begins as a {{c3::child's nightmare}}; Golding uses its growth to track the boys' {{c4::regression from reason to fear}}.

In the middle, Simon is the only boy to question the beast's nature: '{{c1::What I mean is… maybe it's only us}}' — the pronoun '{{c2::us}}' is the novel's central revelation. The boys {{c3::cannot hear}} him; he is {{c4::laughed at and ignored}}, just as rational voices are in Golding's post-war world.

In the middle, the dead parachutist gives the beast a {{c1::physical form}} — Sam and Eric report something '{{c2::big and horrid}}' sitting on the mountain. Jack uses this to {{c3::seize power}}: the beast becomes a tool of {{c4::political control}}, not genuine fear. This is Golding's comment on how {{c5::leaders manufacture terror}} to manipulate the masses.

In the middle, the Lord of the Flies speaks to Simon: '{{c1::Fancy thinking the Beast was something you could hunt and kill! You knew, didn't you? I'm part of you?}}' — the beast {{c2::cannot be destroyed}} because it is {{c3::inside every boy}}. Simon is killed before he can share this truth — the boys destroy {{c4::the one voice of reason}}.
How does the blood motif develop through LOTF using your teacher's analysis?

Stage 1 — 'unbearable blood': Jack cannot kill; 'unbearable' shows it is intolerable to him. Stage 2 — 'grimaced distastefully' and looked for something to 'clean them': he can kill but is disgusted. Stage 3 — blood 'spouted' over him and he says 'Look': he displays it as a status symbol. Stage 4 — he 'giggled' and 'flicked' it, then 'rubbed' it on other boys: he plays with blood and corrupts others. Progressive transformation from horror to disgust to pride to contagion.

Blood motif Stage 1: '{{c1::unbearable blood}}' — Jack {{c2::cannot}} kill; the adjective shows he finds it morally {{c3::intolerable}}. Stage 2: he '{{c4::grimaced distastefully}}' and tried to '{{c5::clean}}' his hands — he can kill but is {{c6::repulsed}}.

Blood motif Stage 3: the blood '{{c1::spouted}}' over Jack and he says '{{c2::Look}}' — demonstrating {{c3::pride}} in killing, using it as a {{c4::status symbol}}. Stage 4: he '{{c5::giggled}}' and '{{c6::flicked}}' the blood, then '{{c7::rubbed}}' it on other boys — {{c8::corrupting them}} into violence.
How does the conch develop symbolically through LOTF?

Introduction: Ralph 'held up the conch for silence' and Piggy 'cradled the great cream shell' — it commands automatic respect. Middle: even Jack 'held out his hands for the conch...holding the delicate thing carefully in his sooty hands' — he still observes the rules, but his 'sooty hands' pollute it. Decline: Ralph 'cradled the conch' while weeping about the boys' descent — it is now associated with loss. End: the conch 'exploded into a thousand white fragments' as Piggy dies — democracy and intellect destroyed simultaneously. The skull then 'gleamed as white as ever the conch had done' — evil replaces civilisation.

Conch decline: Jack '{{c1::held the delicate thing carefully in his sooty hands}}' — he still respects it but his '{{c2::sooty}}' hands pollute it. Ralph '{{c3::cradling the conch, rocked himself to and fro}}' — the conch now associated with {{c4::grief and loss}} of control. Final: '{{c5::the conch exploded into a thousand white fragments and ceased to exist}}' — democracy {{c6::destroyed}}.

At the end, after the conch shatters, the pig's skull '{{c1::gleamed as white as ever the conch had done and seemed to jeer at him cynically}}' — {{c2::evil and savagery}} have replaced {{c3::civilisation and democracy}}. The conch's colour and gleam now belong to the {{c4::Lord of the Flies}}.

Ralph's hair Stage 1: 'the boy with {{c1::fair hair}}' — 'fair' connotes {{c2::justice and openness}}. Stage 2: 'Ralph pushed back his {{c3::fair hair}}' — the verb 'pushed' shows {{c4::effort}} and the need to look {{c5::presentable}} that the boys are abandoning.

Ralph's hair Stage 3: 'the hair was {{c1::creeping into his eyes again}}' — '{{c2::creeping}}' creates a sinister tone; barbarism is {{c3::advancing unbeknownst}} to Ralph. '{{c4::Again}}' reinforces its constant advancement. Stage 4: '{{c5::he would like to have scissors and cut this filthy hair…cut this filthy hair right back to half an inch}}' — desperate to {{c6::reassert civilisation}}.

Ralph's hair final stage: '{{c1::in the middle of them, with filthy body, matted hair, and unwiped nose, Ralph wept}}' — he is now {{c2::indistinguishable from the savages}}. The naval officer sees '{{c3::a little boy}}' — the loss of identity is {{c4::complete}}. His hair charts the {{c5::entire arc of the novel}}.
How do Piggy's glasses develop as a motif using your teacher's analysis?

Initially: 'thick spectacles' — strong sight AND strong material; Piggy processes the environment 'safely'. Later: 'wiping his glasses' — becoming sullied literally and metaphorically; 'wiping' shows resistance to corruption. 'A deep pink V' on his nose — the weight of intellect has physically marked him. In the middle: Jack strikes Piggy and one lens shatters — the fragmentation of rational sight mirrors the fragmentation of civilisation; Piggy is already half-blind. After Simon's murder: Piggy 'removed his glasses and shifted the one lens to the other eye' — he refuses to see clearly, choosing falsehood to avoid guilt. Near the end: Jack's tribe steals the glasses at night ('The Shell and the Glasses'); they are used to start Jack's fire at Castle Rock — intellect is seized and weaponised by savagery; Piggy must beg for the return of his own ability to see. Glasses destroyed with Piggy = death of rational thought.

Piggy's glasses: 'thick spectacles' ({{c1::strong sight and strong material}}). Later 'wiping his glasses' — {{c2::resistance to corruption}}. 'A deep {{c3::pink V}}' on his nose — weight of intellect, constant responsibility has {{c4::physically harmed}} him. After Simon's murder: Piggy '{{c5::removed his glasses and shifted the one lens to the other eye}}' — refusing to {{c6::see clearly}}, choosing falsehood over guilt.

In the middle, Jack strikes Piggy and '{{c1::one side of his glasses was missing}}' — rational sight is already {{c2::half-destroyed}} before the end; the progressive destruction of the glasses mirrors the progressive {{c3::collapse of civilisation}}.

Near the end, Jack's tribe raids the camp at night and steals Piggy's glasses — the chapter is titled '{{c1::The Shell and the Glasses}}', linking the theft directly to the {{c2::conch}}: both instruments of civilisation are seized. At Castle Rock, the glasses are used to start {{c3::Jack's fire}} — {{c4::intellect, torn from its owner, is weaponised by savagery}}. Piggy must beg Jack for the return of his own {{c5::ability to see}}.

Ralph's hair motif: Ralph '{{c1::discovered dirt and decay}}, understood how much he disliked perpetually {{c2::flicking the tangled hair}} out of his eyes' — the 'tangled hair' represents the chaos on the island; 'perpetually' shows {{c3::exhaustion}} at the endless struggle to maintain {{c4::order}}.

Ralph '{{c1::weeps for the end of innocence, the darkness of man's heart}}' — 'innocence' refers to the myth of human perfectibility which has been destroyed. '{{c2::darkness}}' is the inner evil Ralph has now recognised {{c3::in himself}} as well as in others.

The naval officer 'rest[s] his eyes on the {{c1::trim cruiser in the distance}}' — the warship is emblematic of the {{c2::wider destruction}} adult civilisation is creating. Golding's message: the boys' violence was {{c3::insignificant}} compared to what the 'civilised' world is doing. His word '{{c4::show}}' (the officer expects a 'jolly good show') reveals the {{c5::falsehood}} of performing civilised behaviour.

At the start, Ralph is introduced as having '{{c1::golden body}}' and 'a {{c2::mildness about his mouth and eyes that proclaimed no devil}}' — his physical appearance signals {{c3::innate virtue and worthiness}}. He possesses 'a {{c4::stillness about him that marked him out}}' — natural leadership authority.

At the start, Ralph on rules: '{{c1::We ought to have more rules. Where the conch is, that's a meeting}}' — he immediately establishes {{c2::democracy and order}}. Later in the middle: 'Because the {{c3::rules are the only thing we've got}}' — Ralph understands that without rules, {{c4::civilisation collapses}}.

In the middle, Ralph develops blood lust during the hunt: '{{c1::Ralph too was fighting to get near, to get a handful of that brown, vulnerable flesh. The desire to squeeze and hurt was overmastering}}' — even the protagonist harbours {{c2::violence within}}. This fulfils Golding's argument that evil is {{c3::universal, not exceptional}}.

In the middle, Ralph loses faith in leadership: '{{c1::We can't keep one fire going. And they don't care. And what's more, I don't sometimes}}' and '{{c2::Fat lot of good we are. Three blind mice. I'll give up}}' — he begins to internalise the {{c3::failure of democracy}} on the island.

Ralph is dependent on Piggy: 'Only, decided Ralph, I can't think. Not like Piggy. Piggy could think. He could go {{c1::step by step inside that fat head of his}}…Piggy, for all his ludicrous body, had {{c2::brains}}.' — Ralph recognises his own weakness; {{c3::civilisation needs intellect}} to function.

In the middle, after Simon's death, Ralph's guilt: '{{c1::That was murder…Don't you understand, Piggy? The things that we did}}' — Ralph acknowledges complicity in Simon's death. 'He {{c2::flushed painfully}} at this admission that he had been present at the dance' — shame, not denial.

Near the end, Ralph fears savagery: '{{c1::I'm frightened. Of us. I want to go home}}' — the pronoun '{{c2::us}}' is key; he fears his own group, not an external beast. He also sees a former friend and thinks: 'This was not Bill. This was {{c3::a savage whose image refused to blend}} with that ancient picture of a boy in shorts and shirt.'

At the end, when the naval officer arrives, Ralph's final weeping: '{{c1::Ralph wept for the end of innocence, the darkness of man's heart, and the fall through the air of the true, wise friend called Piggy}}' — 'innocence' is the myth of human perfectibility; 'darkness of man's heart' is Golding's central thesis; naming Piggy as '{{c2::true, wise friend}}' shows Ralph's {{c3::belated recognition}} of his value.

At the start, Jack initially supports rules: '{{c1::I agree with Ralph. We've got to have rules and obey them. After all, we're not savages. We're English, and the English are the best at everything}}' — the {{c2::irony}} is devastating; the boy who most values being 'English' becomes the most {{c3::savage}}. Golding dismantles {{c4::imperial ideology}}.

At the start, Jack's introduction: 'something dark was fumbling along the beach' — the vague noun '{{c1::something}}' creates fear; he is then called '{{c2::the creature}}'. His party '{{c3::march approximately in step in two parallel lines}}' — {{c4::militaristic}} diction showing Jack as an intimidating, commanding leader.

Jack's physical description: 'he was {{c1::tall, thin, and bony}}; and his hair was {{c2::red}} beneath the {{c3::black cap}}' — 'red' connotes {{c4::danger and bloodshed}}; 'black' connotes {{c5::death and evil}}. His cap badge is '{{c6::golden}}' but unlike Ralph's natural golden body, it is only {{c7::worn and impermanent}}.

In the middle, Jack's bloodlust after his first kill: 'His mind was crowded with memories…knowledge that had come to them when they closed in on the struggling pig…they had {{c1::outwitted}} a living thing, {{c2::imposed their will}} upon it, taken away its {{c3::life like a long satisfying drink}}' — the simile 'satisfying drink' presents killing as an {{c4::addiction}}.

In the middle, when Jack first paints his face, the mask: '{{c1::the mask was a thing on its own, behind which Jack hid, liberated from shame and self-consciousness}}' — the mask removes moral responsibility. Golding originally titled the novel '{{c2::Strangers from Within}}' — the mask reveals the stranger {{c3::already inside}}.

In the middle, Jack uses the beast to seize power: '{{c1::Bollocks to the rules! We're strong – we hunt! If there's a beast, we'll hunt it down! We'll close in and beat and beat and beat—!}}' — the expletive shows contempt for democracy; the repetitive '{{c2::beat}}' shows commitment to {{c3::ferocity}}; 'we' shows his ability to {{c4::manipulate}}.

Near the end, when Jack establishes his own tribe at Castle Rock: '{{c1::the conch doesn't count at this end of the island}}' — he physically divides the island to {{c2::create his own jurisdiction}} beyond the rules. Power over {{c3::space becomes power over law}}.

Jack zoomorphism — progressive dehumanisation: '{{c1::dog-like}}' → 'for a minute became less a hunter than a {{c2::furtive thing, ape-like among the tangle of trees}}' → 'power lay in the brown swell of his forearms: {{c3::authority sat on his shoulder and chattered in his ear like an ape}}' — Jack {{c4::regresses}} from domesticated to primitive.

Jack orders corporal punishment: 'He got angry and made us tie {{c1::Wilfred}} up. He's been tied up for hours, {{c2::waiting}}' — the pointless, anticipatory cruelty shows Jack rules through {{c3::fear and sadism}} rather than respect. The Chief '{{c4::prodded Sam in the ribs…the prodding became rhythmic}}' — violence becomes {{c5::routine}}.

At the end, after Piggy's death, Jack: '{{c1::See? See? That's what you'll get! I meant that! There isn't a tribe for you any more! The conch is gone!}}' — the exclamatives show {{c2::glee and triumph}}. He then '{{c3::viciously, with full intention hurled his spear at Ralph}}' — 'full intention' removes any chance of accident; this is {{c4::premeditated murder}}.

At the start, Piggy is introduced as mature and adult-like: 'with the {{c1::martyred expression of a parent}} who has to keep up with the senseless ebullience of the children, he picked up the conch' — Piggy is {{c2::older in temperament}} than the others; he treats them as a {{c3::responsible adult}} would treat children.

Piggy is excluded: 'there had grown up tacitly among the bigguns the opinion that Piggy was {{c1::an outsider}}, not only by accent…but by {{c2::fat}}, and {{c3::ass-mar}} and {{c4::specs}}, and a certain {{c5::disinclination for manual labour}}' — Golding shows how society marginalises those who are {{c6::intellectually capable but physically different}}.

In the middle, Piggy on reason and science: '{{c1::Life…is scientific, that's what it is}}…I know there isn't no beast…but I know there isn't no fear either…{{c2::unless we get frightened of people}}' — Piggy identifies the real beast: {{c3::human fear itself}}. He is proved right; the 'beast' that kills Simon is the boys' own panic.

Piggy blossoms without Jack: 'Piggy was so full of {{c1::delight and expanding liberty}} in Jack's departure…that he helped to fetch wood' — Piggy is {{c2::liberated}} by Jack's absence, showing Jack's presence has been actively {{c3::suppressing rational voices}}.

Piggy on Jack: '{{c1::I'm scared of him…and that's why I know him. If you're scared of someone you hate him but you can't stop thinking about him}}' — Piggy's psychological insight is {{c2::more sophisticated}} than any other boy's. He understands Jack's {{c3::power over fear}} precisely because he has experienced it.

At the end, before his death at Castle Rock, Piggy refuses to be a savage: '{{c1::Which is better – to be a pack of painted niggers like you are, or to be sensible like Ralph is? Which is better – to have rules and agree, or to hunt and kill?}}' — Piggy's rhetorical questions are {{c2::unanswered}}; the boys' silence is Golding's answer: {{c3::savagery wins}}.

At the end, Piggy's death: 'Piggy, saying nothing, with no time even for a grunt, travelled through the air…his {{c1::head opened and stuff came out and turned red}}. Piggy's arms and legs {{c2::twitched a bit, like a pig's after it had been killed}}' — the simile comparing him to {{c3::a pig}} reduces him to the very animal the boys hunt. Death is {{c4::undignified and animalistic}}.

Piggy and the conch at death: '{{c1::by him stood Piggy still holding out the talisman, the fragile, shining beauty of the shell}}' — the word '{{c2::talisman}}' (a protective charm) shows Piggy's desperate belief that civilisation can protect him. It {{c3::cannot}}. The conch '{{c4::exploded into a thousand white fragments and ceased to exist}}' simultaneously.

At the start, Simon is introduced dismissively: '{{c1::He's always throwing a faint}}' — the boys mock his spirituality as {{c2::physical weakness}}. Yet Golding shows him acting with quiet {{c3::goodness}}: 'Simon found for them the fruit they could not reach, pulled off the choicest…passed them back down to the endless, outstretched hands.'

In the middle, Simon's affinity with nature in his secret clearing: 'the {{c1::candlebuds stirred. Their green sepals drew back a little and the white tips of the flowers rose delicately to meet the open air}}' — nature {{c2::responds to Simon's presence}}. This quasi-mystical connection marks him as a {{c3::spiritual figure}}, at harmony with the world the others are destroying.

Simon's selflessness: 'Simon, sitting between the twins and Piggy, {{c1::wiped his mouth and shoved his piece of meat over the rocks to Piggy}}' — a small, quiet act of {{c2::compassion}} that no other boy performs. Simon acts not for {{c3::status or reward}} but out of pure {{c4::goodness}}.

Jack threatens Simon: '{{c1::I'm warning you. I'm going to get angry. D'you see? You're not wanted. Understand? We are going to have fun on this island…So don't try it on, my poor misguided boy, or else—}}' — the dash leaves the threat {{c2::unfinished}}, which is more menacing. Jack identifies Simon as a threat to {{c3::his version of the island}}.

In the middle, Simon's death at the ritual dance: '{{c1::Kill the beast! Cut his throat! Spill his blood! Do him in!}}' — the imperative commands are a {{c2::chant}}, showing how mob mentality erases individual conscience. 'A silver shape beneath the steadfast constellations, Simon's dead body moved out toward the open sea' — the word '{{c3::steadfast}}' contrasts with the boys' {{c4::chaos}}, and the sea ({{c5::heaven}}) receives him.
What must a LOTF essay overview always include?

1. Era context FIRST: 'Writing in Post-WWII Britain...'. 2. Golding's authorial view stated directly. 3. A direct Golding quote — 'diseased and fallen being' or 'man produces evil as a bee produces honey'. 4. Label Golding as a 'fabulist'. 5. Clear thesis — how the character/theme functions. 6. Signal your argument: 'primarily Golding uses [X] to...'
What is the timing and mark breakdown for Paper 2 Section A (Lord of the Flies)?

34 marks total. Extended essay from a choice of two questions — whole text only, no extract given. Suggested timing: 45 minutes. There is no reading time for an extract, so begin planning and writing immediately.

Paper 2 Section A (Lord of the Flies): {{c1::34}} marks total. Spend {{c2::45}} minutes. Choose from {{c3::two}} questions — whole text essay, {{c4::no extract}}. AO1 = {{c5::12}} marks, AO2 = {{c6::12}} marks, AO3 = {{c7::6}} marks. Begin planning immediately — there is no reading time for an {{c8::extract}}.
How does the mark-a-minute rule apply to the Lord of the Flies question?

A mark a minute is typically a good guide. Section A is 34 marks — aim for roughly 34 minutes of writing, leaving about 5–8 minutes for planning (total: ~40–42 minutes, fitting within the 45 minute window). There is no extract to read, so planning time is shorter than Paper 1. Use the remaining minutes to check your argument is consistent.
What are the potential question focuses for Power and Conflict?

Power of time, nature, weather, people, memory. Conflict — internal, physical, emotional. Loss, truth, fighting. The exam will ask you to compare a printed poem with one of your choice from the anthology.
What should a good overview paragraph contain?
WHEN the poets are writing. WHY they are writing. Focus on the QUESTION. Summary of both poems. Which poems pair well and why. Evidence + structural comments. Relevant context for the argument.
What is the context of Ozymandias and how does it link to AO3?

Written in the Romantic era by Percy Bysshe Shelley, who was a radical — deeply opposed to tyranny and monarchy. The Romantic movement valued nature above human power. AO3: Shelley was responding to the arrogance of rulers like Napoleon. The poem argues that all human power is temporary — nature and time outlast every empire. This makes it a political poem as much as a philosophical one.

Ozymandias — AO3: Shelley was a {{c1::Romantic}} poet who opposed {{c2::tyranny and monarchy}}. The poem reflects the Romantic belief that {{c3::nature}} is more powerful than {{c4::human authority}}. The statue's decay is nature's verdict on {{c5::political arrogance}}.
What is the context of London and how does it link to AO3?

Written during the Industrial Revolution when London's poor faced extreme poverty and exploitation. Blake was a Romantic radical inspired by the French Revolution. AO3: Blake was directly criticising the institutions — Church, monarchy, government — that he believed oppressed ordinary people. The poem is an act of witness and protest. The 'mind-forg'd manacles' suggests people have been psychologically conditioned into accepting their oppression.

London — AO3: Written during the {{c1::Industrial Revolution}}. Blake was inspired by the {{c2::French Revolution}} and believed the {{c3::Church, monarchy and government}} were complicit in London's suffering. The poem is an act of {{c4::political protest}} — Blake is bearing witness to institutionalised oppression.
What is the context of My Last Duchess and how does it link to AO3?

Written by Browning in the Victorian era, set in Renaissance Italy. Based on a real historical figure — Alfonso II, Duke of Ferrara, whose first wife died suspiciously. AO3: Browning uses the dramatic monologue form so that the Duke condemns himself without realising it — the reader sees through his self-justification. Browning is critiquing patriarchal control of women, both in Renaissance Italy and implicitly in Victorian England, where women had very limited legal rights.

My Last Duchess — AO3: Browning uses {{c1::dramatic monologue}} so the Duke {{c2::condemns himself}} without realising it. Written in the {{c3::Victorian era}} when women had limited legal rights — Browning implicitly critiques {{c4::patriarchal control}} of women in both Renaissance Italy and his own time.
What is the context of Charge of the Light Brigade and how does it link to AO3?

Written by Tennyson (the Victorian Poet Laureate) shortly after the Battle of Balaclava during the Crimean War, based on a newspaper report. AO3: As Poet Laureate, Tennyson had a duty to honour the soldiers — but the poem is more complex than simple glorification. 'Someone had blundered' acknowledges the catastrophic mistake. Tennyson honours the soldiers' courage and obedience while quietly implicating those in command — he cannot name them directly in his official role.

Charge of the Light Brigade — AO3: Tennyson was {{c1::Poet Laureate}} — he had an official duty to honour soldiers. 'Someone had {{c2::blundered}}' quietly implicates those in command without naming them — Tennyson cannot be openly {{c3::critical}} in his official role. The poem honours {{c4::obedience}} while hinting at the cost of {{c5::blind duty}}.
What is the context of Exposure and how does it link to AO3?

Written by Wilfred Owen during WWI while convalescing from shellshock. Owen wrote to oppose propaganda that glamorised war — poets like Jessie Pope wrote 'Who's for the Game?' encouraging men to enlist. AO3: Owen's purpose was to tell the truth about the reality of trench warfare — that soldiers were not dying heroically in battle but were being killed slowly by cold, boredom and futility. He wanted to stop men enlisting under false impressions.

Exposure — AO3: Owen wrote to counter {{c1::war propaganda}} poets like {{c2::Jessie Pope}}. He had firsthand experience of {{c3::shellshock and trench warfare}}. His purpose was to convey the {{c4::truth}} of war to civilians who romanticised it — soldiers died of {{c5::cold and futility}}, not heroic combat.
What is the context of Bayonet Charge and how does it link to AO3?

Written by Ted Hughes in the post-WWII era, based on his father's WWI experiences. Hughes did not fight — he is writing imaginatively. AO3: Hughes's distance from the event allows him to focus on the universal psychological experience of combat rather than specific facts. The poem explores how ideology collapses in the face of physical reality — the soldier doesn't think of king or country, only survival. This universalises the anti-war message beyond one conflict.

Bayonet Charge — AO3: Hughes wrote in the {{c1::post-WWII}} era, based on his {{c2::father's}} experiences — he did not fight himself. His distance allows him to focus on the {{c3::universal psychology}} of combat. 'King, honour, human dignity, {{c4::etcetera}}' — ideology becomes meaningless when faced with physical {{c5::survival}}.
What is the context of Remains and how does it link to AO3?

Based on a real soldier's experience in Iraq, taken from a Channel 4 documentary about PTSD. Armitage uses colloquial, first-person voice to blur the line between poet and speaker. AO3: The poem criticises not just war but the institutional failure to support soldiers afterwards — Tromans was 'laughed out of the army' when he sought help. Armitage forces the reader to inhabit the soldier's perspective — we cannot remain detached bystanders.

Remains — AO3: Based on a real {{c1::Iraq War}} soldier's PTSD. The army {{c2::refused to help}} Guardsman Tromans. Armitage uses {{c3::colloquial first-person}} voice to force the reader into the soldier's perspective — we cannot remain {{c4::detached}}. The poem critiques both war and {{c5::institutional abandonment}} of veterans.
What is the context of War Photographer and how does it link to AO3?

Inspired by real war photographer Don McCullin, written by Carol Ann Duffy who was later Poet Laureate. AO3: Duffy is critiquing Western media culture and the moral indifference of comfortable audiences who consume images of suffering as entertainment. The photographer is a moral witness — he feels the weight of what he has seen. The reader is implicated: we are 'the reader' who glances at the photos and moves on.

War Photographer — AO3: Duffy critiques {{c1::Western media culture}} and its {{c2::commodification of suffering}}. The reader is {{c3::implicated}} — 'between bath and pre-lunch beers' we consume war as entertainment. The photographer is a {{c4::moral witness}} whose trauma the comfortable audience refuses to acknowledge.
What is the context of Poppies and how does it link to AO3?

Written by Jane Weir around Armistice Sunday, exploring maternal grief. Weir did not have a son who went to war — the poem is imaginative. AO3: By writing from an imagined mother's perspective, Weir universalises grief — this could be any mother, any war, any generation. The poem challenges the public, collective nature of Armistice Day by insisting on the private, individual cost of conflict. The poppy, a symbol of collective remembrance, is reclaimed as a symbol of personal loss.

Poppies — AO3: Weir did {{c1::not}} have a son at war — she writes imaginatively to {{c2::universalise}} maternal grief. The poem challenges {{c3::public collective remembrance}} (Armistice Day) by insisting on {{c4::private individual cost}}. The poppy — symbol of collective memory — is reclaimed as a symbol of {{c5::personal loss}}.
What is the context of Kamikaze and how does it link to AO3?

Written in the contemporary era exploring WWII Japanese culture. Kamikaze pilots were considered heroes — to return was dishonour. AO3: Garland uses the pilot's choice to survive to challenge the idea that dying for one's country is always noble. The poem argues that the social punishment for choosing life (ostracism, being erased by your own family) can be worse than death. It critiques any culture — past or present — that values ideology over individual human life.

Kamikaze — AO3: Garland challenges the idea that dying for one's country is {{c1::always noble}}. The pilot chooses {{c2::life}} but is punished with {{c3::social death — ostracism}} by his own family. The poem critiques any culture that values {{c4::ideology}} over {{c5::individual human life}}.
What is the context of Checking Out Me History and how does it link to AO3?

Written by John Agard, who grew up in the Caribbean before moving to Britain. Written in the post-colonial era. AO3: Agard is directly criticising the British education system which taught Eurocentric history while erasing Black history. The use of Creole dialect is itself a political act — reclaiming a language associated with slavery and colonialism as a language of power and pride. The poem is not just about history — it is about who controls the narrative of history.

Checking Out Me History — AO3: Agard grew up in the {{c1::Caribbean}} and experienced a {{c2::Eurocentric}} British education. The use of {{c3::Creole dialect}} is a political act — reclaiming a language of {{c4::colonialism}} as one of {{c5::power and pride}}. The poem asks: who controls the {{c6::narrative of history}}?
What is the context of Storm on the Island and how does it link to AO3?

Written by Seamus Heaney, a Northern Irish poet who lived through The Troubles — decades of violent political conflict in Northern Ireland. AO3: The storm may be read as a metaphor for political violence. The community prepares for what they know is coming, just as Northern Irish communities lived under constant threat. 'It is a huge nothing that we fear' captures the terror of invisible, unpredictable violence — you cannot defend against what you cannot see coming.

Storm on the Island — AO3: Heaney lived through {{c1::The Troubles}} in Northern Ireland. The storm may be a metaphor for {{c2::political violence}} — invisible, unpredictable, impossible to fully {{c3::defend against}}. 'A huge nothing that we fear' captures the terror of {{c4::living under constant threat}}.
What must a poetry comparison overview always include?

1. Name BOTH poets and BOTH poems immediately. 2. Situate in era (not year) — e.g. 'both writing after the fall of the Bastille'. 3. Link both poets' shared stance/purpose. 4. Signal the contrast between the poems in one sentence. NO quotes in the overview — argument and context only.
Ozymandias key quote cloze — what are the three main quotations from the opening, middle and end?

Opening: 'Two vast and trunkless legs of stone' (image of collapse). Middle: 'My name is Ozymandias, King of Kings' (arrogance). Closing: 'The lone and level sands stretch far away' (nature's final victory).

Ozymandias key quote: '{{c1::My name is Ozymandias, King of Kings}}: / Look on my works, ye Mighty, and despair!' — the imperative '{{c2::Look}}' shows his commanding nature, but is immediately undercut by '{{c3::Nothing beside remains}}'.

Ozymandias structure: it is a {{c1::sonnet}}, typically used for love poems — Shelley uses it to mock Ozymandias's {{c2::self-love}}. The rhyme scheme breaks down as the poem progresses, mirroring the {{c3::crumbling}} of the statue and his power.

Ozymandias final line: '{{c1::The lone and level sands stretch far away}}' — the {{c2::sibilance}} creates a desolate, whispering atmosphere. Nature's power is limitless; the statue is {{c3::swallowed}} by the desert.

Ozymandias: '{{c1::sneer of cold command}}' — the {{c2::alliteration}} of 'cold command' creates a {{c3::condemnatory tone}}, echoing the contemptuous way Ozymandias spoke to his subjects. The adjective '{{c4::cold}}' suggests emotional detachment — he ruled through {{c5::intimidation}} rather than care.

Ozymandias: the '{{c1::pedestal}}' suggests he physically raises himself above his people {{c2::and nature}}. The sonnet form, usually reserved for {{c3::love poems}}, mocks Ozymandias's {{c4::self-absorption and love for power}}. The {{c5::irregular}} rhyme scheme mirrors his {{c6::damaged rule and damaged statue}}.

Ozymandias: '{{c1::lone and level sands stretch far away}}' — '{{c2::lone}}' shows that even one small thing (sand) can {{c3::topple great power}}; '{{c4::level}}' connotes nature as the great {{c5::leveller}} — it does not matter if you were powerful or powerless, {{c6::nature outlasts all}}.

Ozymandias: '{{c1::Nothing beside remains}}' — the {{c2::sparse, short sentence}} imitates the now {{c3::empty landscape}}. Placed directly after Ozymandias's boastful challenge to 'ye Mighty', the {{c4::irony}} is devastating — his kingdom no longer exists.

Ozymandias — teacher's 3 key quotes: '{{c1::Two vast and trunkless legs of stone}}' (opening image of collapse). '{{c2::My name is Ozymandias, King of Kings}}' (arrogance). '{{c3::The lone and level sands stretch far away}}' (nature's victory, closing image).

London key quote: '{{c1::mind-forg'd manacles I hear}}' — the oxymoron of a mental/physical restraint shows people are {{c2::mentally imprisoned}}. The alternate rhyme scheme mirrors the {{c3::strict control}} of government over citizens.

London's acrostic: the third stanza spells '{{c1::HEAR}}' — Blake breaks from the strict structure to signal that readers should listen to the {{c2::cries of suffering}} and consider {{c3::revolution}}.
