London final image: '{{c1::blights with plagues the marriage hearse}}' — the oxymoron of marriage (life/hope) and hearse (death) shows society has made even {{c2::love and new life}} corrupt and deadly.

London: '{{c1::charter'd street}}' and '{{c2::charter'd Thames}}' — the quick {{c3::repetition}} of 'charter'd' shows the {{c4::overwhelming dominance}} of the ruling elite. The juxtaposition of 'charter'd' and '{{c5::flow}}' creates tension between {{c6::restriction and freedom}} — the Thames, a natural feature, is being regulated.

London structure: Blake's line containing '{{c1::flow}}' is the {{c2::longest}} in the poem — the line length {{c3::mimics the freedom and power of nature}}, shirking off its status of being 'charter'd'.

London: the {{c1::alternate rhyme}} scheme creates a sense of {{c2::strict control}}, mimicking the unchanging rules and suffering in London. In the {{c3::third stanza}}, Blake creates an {{c4::acrostic}} spelling '{{c5::HEAR}}' — a severance from the norm, showing Blake's refusal to conform and his call for {{c6::revolution}}.

London: '{{c1::Palace walls}}' vs '{{c2::youthful Harlot}}' — the juxtaposition shows the huge {{c3::division of society}}. The Romantic movement held the {{c4::child}} as a figure of purity outside societal control — making the 'youthful Harlot' even more shocking. Her '{{c5::new born infant}}' shows even the {{c6::most vulnerable}} are suffering.

London: Blake situates himself as a {{c1::flâneur}} — an enlightened observer who witnesses common suffering from the city streets. His lone but {{c2::morally informed}} stance gives the poem authority as {{c3::eyewitness testimony}}.

London — teacher's 3 key quotes: '{{c1::In every cry of every man}}' (universal suffering, anaphora). '{{c2::mind-forg'd manacles I hear}}' (mental imprisonment). '{{c3::blights with plagues the marriage hearse}}' (oxymoron — society corrupts even love).

Prelude key quote: '{{c1::a huge peak, black and huge}} / As if with voluntary power instinct, upreared its head' — the repetition of 'huge' emphasises the {{c2::overwhelming}} scale. The personification of the mountain as having '{{c3::voluntary power}}' shows nature as a {{c4::conscious, moral force}}.

Prelude: '{{c1::I went alone into a shepherd's boat}}' — the act of taking what is not his establishes the speaker's {{c2::youthful arrogance}}; nature's response is a direct consequence of this {{c3::transgression}}.

Prelude: '{{c1::the mountain's outline}}' towered up between me and the stars' — the mountain seems to {{c2::grow and pursue}} the boy; Wordsworth uses {{c3::personification}} to show nature as a {{c4::conscious moral force}}.

Prelude: after the encounter, '{{c1::my brain / Worked with a dim and undetermined sense / Of unknown modes of being}}' — the disruption of his {{c2::certainty}} about the world is the poem's key message; nature {{c3::humbles human pride}}.

Prelude: '{{c1::o'er my thoughts / There hung a darkness}}' — the darkness is {{c2::psychological}}, not physical; Wordsworth shows how {{c3::sublime experiences}} in nature leave lasting emotional and moral impressions.

Prelude — teacher's 3 key quotes: '{{c1::it was an act of stealth and troubled pleasure}}' (opening — oxymoron, guilt). '{{c2::a huge peak, black and huge}}' (repetition — overwhelming). '{{c3::There hung a darkness, call it solitude}}' (psychological aftermath).

My Last Duchess key quote: 'I gave commands; then {{c1::all smiles stopped together}}' — the euphemism implies he {{c2::had her killed}}. The parenthetical '{{c3::(since none puts by / The curtain I have drawn for you, but I)}}' shows his obsessive {{c4::control}} over her even in death.

My Last Duchess key quote: '{{c1::My gift of a nine-hundred-years-old name}} / With anybody's gift' — his outrage that she did not value his {{c2::aristocratic name}} above all else reveals his {{c3::possessiveness}} and sense of entitlement.

My Last Duchess: 'She had / A heart — {{c1::how shall I say?}} — too soon made glad, / {{c2::Too easily impressed}}' — the Duke complains his wife found joy in everyone equally. The interruption '{{c3::how shall I say?}}' performs {{c4::false modesty}}: he knows exactly what he means. His real anger is that she did not recognise his {{c5::rank as superior to all other gifts}} — her equal gratitude is, to him, a {{c6::betrayal}}.

My Last Duchess — teacher's 3 key quotes: '{{c1::(since none puts by / The curtain I have drawn for you, but I)}}' (obsessive control even in death). '{{c2::My gift of a nine-hundred-years-old name}}' (possessiveness). '{{c3::I gave commands; then all smiles stopped together}}' (euphemism for murder).

Charge key quote: '{{c1::Into the valley of Death / Rode the six hundred}}' — the capital D in Death personifies it as an inevitable force. '{{c2::Someone had blundered}}' — the passive construction distributes {{c3::blame}} without naming the officer.

Charge key quote: 'Theirs not to reason why, / {{c1::Theirs but to do and die!}}' — the soldiers are shown as {{c2::unthinking instruments}} of war; however Tennyson {{c3::glorifies}} rather than critiques this — they are honoured as '{{c4::Noble six hundred}}'.

Charge of the Light Brigade: '{{c1::Half a league, half a league, / Half a league onward}}' — the {{c2::dactylic rhythm}} mimics the {{c3::galloping horses}}; the repetition creates momentum and urgency that mirrors the charge itself.

Charge key quote: '{{c1::Into the jaws of Death / Into the mouth of Hell}}' — the personification of Death as a predator with '{{c2::jaws}}' shows the soldiers riding knowingly to their deaths; the religious imagery of '{{c3::Hell}}' intensifies the horror.

Charge key quote: '{{c1::Cannon to right of them / Cannon to left of them / Cannon in front of them}}' — the {{c2::anaphora}} of 'Cannon' creates a sense of {{c3::encirclement and inevitability}}; the soldiers have no escape.

Charge key quote: '{{c1::When can their glory fade? / O the wild charge they made!}}' — the {{c2::rhetorical question}} and exclamation shows Tennyson's {{c3::admiration}} despite the futility. Compare with Exposure where Owen refuses to {{c4::glorify}} soldiers' deaths.

Charge: 'Honour the {{c1::Light Brigade}} / Noble {{c2::six hundred}}' — Tennyson honours their {{c3::obedience and courage}} despite the mistake. The poem was written {{c4::days after}} the event based on a newspaper report.
Charge structure: {{c1::six stanzas}} mirror the {{c2::six hundred}} soldiers. The poem uses a {{c3::cavalry charge rhythm}} throughout — Tennyson crafts the form to embody the content.

Charge: the soldiers' '{{c1::sabres}}' and '{{c2::flash}}' in battle have connotations of {{c3::light}}, suggesting these men are {{c4::blessed by God}} — their fighting has an almost {{c5::holy}} quality. The verb '{{c6::sabring}}' is placed in the {{c7::initial position}} of the line to demonstrate their power and supremacy.

Charge of the Light Brigade — teacher's 3 key quotes: '{{c1::Into the valley of Death / Rode the six hundred}}' (inevitable death). '{{c2::Someone had blundered}}' (passive — distributes blame). '{{c3::Honour the Light Brigade, noble six hundred!}}' (glorification despite futility).

Exposure key quote: '{{c1::Our brains ache, in the merciless iced east winds that knive us}}' — the {{c2::personification}} of the wind as an attacker shows soldiers fought {{c3::nature}}, not just the enemy.

Exposure refrain: '{{c1::But nothing happens}}' — repeated throughout. The noun '{{c2::nothing}}' conveys {{c3::emptiness}}; Owen shows war strips soldiers of agency, leaving them 'hollow shells'. Pararhyme (almost-rhyme) throughout creates {{c4::dissonance}}, mirroring soldiers' dissatisfaction.

Exposure uses {{c1::plural pronouns}} ('our', 'we') throughout — showing all soldiers feel the same; the suffering is {{c2::collective}}. Multiple {{c3::ellipses}} in the first stanza show the soldiers' {{c4::boredom}} and the slowness of time.

Exposure: '{{c1::the mad gusts tugging on the wire}}' — 'mad' may indicate the wind's {{c2::hatred}} toward the men OR allude to the {{c3::mental instability}} of soldiers. The 'twitching agonies of men among its brambles' creates a {{c4::grotesque, animal-like}} image of inglorious death.

Exposure: '{{c1::For the love of God seems dying}}' — the soldiers feel {{c2::abandoned by God}}, as 'doors are closed' including those of {{c3::heaven and salvation}}. The noun '{{c4::dying}}' across multiple refrains creates a sense of decay — war {{c5::corrupts and strips masculinity}} rather than asserting it.

Exposure: '{{c1::Worried by silence}}, sentries whisper, {{c2::curious, nervous}}' — the {{c3::oxymoron}} shows soldiers are unsettled by absence of sound, not its presence. The {{c4::asyndetic list}} enacts their anxious alertness. The refrain '{{c5::But nothing happens}}' immediately deflates all tension: the real enemy is not the Germans but {{c6::waiting itself}}.

Exposure — teacher's 3 key quotes: '{{c1::the merciless iced East winds that knive us}}' (nature as enemy). '{{c2::But nothing happens}}' (refrain — futility, emptiness). '{{c3::All their eyes are ice}}' (soldiers becoming part of the cold — dehumanisation).

Bayonet Charge opens {{c1::in media res}}: 'Suddenly he awoke and was running' — the disorientation of being thrown mid-action mirrors the {{c2::chaos}} of battle. {{c3::Enjambment}} throughout creates a sense of panic — the lines don't end, just as war never truly {{c4::ends}}.

Bayonet Charge key quote: '{{c1::King, honour, human dignity, etcetera}}' — the word '{{c2::etcetera}}' reduces patriotic ideals to a throwaway list, showing the soldier has completely {{c3::lost faith}} in why he is fighting.

Bayonet Charge key quote: 'In what {{c1::cold clockwork}} of the stars and nations / Was he the hand pointing that {{c2::second}}?' — the metaphor of a clock reduces the soldier to a tiny, {{c3::insignificant}} cog in the vast machinery of war.

Bayonet Charge: 'the {{c1::raw-seamed hot khaki}}' and '{{c2::lugging a rifle numb as a smashed arm}}' — the uniform, symbol of patriotism, is {{c3::physically harming}} the soldier; the weapon, his only defence, is likened to a {{c4::useless broken limb}}. Hughes shows the soldier is {{c5::ill-equipped and poorly prepared}} for battle.

Bayonet Charge: the soldier '{{c1::stumbling across the field}}' — the verb connotes inability to remain {{c2::steady or upright}}. Hughes uses a {{c3::lengthy line}} to replicate the long distance across no man's land, decreasing the soldier's chance of survival. The repetition of 'stumbling' into the final stanza reinforces the {{c4::time taken}} and difficulty.

Bayonet Charge: the ideology of war is interrupted by '{{c1::a yellow hare that rolled like a flame}}' — the hare represents {{c2::innocent life}} caught in the same violence as the soldier. The simile '{{c3::rolled like a flame}}' connotes both {{c4::desperate speed}} and {{c5::fragility}} (flames extinguish easily). Hughes uses this sudden natural image to momentarily restore the soldier's {{c6::humanity}} before the machinery of war reclaims him.

Bayonet Charge — teacher's 3 key quotes: '{{c1::Suddenly he was awake and running}}' (in media res — disorientation). '{{c2::In what cold clockwork of the stars and nations / Was he the hand pointing that second?}}' (insignificance). '{{c3::King, honour, human dignity, etcetera}}' (ideology reduced to a list).

Remains is from Armitage's {{c1::2008}} collection 'The Not Dead', based on a {{c2::Channel 4}} documentary. It depicts Guardsman {{c3::Tromans}}'s experience in {{c4::Iraq}} — he shot a looter and suffered severe {{c5::PTSD}}. When he sought help from the army he was {{c6::laughed out of the office}}.

Remains key quote: '{{c1::probably armed, possibly not}}' — the uncertainty and parallel structure shows the {{c2::moral ambiguity}} of modern warfare. Soldiers must make impossible decisions in an {{c3::instant}}.

Remains key quote: '{{c1::the drink and drugs won't flush him out}}' — the verb '{{c2::flush}}' connotes cleansing but the looter cannot be washed away. The {{c3::dash}} replicates Tromans's fractured mental state — he cannot complete a thought without {{c4::flashbacks}}.

Remains: 'his {{c1::bloody life in my bloody hands}}' — Armitage alludes to {{c2::Macbeth}}'s blood motif, linking the guilt of soldiers to Shakespeare's exploration of the {{c3::psychological cost}} of taking a life.

Remains: '{{c1::he's here in my mind when I close my eyes}}' — the traumatic {{c2::flashbacks}} mean the looter is 'dug into' his mind. In {{c3::Freudian}} terms, this is the {{c4::uncanny return of the repressed}} — the speaker's attempts to bury the memory are futile.

Remains: after Tromans '{{c1::blinks}}', enjambment creates momentary respite — the blink is a {{c2::pause}} — but 'the looter {{c3::bursts again}}' into his sight. The verb 'burst' is {{c4::intrusive and violent}}; 'again' reinforces the {{c5::relentless repetitiveness}} of his trauma.

Remains: 'he's there on the ground {{c1::sort of inside out}}' — the {{c2::colloquial}} phrase 'sort of' mimics Tromans's verbal {{c3::deflection}} from trauma: he cannot speak directly about what he witnessed. 'Inside out' literalises {{c4::visceral horror}} in casual language, revealing how the soldier uses {{c5::understatement}} to manage unbearable memory. Armitage shows how trauma distorts {{c6::language itself}}.

Remains — teacher's 3 key quotes: '{{c1::probably armed, possibly not}}' (moral ambiguity). '{{c2::tosses his guts back into his body}}' (visceral, grotesque). '{{c3::the drink and drugs won't flush him out}}' (PTSD — cannot escape memory).

War Photographer key quote: '{{c1::spools of suffering set out in ordered rows}}' — the alliterative sibilance and the comparison of film spools to {{c2::gravestones}} shows the weight of death the photographer carries.

War Photographer key quote: 'The reader's eyeballs prick / With tears between {{c1::bath and pre-lunch beers}}' — the {{c2::plosive}} 'b' sounds give a flippant tone; both 'bath' and 'beers' are luxuries suggesting Western audiences treat war as {{c3::entertainment}} consumed between comfortable activities.

War Photographer structure: the rhyme scheme {{c1::falls away}} towards the end, mirroring the photographer's {{c2::declining mental state}}. 'from which his editor will {{c3::pick out five or six}}' — the reduction of '{{c4::a hundred agonies}}' to a handful shows media's {{c5::commodification of suffering}}.

War Photographer: '{{c1::The reader's eyeballs prick / With tears between bath and pre-lunch beers}}' — {{c2::internal rhyme}} emphasises the indifference of newspaper readers. The plosive '{{c3::b}}' sounds create a {{c4::flippant fluency}} underpinned by Duffy's bitterness. The verb '{{c5::prick}}' suggests minor, short-lived pain — the reader forgets immediately.

War Photographer: Duffy uses {{c1::internal rhyme}} deliberately — the comfortable rhyme of 'beers/tears' mimics the comfortable distance the reader maintains from the suffering. The photographer's work has '{{c2::no impact}}' — 'from which his editor will pick out {{c3::five or six}}' — the reduction of a hundred agonies to five or six images shows {{c4::media's commodification of suffering}}.

War Photographer: '{{c1::All flesh is grass}}' — a direct {{c2::biblical allusion}} (Isaiah 40:6) meaning all human life is {{c3::temporary}}. Duffy quotes scripture without attribution, suggesting the photographer has become a kind of {{c4::secular witness to mortality}}. The {{c5::three-word sentence}} — the shortest in the poem — carries the weight of {{c6::absolute judgement}}, structurally isolated from the longer descriptive stanzas around it.

War Photographer — teacher's 3 key quotes: '{{c1::spools of suffering set out in ordered rows}}' (film = graves). '{{c2::of running children in a nightmare heat}}' (visceral — Vietnam reference). '{{c3::from which his editor will pick out five or six}}' (media commodifies suffering).

Poppies key quote: '{{c1::Three days before Armistice Sunday}}' — the specific time grounds the poem in both {{c2::personal}} and {{c3::collective}} remembrance. The poppy is both a symbol of death (war memorial) and life (the poem's hope).

Poppies key quote: 'I resisted the impulse to {{c1::run my fingers / through the gelled blackthorns of your hair}}' — the mother suppresses her physical desire to protect her son; '{{c2::blackthorns}}' introduces pain and danger into the {{c3::domestic, tender}} image.

Poppies: multiple commas at the end of the third stanza show the {{c1::fracturing}} of the relationship between mother and child. The final image: '{{c2::your playground voice catching in the wind}}' — she listens desperately for his voice, showing {{c3::grief}} and {{c4::hope}}.

Poppies: '{{c1::Sellotape}} {{c3::bandaged}} around my hand' — the noun is a modern, {{c2::domestic}} material entirely out of place in a war context. The verb belongs to a {{c4::semantic field of injury}}, revealing the mother already preparing for loss. Weir suggests that maternal preparation for military deployment echoes {{c5::tending wounds}}: war enters the home before the soldier has left.

Poppies: 'I had {{c1::released a songbird from its cage}}' — the extended metaphor of a {{c2::songbird}} implies her son is someone whose talent and voice she has nurtured. Releasing a bird suggests both {{c3::freedom}} and {{c4::danger}} — a caged bird is safe; a freed one is vulnerable. The mother's letting go is framed as an act of love she had {{c5::no choice}} but to perform, creating a sense of {{c6::resigned grief}}.

Poppies — teacher's 3 key quotes: '{{c1::Three days before Armistice Sunday}}' (personal vs collective remembrance). '{{c2::I resisted the impulse to run my fingers}}' (suppressed maternal instinct). '{{c3::your playground voice catching in the wind}}' (desperate hope — grief).

Kamikaze key quote: '{{c1::Her father embarked at sunrise}}' — 'sunrise' connotes hope and Japan's identity as the '{{c2::Land of the Rising Sun}}'. The word '{{c3::embarked}}' also means to begin a journey — double meaning of mission and {{c4::life choice}}.

Kamikaze key quote: 'full of {{c1::powerful incantations}}' — the noun (a magical spell) shows that {{c2::propaganda}} and patriotic conditioning is almost {{c3::supernatural}} in its control over the pilot's mind.

Kamikaze: 'till gradually we too {{c1::learned to be silent}}' — the family's {{c2::ostracism}} is not sudden but gradual, making it more chilling. Final line: 'he must have wondered which had been {{c3::the better way to die}}' — the ambiguous ending questions whether {{c4::social death}} or physical death is worse.

Kamikaze: '{{c1::her father}}' — the possessive creates a sense of {{c2::pride and ownership}} from the family. He '{{c3::embarks}}' at '{{c4::sunrise}}' — connoting hope and glory; 'sunrise' also recalls Japan's exonym '{{c5::Land of the Rising Sun}}', showing his mission is bound to national {{c6::identity}}.

Kamikaze: 'a {{c1::samurai sword in the cockpit}}' is {{c2::redundant}} on a suicide mission — it serves a purely {{c3::symbolic}} role, a visual reminder of his duty and of his {{c4::Japanese martial heritage}} (indifference to pain, unflinching loyalty).

Kamikaze: '{{c1::shaven head}}' — pilots underwent {{c2::ceremonial head shaving}}, marking them in public as figures worthy of {{c3::respect and prayers}}. This cult of celebrity ensured {{c4::societal pressure}} was inescapable.

Kamikaze: 'full of {{c1::powerful incantations}}' — the quantifier '{{c2::full}}' reveals the pilot has been trained into {{c3::blind obedience}} — nothing else occupies his mind. '{{c4::Incantations}}' (a magic spell/charm) reveals that propaganda functions like {{c5::supernatural control}}, conditioning people as though they have been {{c6::charmed}}.

Kamikaze — teacher's 3 key quotes: '{{c1::one way flight into history}}' (certain death, cultural weight). '{{c2::little boats strung out like bunting}}' (beauty of life — what he turns back for). '{{c3::he must have wondered which had been the better way to die}}' (ambiguous — social death vs physical death).

COMH key quote: '{{c1::Bandage up me eye with me own history}}' — the metaphor of {{c2::blindfolding}} shows his education deliberately hid his own cultural identity; 'me own history' emphasises what has been {{c3::stolen}} from him.

COMH key quote: '{{c1::I carving out me identity}}' — the present continuous tense shows this is an {{c2::ongoing}} process. 'Carving' suggests it requires {{c3::effort and force}} — identity is not freely given but must be actively claimed.

COMH contrasts: heroes taught in school — {{c1::Dick Whittington, Florence Nightingale, 1066}} — with erased figures: {{c2::Toussaint L'Ouverture}} (led Haitian Revolution), {{c3::Mary Seacole}} (Crimean War nurse), {{c4::Nanny de Maroon}} (Jamaican freedom fighter).

COMH: '{{c1::Dem tell me / Wha dem want to tell me}}' — the {{c2::Creole dialect}} immediately establishes Agard's cultural identity and resistance; the repetition of 'dem' (them) creates an {{c3::us vs them}} dynamic between coloniser and colonised.

COMH: '{{c1::Dick Whittington and his cat}}' contrasted with '{{c2::Toussaint / a slave / with vision}}' — the {{c3::juxtaposition}} of trivial English folklore against a significant Black revolutionary shows how {{c4::colonial education}} trivialised non-European history.

COMH: '{{c1::Nanny de maroon / see-far woman / of mountain dream}}' — the {{c2::fragmented lines}} and lack of punctuation mirror the {{c3::broken, suppressed}} nature of Black history. The praise-style language elevates her as a {{c4::hero}}.

COMH: '{{c1::Toussaint / he walked like he owned / the whole universe}}' — the confident stride contrasts with the passive victims colonial education presents; Agard reclaims {{c2::Black dignity and power}}.

COMH structure: the poem alternates between {{c1::standard English}} (colonial education — mocking) and {{c2::Creole}} (Agard's own reclaimed history — passionate). The structural contrast {{c3::performs}} the poem's argument.

Checking Out Me History — teacher's 3 key quotes: '{{c1::bandage up my identity}}' (colonial education blindfolds him). '{{c2::Toussaint / a slave / with vision}}' (reclaiming Black heroism). '{{c3::I carving out my identity}}' (active, ongoing — present continuous).

The Emigree key quote: '{{c1::but my memory of it is sunlight clear}}' — the word '{{c2::sunlight}}' appears at the end of every stanza, showing positive memories persist {{c3::regardless}} of political reality. The speaker's homeland has been '{{c4::branded}}' on her mind.

The Emigree key quote: '{{c1::They accuse me of absence}}' and 'my {{c2::shadow falls as evidence of sunlight}}' — though the speaker is accused of not belonging, her very existence {{c3::proves the light}} of her homeland memory. She cannot be erased.

The Emigree: '{{c1::There once was a country}}' — the fairy-tale opening suggests the homeland is now almost {{c2::mythological}}; it exists in memory and imagination rather than {{c3::physical reality}}.

The Emigree: '{{c1::It may be sick with tyrants}}' — the speaker acknowledges the homeland's problems but the '{{c2::may be}}' shows {{c3::uncertainty}}; she cannot know what it is like now as she {{c4::cannot return}}.

The Emigree: '{{c1::I am branded by an impression of sunlight}}' — the word '{{c2::branded}}' (like livestock) suggests the homeland has been {{c3::permanently marked into}} her identity; she cannot escape it even if she wanted to.

The Emigree: '{{c1::They no longer know their own reflection}}' — the speaker's oppressors have lost {{c2::self-knowledge}}; they cannot recognise what they have become. Connects to Jekyll & Hyde — Hyde also distorts {{c3::reflection of the self}}.

The Emigree: 'That {{c1::child's vocabulary I carried here}} / like a {{c2::hollow doll}}' — the simile '{{c3::hollow doll}}' suggests the speaker's childhood language is a vessel with nothing inside: the culture it described is {{c4::gone}}. Yet she still {{c5::carries}} it — language is both burden and possession. Rumens explores how language preserves {{c6::identity}} even when the homeland is lost or destroyed.

The Emigree — teacher's 3 key quotes: '{{c1::but my memory of it is sunlight clear}}' (positive memory persists). '{{c2::be a lie, banned by the state}}' (truth suppressed by political power). '{{c3::my shadow falls as evidence of sunlight}}' (existence proves homeland memory).

Tissue key quote: '{{c1::Paper that lets the light shine through}} / this is what could alter things' — paper (fragile, transparent, human) is more powerful than {{c2::brick}} or stone. Light symbolises {{c3::truth}} breaking through rigid, man-made structures.

Tissue key quote: '{{c1::Maps too. The sun shines through / their borderlines}}' — national borders are {{c2::artificial}} constructs; the sun (nature) ignores them. The enjambment throughout mirrors the {{c3::flowing, delicate}} nature of tissue and human life.

Tissue: '{{c1::If buildings were paper}}' — the conditional opens the poem with a {{c2::hypothetical}} that Dharker uses to reimagine rigid human structures as {{c3::fragile and temporary}} as tissue paper.

Tissue: '{{c1::an architect could use / a paper, making / a structure as if alive}}' — the {{c2::enjambment}} across stanzas mirrors the {{c3::flowing, uncontained}} nature of paper itself; form embodies meaning.

Tissue: '{{c1::the sun shines through / their borderlines, the marks / that rivers make}}' — nature (sun, rivers) does not recognise human {{c2::borders}}; Dharker suggests natural forces are more powerful than {{c3::political constructs}}.

Tissue: '{{c1::turned into your skin}}' — the final image connects paper to {{c2::human tissue}}; life itself is fragile, translucent, mortal. The poem ends on the human body as the {{c3::most delicate paper of all}}.

Tissue — teacher's 3 key quotes: '{{c1::Paper that lets the light shine through}}' (fragility = power). '{{c2::Maps too, the sun shines through their borderlines}}' (nature ignores human borders). '{{c3::Turned into your skin}}' (paper = human life — equally fragile).

Storm on the Island key quote: '{{c1::We build our houses squat, we are prepared}}' — the confident opening is ironic; by the end '{{c2::it is a huge nothing we fear}}' — the paradox of 'huge nothing' shows the terror of {{c3::invisible, uncontrollable forces}}.

Storm on the Island: '{{c1::We are prepared: we build our houses squat}}' — the confident first person plural '{{c2::we}}' creates community solidarity; 'prepared' is immediately {{c3::ironic}} as the storm overwhelms all preparation.

Storm: '{{c1::Strange, it is a huge nothing that we fear}}' — the paradox of 'huge nothing' captures the terror of {{c2::invisible, intangible forces}}. May refer to '{{c3::The Troubles}}' — political violence is similarly invisible until it strikes.

Storm: '{{c1::the wizened earth had never troubled us / with hay, so, as you see, there are no stacks / or stooks that can be lost}}' — the island has {{c2::nothing to lose}} materially, yet this does not protect it; the storm attacks {{c3::psychologically}}.

Storm: '{{c1::Spits like a tame cat / Turned savage}}' — the simile of a {{c2::domesticated animal turning wild}} suggests nature (or political forces) can never truly be {{c3::tamed}}; safety is always {{c4::illusory}}.

Storm on the Island — teacher's 3 key quotes: '{{c1::We build our houses squat, we are prepared}}' (confident opening — ironic). '{{c2::spits like a tame cat / Turned savage}}' (nature domesticated then wild). '{{c3::it is a huge nothing we fear}}' (paradox — invisible threat).
What are the rules for a high-grade poetry comparison essay?

1. Link poems by the poets' stance/time written. 2. Comparison is COMBINED — comparative terms throughout. 3. 1:1 evidence ratio — one piece of evidence from each poem, alternating. 4. Thread of argument maintained throughout. 5. Side-by-side language analysis (e.g. compare a verb from each poem). 6. Side-by-side structural analysis (e.g. compare enjambment from each poem).
Give an example of a strong poetry comparison overview (Ozymandias/London).

'As Romantics, both Shelley (Ozymandias) and Blake (London) condemn corrupt ruling forces. Blake, writing after the fall of the Bastille, seeks to inspire an English revolution, giving more power to the common man. In contrast, Shelley exposes how power is all-consuming in the wrong hands, yet offers comfort as nature ultimately triumphs over man.' — Era + shared purpose + contrast in three sentences. No quotes.
Best comparison poem for Ozymandias?
London (both Romantic poets critiquing power/rulers, nature vs man). Extract from the Prelude (power of nature over human pride, both Romantic).
Best comparison for the Prelude?
Ozymandias (both show nature humbling human pride, both Romantic). Exposure (both show nature as an overwhelming, almost hostile force).
Best comparison for My Last Duchess?

Checking Out Me History (both are dramatic monologues; both explore how powerful figures suppress and control identity — the Duke controls the Duchess's story, colonial education suppresses Black identity).
Best comparison for Exposure?

Remains (both show psychological effects of war; mental suffering). Bayonet Charge (both show the reality vs propaganda of war, written from different experiences — Owen first-hand, Hughes via his father).
Best comparison for Remains?
Exposure (both show psychological effects on soldiers, both show war is never truly over). War Photographer (both explore trauma and the indifference of those at home to soldiers' suffering).
Best comparison for Kamikaze?
Remains (both show guilt and the psychological cost of conflict). Poppies (both explore family conflict — a parent/child relationship torn apart by war duty).
Which poems compare best for 'effects of war on soldiers'?
Physical effects: Bayonet Charge + Charge of the Light Brigade. Mental/psychological effects: Remains + Exposure or War Photographer. Both physical and mental: Bayonet Charge + Exposure.
Which poems compare best for 'identity'?

Kamikaze + Checking Out Me History (both explore identity suppressed by external forces — society/colonial education). The Emigree + Checking Out Me History (both explore displaced or erased cultural identity).
Which poems compare best for 'power of nature'?
Ozymandias + Extract from the Prelude (both Romantic, both show nature humbling human pride). Storm on the Island + Exposure (both show nature as an overwhelming, threatening force).

Charge vs Exposure — agency in death: in Tennyson, soldiers are {{c1::active}} even in death ('sabring the gunners'). In Owen, soldiers are {{c2::static}} — the '{{c3::wind}}' is the active agent and the soldiers can only '{{c4::hear}}' it, communicating the {{c5::futility and demoralisation}} of trench warfare.
How does the Bayonet Charge/Exposure comparison work structurally?

Hughes: soldier is active — 'suddenly awaking and running', 'stumbling across the field', 'lugging a rifle'. Owen: soldiers are static — the 'wind' is the active agent, soldiers can only 'hear' it. Hughes shows war's physical difficulty; Owen shows its psychological futility. Hughes depicts 'going over the top' (attack); Owen depicts waiting in trenches (stasis). Both show war destroys the soldier — in different ways.
What is the universal message that unites Remains and Exposure according to your teacher's model?

Both poems present one truth: 'conflict is not a romanticised masculine exploit, but rather something that subverts masculinity, human dignity and even nature itself — the suffering it causes will always be dug deep into the psyche of those humans unlucky enough to experience it.' This is a powerful concluding argument that links both poems thematically.
What is the thematic conclusion linking War Photographer and Remains?

Both Duffy and Armitage align moral conscience with purity and cleanliness. Tromans cannot be clean — 'the drink and drugs won't flush him out' — while readers 'bathe' between looking at war photographs. Both writers show that those who witness war are 'stained' by it while those at home maintain an illusion of purity. The conclusion: more must be done to confront our roles in warfare's violence and to support those who bear its psychological cost.
What are the timings for Paper 2 Sections B and C (Poetry)?

Section B — Poetry Anthology: 30 marks. Extended comparative essay (one named poem + one other you choose). Suggested timing: 45 minutes. Section C — Unseen Poetry: two questions. Part 1 (12 marks): 30 minutes total (5 min reading + 25 min writing). Part 2 (8 marks): 15 minutes total (5 min reading + 10 min writing).

Paper 2 Section B (Poetry Anthology): {{c1::30}} marks. Spend {{c2::45}} minutes. Compare {{c3::one named poem}} with {{c4::one other you choose}}. Section C Unseen Part 1: {{c5::12}} marks — spend {{c6::30}} minutes ({{c7::5}} reading + {{c8::25}} writing). Section C Unseen Part 2: {{c9::8}} marks — spend {{c10::15}} minutes ({{c11::5}} reading + {{c12::10}} writing).
How does the mark-a-minute rule apply to Paper 2 poetry sections?

A mark a minute is typically a good guide. Section B (30 marks): aim for 30 minutes writing + ~10 minutes annotation and planning = ~40 minutes, within the 45 minute window. Section C Unseen Part 1 (12 marks): 25 minutes writing is slightly above a mark a minute — you need reading time. Section C Unseen Part 2 (8 marks): 10 minutes writing is tight — be concise and comparative from the first sentence.
Photosynthesis equation: {{c1::6CO₂}} + {{c2::6H₂O}} → {{c3::C₆H₁₂O₆}} + {{c4::6O₂}}. It is an {{c5::endothermic}} reaction (absorbs energy from light).

{{c1::Metabolism}} is the sum of all {{c2::chemical reactions}} in a cell or the body. It includes {{c3::respiration}} (breaking down glucose to transfer energy), {{c4::protein synthesis}} (from amino acids), and {{c5::photosynthesis}} (plants building glucose). The energy transferred by respiration is used to {{c6::build larger molecules}}, {{c7::contract muscles}}, {{c8::maintain body temperature}} and transmit nerve impulses.
What are the factors that limit the rate of photosynthesis?

Light intensity, CO₂ concentration, temperature, and chlorophyll concentration. Increasing any one of these increases the rate until another factor becomes limiting. At high temperatures the enzymes controlling photosynthesis denature.
How do plants use glucose?
Respiration (energy for growth). Made into starch for storage. Made into cellulose for cell walls. Made into lipids (oils/fats) for storage. Combined with nitrates to make amino acids and proteins.
Aerobic respiration equation: {{c1::C₆H₁₂O₆}} + {{c2::6O₂}} → {{c3::6CO₂}} + {{c4::6H₂O}} (+ energy). Occurs in the {{c5::mitochondria}}. Releases {{c6::more}} energy than anaerobic respiration.

Anaerobic respiration in animals: {{c1::glucose}} → {{c2::lactic acid}} (+ small amount of energy). Causes {{c3::oxygen debt}} — lactic acid must be oxidised after exercise. In plants/yeast: glucose → {{c4::ethanol}} + {{c5::CO₂}} (fermentation).
What happens to the body during exercise?

Heart rate, breathing rate and breath volume increase to deliver more O₂ and glucose to muscles and remove CO₂. Muscles respire aerobically then anaerobically when O₂ can't be delivered fast enough. Lactic acid builds up causing muscle fatigue. After exercise, oxygen is used to break down the lactic acid — this is why breathing rate stays elevated after stopping.

Rate of photosynthesis = {{c1::volume of oxygen produced}} ÷ {{c2::time}}. Units: {{c3::cm³/s}} or {{c4::cm³/min}}. A higher rate means {{c5::more oxygen produced in the same time}} (or the same volume produced faster).
How do you investigate the effect of light intensity on the rate of photosynthesis (pondweed / Elodea)?

Method: place pondweed in sodium hydrogen carbonate solution (provides CO₂). Shine a lamp at different distances. Count bubbles per minute (or measure volume of O₂ collected using a gas syringe) at each distance.
Light intensity ∝ 1/distance² (inverse square law) — double the distance → quarter the light intensity.
Control variables: temperature (use water bath), CO₂ concentration (sodium hydrogen carbonate keeps constant), same piece of pondweed.
Expected result: as light intensity increases, rate increases (up to the point where CO₂ or temperature becomes limiting).
Draw and interpret limiting factor graphs for photosynthesis.

Light intensity graph: rate increases then levels off — at plateau, light is no longer limiting (CO₂ or temperature now limits).
CO₂ concentration graph: increases then plateaus — at high CO₂, another factor limits.
Temperature graph: increases up to ~40°C then sharply decreases — enzymes denature above optimum (not a simple plateau).
Key exam point: to determine what is limiting at any point on the graph — the factor that makes the plateau rise when increased is the one that was limiting. If light increases but rate doesn't — something else is limiting.

The word equation for aerobic respiration: {{c1::glucose}} + {{c2::oxygen}} → {{c3::carbon dioxide}} + {{c4::water}} (+ energy). For anaerobic in mammals: glucose → {{c5::lactic acid}} (+ small amount of energy). In yeast: glucose → {{c6::ethanol}} + {{c7::carbon dioxide}}. Anaerobic produces far {{c8::less}} ATP than aerobic.
What is the difference between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells?

Eukaryotic cells (animal/plant) have a true membrane-bound nucleus. Prokaryotic cells (bacteria) have no nucleus — DNA is a single circular strand in the cytoplasm. Prokaryotes have no mitochondria and are much smaller (1–10μm vs 10–100μm).

Animal cell organelles: {{c1::nucleus}} (contains DNA), {{c2::cell membrane}} (controls entry/exit), {{c3::cytoplasm}} (site of reactions), {{c4::mitochondria}} (aerobic respiration), {{c5::ribosomes}} (protein synthesis).
Plant cells additionally contain: {{c1::cell wall}} (cellulose — support), {{c2::chloroplasts}} (photosynthesis), {{c3::permanent vacuole}} (cell sap, maintains turgor pressure).
Bacterial cells contain: {{c1::cell wall}}, {{c2::cell membrane}}, {{c3::cytoplasm}}, {{c4::ribosomes}}, {{c5::circular DNA strand}}, and sometimes a {{c6::plasmid}} and {{c7::flagellum}}.
Label the animal cell — name each numbered part

① Cell membranecontrols what enters and exits the cell
② Nucleuscontains DNA — controls cell activity
③ Mitochondriasite of aerobic respiration — produces ATP
④ Ribosomessite of protein synthesis
⑤ Cytoplasmaqueous solution — site of metabolic reactions
Label the plant cell — name each numbered part

① Cell wallcellulose — rigid support, prevents bursting
② Cell membranecontrols entry/exit of substances
③ Nucleuscontains DNA — controls cell activity
④ Permanent vacuolecontains cell sap — maintains turgor pressure
⑤ Chloroplastcontains chlorophyll — site of photosynthesis
⑥ Cytoplasmsite of metabolic reactions

What is the magnification formula? Use the triangle.

I = Image size  |  M = Magnification  |  A = Actual size

Find M:M = I ÷ A
Find I:I = M × A
Find A:A = I ÷ M

Worked example: image = 20 mm, actual = 0.02 mm → M = 20 ÷ 0.02 = ×1000
Unit conversions: 1 mm = 1000 μm  |  1 μm = 1000 nm
Standard form: 0.02 mm = 2 × 10⁻² mm
Magnification = {{c1::image size}} ÷ {{c2::actual size}}. 1mm = {{c3::1000}}μm. An electron microscope has higher {{c4::magnification}} AND {{c5::resolution}} than a light microscope.
What is mitosis used for?

Mitosis produces two genetically identical daughter cells with the same number of chromosomes as the parent. Used for growth, repair and asexual reproduction. DNA replicates before division so both cells receive a complete copy.
What are stem cells and why are they medically important?

Undifferentiated cells that can divide and specialise into many cell types. Embryonic stem cells can become any cell type. Adult stem cells (e.g. bone marrow) are more limited. Used to treat leukaemia (bone marrow transplant) and potentially Parkinson's and diabetes. Therapeutic cloning prevents rejection.
What is the difference between diffusion, osmosis and active transport?

Diffusion: passive movement of particles from high to low concentration. Osmosis: passive movement of water molecules across a partially permeable membrane from high to low water potential. Active transport: movement of substances against a concentration gradient using energy (ATP) from respiration — e.g. uptake of mineral ions by root hair cells.

Factors affecting rate of diffusion: {{c1::concentration gradient}} (steeper = faster), {{c2::surface area}} (larger = faster), {{c3::temperature}} (higher = faster), {{c4::distance}} (shorter = faster).
What is the required practical for osmosis? What results would you expect?

Method: cut potato cylinders of equal length/mass. Place in different concentrations of sucrose solution (e.g. 0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0 mol/dm³). Leave 20–30 minutes. Remove, dry, re-weigh (or re-measure length).
Results: in distilled water (hypotonic) → cylinder gains mass (water enters by osmosis). In concentrated solution (hypertonic) → cylinder loses mass (water leaves). At one concentration the mass doesn't change — this equals the concentration of cell sap (isotonic point).
Graph: plot % change in mass vs concentration → straight line crossing x-axis.
What is the surface area to volume ratio (SA:V) and why is it important for organisms?

As an organism gets larger, its volume increases faster than its surface area — the SA:V ratio decreases.
Importance: small organisms (bacteria, single cells) have large SA:V → enough diffusion surface to supply all cells. Large organisms need specialised exchange surfaces (lungs, villi, gills) to increase SA for gas/nutrient exchange.
Exam example: a 1cm cube: SA = 6cm², V = 1cm³, SA:V = 6:1. A 2cm cube: SA = 24cm², V = 8cm³, SA:V = 3:1. As size doubles, SA:V halves.
Describe the adaptations of specialised cells: red blood cell, sperm cell, nerve cell, root hair cell, and muscle cell.

Red blood cell: biconcave disc → large SA for O₂ absorption. No nucleus → more space for haemoglobin. Flexible → fits through narrow capillaries.
Sperm cell: long tail (flagellum) for motility. Many mitochondria for energy. Acrosome contains enzymes to penetrate egg.
Nerve (neurone): very long → transmits impulses far distances. Myelin sheath → insulates, speeds transmission.
Root hair cell: long projection into soil → increased SA for water/mineral absorption. Large permanent vacuole → maintains osmotic gradient.
Muscle cell: contains many mitochondria to supply ATP for contraction. Contains proteins that allow the cell to shorten and generate force for movement.

Mitosis produces {{c1::2}} genetically {{c2::identical}} daughter cells. Stage 1: DNA {{c3::replicates}} (chromosomes duplicate). Stage 2: chromosomes are pulled to {{c4::opposite ends}} of the cell / {{c5::two nuclei}} form. Stage 3: {{c6::cytoplasm divides}} → two identical cells formed. Used for {{c7::growth}}, {{c8::repair}} and {{c9::asexual reproduction}}.
Label the stages of mitosis — what is happening at each numbered stage?

① InterphaseCell grows; DNA replicates — chromosomes are copied ready for division
② Replicated chromosomesEach chromosome now consists of two identical sister chromatids joined together (X-shape)
③ MetaphaseChromosomes line up at the equator (cell centre), attached to spindle fibres
④ AnaphaseSister chromatids pulled to opposite poles; cell elongates
⑤ Two daughter cellsCytoplasm divides (cytokinesis) → 2 genetically identical diploid cells. Used for: growth, repair, asexual reproduction.
What is the required practical for microscopy? How do you prepare a slide and use a light microscope?

Slide preparation: place a thin section of material (e.g. onion epidermis or cheek cells) on a glass slide. Add a drop of stain — iodine (shows cell walls/nucleus in plant cells) or methylene blue (shows nuclei in animal cells). Lower a coverslip gently at 45° to avoid air bubbles.
Using the microscope: start on the lowest magnification objective. Use the coarse focus knob to bring the specimen into view, then fine focus for clarity. Increase the objective lens for greater magnification.
Total magnification = eyepiece lens × objective lens (e.g. ×10 eyepiece × ×40 objective = ×400).
Drawing cells: use single clear pencil lines, no shading, label with ruled lines, state the magnification.
Light vs electron microscope: light — can view living specimens, max ~×2000 useful magnification, resolution ~200 nm. Electron — higher resolution (~0.2 nm), max ~×2,000,000, but specimens must be dead/preserved (vacuum required).

Stem cells are {{c1::undifferentiated}} cells that can divide and develop into {{c2::specialised}} cell types. {{c3::Embryonic}} stem cells can become any cell type. {{c4::Adult}} stem cells (e.g. in bone marrow) are more limited. Medical uses: treat {{c5::leukaemia}} via bone marrow transplant; potentially {{c6::Parkinson's disease}} and {{c7::diabetes}}. {{c8::Therapeutic cloning}} produces an embryo with the patient's own DNA — reduces rejection risk.
What are the four types of pathogen?

Bacteria (e.g. Salmonella, gonorrhoea), Viruses (e.g. measles, HIV, tobacco mosaic virus), Fungi (e.g. rose black spot), Protists (e.g. malaria — spread by mosquito vector). Bacteria produce toxins that damage tissues. Viruses replicate inside host cells, bursting them.
Measles: caused by a {{c1::virus}}, spread by {{c2::droplets}}. Symptoms: {{c3::fever and rash}}. Can be fatal if complications occur. Prevented by {{c4::MMR vaccine}}.

HIV: caused by a {{c1::virus}}, spread by {{c2::sexual contact or exchange of bodily fluids}}. Attacks {{c3::white blood cells}}, leading to {{c4::AIDS}} when the immune system can no longer fight infections. Controlled with {{c5::antiretroviral drugs}} — there is no cure.

Malaria: caused by a {{c1::protist}}, spread by {{c2::mosquitoes}} (vectors). Symptoms: {{c3::fever, shaking, recurring illness}}. Spread is controlled by preventing mosquitoes from {{c4::breeding}} and using {{c5::mosquito nets}}.

Salmonella: caused by {{c1::bacteria}}, spread through {{c2::contaminated food}}. Bacteria produce {{c3::toxins}} causing {{c4::fever, vomiting and diarrhoea}}. Controlled by {{c5::vaccinating poultry}}.
What are the non-specific defences of the human body?

Skin: physical barrier, produces antimicrobial secretions. Nose: hairs and mucus trap pathogens. Trachea and bronchi: ciliated cells and mucus trap and sweep out pathogens. Stomach: hydrochloric acid (pH 2) kills pathogens in food/drink.
How does the immune system respond to pathogens?

White blood cells: 1) Phagocytosis — phagocytes engulf and digest pathogens. 2) Antibody production — lymphocytes produce specific antibodies that match antigens on the pathogen, causing clumping and destruction. 3) Antitoxin production — neutralises toxins produced by bacteria. Memory cells remain for faster response on re-infection.
How do vaccines work?

A vaccine contains dead/weakened/inactive pathogen (or antigens). White blood cells produce antibodies and memory cells without you becoming ill. On future exposure, memory cells rapidly produce antibodies to destroy the pathogen before you get sick. Herd immunity: enough people vaccinated so the pathogen can't spread.
What is the difference between antibiotics and antivirals?

Antibiotics kill or inhibit the growth of bacteria but DO NOT work on viruses (different structure). Antivirals (e.g. antiretroviral drugs for HIV) slow viral replication but rarely cure. Antibiotic resistance develops when bacteria mutate — MRSA is a key example. Overuse of antibiotics accelerates resistance.
What are the stages of drug development and testing?

Preclinical testing: tested on cells/tissues/live animals for safety and efficacy. Clinical trials: Phase 1 — small group of healthy volunteers (safety/dosage). Phase 2 — small group of patients (effectiveness). Phase 3 — large randomised double-blind placebo-controlled trial. Peer reviewed before approval.
What plant diseases are included in the AQA spec? How are they identified and treated?

Tobacco mosaic virus (TMV): virus. Spread by contact. Causes discoloured mosaic pattern on leaves → reduces photosynthesis → stunted growth. No cure — use resistant varieties.
Rose black spot: fungal disease. Black/purple spots on leaves → leaves turn yellow and drop. Spread by water/wind. Treated with fungicides or removing infected leaves.
Identification of disease generally: observe symptoms, use lab tests (testing for pathogen's DNA/proteins), send samples for analysis.
Plant defences: waxy cuticle (physical barrier), cell wall (physical barrier), chemical defences (antibacterial chemicals, poisons to deter predators).

Gonorrhoea: caused by {{c1::bacteria}}, spread by {{c2::unprotected sexual contact}}. Symptoms: {{c3::thick yellow or green discharge}} from penis or vagina, {{c4::pain when urinating}}. Treated with {{c5::antibiotics}}, but {{c6::antibiotic resistance}} is increasing — some strains now untreatable. Prevented by {{c7::condoms}}.
What is the required practical for culturing microorganisms? What is aseptic technique?

Method: sterilise agar (autoclaved). Pour into Petri dish — allow to set with lid on. Use sterile inoculating loop (flamed red-hot) to transfer bacteria onto agar. Seal with tape (not all the way round — allows some O₂ in, prevents contamination from outside). Incubate at 25°C in school (not 37°C — prevents growth of human pathogens).
Measuring antibiotic effectiveness: place antibiotic-soaked paper discs on the inoculated plate. Incubate. Measure the inhibition zone (clear area around disc where bacteria are killed). Larger zone = more effective antibiotic. No zone = bacteria are resistant.
What was the discovery of penicillin and why is antibiotic resistance such a concern?

Discovery: Alexander Fleming (1928) noticed mould (Penicillium) killed bacteria on his petri dish. Florey and Chain later developed it into a usable antibiotic (WWII).
MRSA: methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus — bacteria resistant to most antibiotics. Evolved through natural selection: random mutations give some bacteria resistance → antibiotics kill non-resistant bacteria → resistant bacteria survive and reproduce.
Reducing resistance: complete full courses of antibiotics (don't stop when feeling better — leaves partially resistant bacteria), don't use antibiotics for viral infections, restrict use in farming.

A communicable disease is caused by a {{c1::pathogen}} — a microorganism (bacterium, virus, fungus or protist) — that can {{c2::spread from one organism to another}}. They are also called {{c3::infectious diseases}}. Transmission routes include: {{c4::direct contact}}, {{c5::droplets in the air}}, {{c6::contaminated water or food}}, and animal {{c7::vectors}} (e.g. mosquitoes). Non-communicable diseases {{c8::cannot}} spread between organisms.

Antibodies are {{c1::proteins}} produced by {{c2::lymphocytes}} (a type of white blood cell). Each antibody has a {{c3::specific shape}} complementary to one particular {{c4::antigen}} on the pathogen's surface. Binding causes pathogens to {{c5::clump together}}, making them easier for phagocytes to engulf. After infection, {{c6::memory lymphocytes}} remain in the blood. On re-exposure to the same pathogen, they produce antibodies {{c7::much more rapidly}} — providing {{c8::immunity}}.
How were early medicines discovered? Give examples of drugs originally derived from natural sources.

Many medicines were originally extracted from plants or microorganisms before being chemically synthesised:
• Aspirin — pain relief and anti-inflammatory. Originally derived from willow bark (contains salicylic acid).
• Penicillin — antibiotic. Discovered by Alexander Fleming (1928) from the mould Penicillium.
• Digitalis — heart medicine (treats irregular heartbeat). Derived from foxglove plants.
Modern drug discovery: computers and large databases are used to screen thousands of molecules for potential activity. Traditional knowledge from different cultures has guided researchers towards useful natural compounds. Once a promising compound is found, clinical trials test its safety and effectiveness.
Levels of organisation (smallest to largest): {{c1::cell}} → {{c2::tissue}} → {{c3::organ}} → {{c4::organ system}} → {{c5::organism}}.
What are the four main tissue types in animals?
Epithelial tissue (covers surfaces), muscle tissue (contracts for movement), connective tissue (supports and connects), glandular tissue (secretes substances — e.g. enzymes, hormones).
What is the role of the digestive system and name the enzymes involved?

Breaks down large insoluble food molecules into small soluble ones for absorption. Amylase: breaks down starch → simple sugars (made in salivary glands, pancreas, small intestine). Protease: proteins → amino acids (stomach, pancreas, small intestine). Lipase: lipids → fatty acids + glycerol (pancreas, small intestine).

The stomach produces {{c1::hydrochloric acid}} (pH {{c2::2}}) to kill bacteria and provide optimum pH for {{c3::protease (pepsin)}}. The small intestine is neutralised by {{c4::bile}} produced in the {{c5::liver}} and stored in the {{c6::gallbladder}}.
How is the small intestine adapted for absorption?

Villi increase surface area. Microvilli (on villi) further increase surface area. Rich blood supply maintains concentration gradient. Thin walls (one cell thick) = short diffusion distance. Lacteals absorb fatty acids and glycerol into the lymphatic system.
What is the structure of the heart and how does blood flow through it?

Right side receives deoxygenated blood from body (vena cava) → pumps to lungs (pulmonary artery). Left side receives oxygenated blood from lungs (pulmonary vein) → pumps to body (aorta). Four chambers: right atrium, right ventricle, left atrium, left ventricle. Valves prevent backflow.

Label the heart — name each numbered part (1–8)

Blue = right side (deoxygenated)  |  Red = left side (oxygenated)
1 Vena Cava  |  2 Right Atrium  |  3 Right Ventricle  |  4 Pulmonary Artery  |  5 Pulmonary Vein  |  6 Left Atrium  |  7 Left Ventricle  |  8 Aorta
What is the double circulatory system and why is it an advantage?

The heart has two separate circuits. Right side → pulmonary circuit: pumps deoxygenated blood to the lungs to pick up oxygen, returns oxygenated blood via the pulmonary vein. Left side → systemic circuit: pumps oxygenated blood to the rest of the body, returns deoxygenated blood via the vena cava. Advantage: blood returns to the heart between the two circuits, so pressure is boosted before delivery to the body — allowing faster, more efficient oxygen delivery.

Double circulatory system: the {{c1::right}} side pumps blood to the {{c2::lungs}} (pulmonary circuit); the {{c3::left}} side pumps blood to the {{c4::body}} (systemic circuit). Blood enters the right atrium via the {{c5::vena cava}}; oxygenated blood enters the left atrium via the {{c6::pulmonary vein}}; blood leaves the left ventricle via the {{c7::aorta}}.
Name the four chambers and four major blood vessels of the heart and what each does.

Chambers: right atrium (receives deoxygenated blood from body), right ventricle (pumps to lungs), left atrium (receives oxygenated blood from lungs), left ventricle (pumps to body — thickest walls). Vessels in: vena cava (deoxygenated, from body → right atrium), pulmonary vein (oxygenated, from lungs → left atrium). Vessels out: pulmonary artery (deoxygenated, right ventricle → lungs), aorta (oxygenated, left ventricle → body).

The {{c1::left ventricle}} has thicker muscular walls than the right ventricle because it must pump blood {{c2::all the way round the body}} at higher pressure. The right ventricle only pumps to the {{c3::nearby lungs}}. Both ventricles pump the {{c4::same volume}} of blood per beat.

Heart valves sit between the atria and ventricles, and at the exit of each ventricle into the arteries. They prevent {{c1::backflow}} of blood. Faulty valves may become {{c2::leaky}} (blood flows backwards) or {{c3::stiff}} (restrict forward flow) — both reduce cardiac output. Faulty valves can be replaced using {{c4::biological or mechanical valves}}.
What are coronary arteries and what happens if they are blocked?

Coronary arteries branch off the aorta and supply the heart muscle itself with oxygenated blood and glucose. If fatty deposits (atheroma) build up inside the coronary arteries, blood flow to the heart muscle is restricted. The heart muscle is deprived of oxygen and begins to die — this is a heart attack (myocardial infarction). This condition is called coronary heart disease (CHD).

The heart's natural pacemaker is a {{c1::group of cells}} located in the wall of the {{c2::right atrium}}. It sends electrical impulses that control the {{c3::rate and rhythm}} of the heartbeat. If this fails, an {{c4::artificial pacemaker}} (small electronic device implanted under the skin) can regulate the heart rate. Cardiac output = {{c5::heart rate}} × {{c6::stroke volume}}.

Coronary heart disease: {{c1::fatty deposits (atheroma)}} build up inside the coronary arteries, narrowing the lumen and restricting blood flow to the {{c2::heart muscle}}. Treatments: {{c3::statins}} (reduce blood cholesterol, slow fatty deposit build-up), {{c4::stents}} (wire mesh tube inserted to hold artery open), {{c5::bypass surgery}} (healthy vessel grafted around blockage), {{c6::donated heart transplant}}, {{c7::artificial heart}} (used to keep patient alive while waiting for a donor).
Compare the structure and function of arteries, veins and capillaries.

Arteries: carry blood away from the heart at high pressure. Thick walls with elastic and muscle tissue to withstand and maintain pressure. Narrow lumen. Usually carry oxygenated blood (except pulmonary artery). Veins: carry blood towards the heart at low pressure. Thinner walls, wider lumen. Have valves to prevent backflow. Usually carry deoxygenated blood (except pulmonary vein). Capillaries: tiny vessels forming networks in tissues. Walls one cell thick — short diffusion distance for exchange of oxygen, glucose, CO₂, urea between blood and cells.

Arteries have {{c1::thick}} walls with {{c2::elastic and muscle tissue}} to withstand {{c3::high pressure}}; they carry blood {{c4::away}} from the heart. Veins have {{c5::thinner}} walls, a {{c6::wider lumen}}, and {{c7::valves}} to prevent backflow; they carry blood {{c8::to}} the heart. Capillaries have walls {{c9::one cell thick}} for rapid {{c10::diffusion}} of substances between blood and tissues.
What is cancer and what are the differences between benign and malignant tumours?

Cancer: uncontrolled cell division. Benign: grows in one place, doesn't spread, can be removed. Malignant: invades neighbouring tissues and can metastasise (spread through blood/lymph to other parts of the body). Risk factors: smoking, UV radiation, viral infection, genetics.

Plants have: {{c1::meristem tissue}} (growth at root and shoot tips), {{c2::xylem}} (transports water and minerals UP — dead cells, lignin walls), {{c3::phloem}} (transports sugars UP and DOWN — living cells). Transpiration pulls water up through {{c4::stomata}}.

Blood components: {{c1::red blood cells}} (carry O₂ via haemoglobin, no nucleus), {{c2::white blood cells}} (immune defence — phagocytes and lymphocytes), {{c3::platelets}} (cell fragments, trigger blood clotting), {{c4::plasma}} (liquid component carrying CO₂, hormones, glucose, urea, antibodies, proteins).
What are the adaptations of the red blood cell and why are they suited to its function?

Biconcave disc shape: increased surface area for O₂ absorption and diffusion.
No nucleus: more space for haemoglobin molecules → can carry more O₂.
Contains haemoglobin: binds O₂ in lungs (oxyhaemoglobin), releases it in tissues where O₂ concentration is low.
Small and flexible: can squeeze through narrow capillaries (5–8 μm wide).
Exam tip: haemoglobin + O₂ ⇌ oxyhaemoglobin (reversible, direction depends on O₂ concentration).
What are the factors affecting the rate of transpiration and how would you investigate them?

Factors that increase transpiration rate: higher temperature (more evaporation), lower humidity (steeper concentration gradient), higher light intensity (stomata open wider), higher wind speed (removes water vapour — steeper gradient).
Investigation — potometer: measures water uptake (proxy for transpiration). Bubble moves along capillary tube as water is absorbed. Change one variable at a time (temperature/humidity/wind/light) and measure rate of bubble movement.
Key point: guard cells open stomata in light (for photosynthesis) → most transpiration occurs during the day.
How do plants transport water and minerals? What is the transpiration stream?

Route: water enters by osmosis through root hair cells → moves through root by osmosis → enters xylem vessels → moves up stem by transpiration pull → exits through stomata in leaves as water vapour.
Xylem: dead, hollow cells with lignified walls forming continuous tubes. Lignin is waterproof — prevents water loss through walls. No end walls — continuous column of water.
Transpiration pull: evaporation from leaves creates tension → pulls water up from roots. Works because water molecules are cohesive (stick together) — cohesion-tension model.
Mineral ions dissolved in water travel with it.
What are enzymes and how do they work?

Enzymes are biological catalysts — they speed up metabolic reactions without being used up. They are proteins with a specific active site shape complementary to their substrate (lock and key model). When the substrate fits the active site, an enzyme-substrate complex forms, the reaction occurs, and products are released. Optimum temperature (~37°C for human enzymes) and pH — outside this range, the enzyme denatures (active site permanently changes shape, can't bind substrate). High temperature = molecules vibrate too fast → bonds break → denature. Wrong pH = charges in active site disrupted → denature.

Enzymes are biological {{c1::catalysts}}. They have an {{c2::active site}} complementary in shape to their {{c3::substrate}} — called the {{c4::lock and key}} model. Increasing temperature above the optimum causes {{c5::denaturation}} — the active site {{c6::changes shape permanently}}. Each enzyme has an optimum {{c7::temperature}} and {{c8::pH}}. Digestive enzymes: {{c9::amylase}} (starch → sugars), {{c10::protease}} (proteins → amino acids), {{c11::lipase}} (lipids → fatty acids + glycerol).
What are the required practicals for Biology Paper 1? Describe the food tests.

Food tests (required practical):
• Starch: add iodine solution → blue/black colour indicates starch present.
• Glucose (reducing sugars): add Benedict's solution + heat → colour changes from blue to brick-red/orange if reducing sugar present.
• Protein (Biuret test): add NaOH then CuSO₄ → turns purple/lilac if protein present.
• Fat (ethanol emulsion test): dissolve food in ethanol, pour into water → white emulsion/cloudy if fat present.
Control: test with distilled water to confirm reagents don't give false positive.
Hazards: Bunsen burner — tie back hair, no loose clothing, use heatproof mat. Hot water bath — risk of scalding, use tongs to handle test tubes. NaOH (Biuret) — corrosive, wear goggles. Ethanol (emulsion test) — flammable, keep away from naked flame.
Label the gas exchange system — name each numbered part

① Tracheawindpipe — carries air to/from lungs; lined with cilia and mucus to trap pathogens
② Bronchustwo main branches, one entering each lung
③ Bronchiolessmaller branches that distribute air throughout the lung
④ Alveolitiny air sacs where gas exchange occurs — O₂ into blood, CO₂ out
⑤ Diaphragmcontracts and flattens during inhalation to increase lung volume
How are alveoli adapted for efficient gas exchange?

Alveoli are the site of gas exchange between air and blood. They are highly adapted:
• Huge number → very large total surface area (≈70 m² in humans) for diffusion
• Walls one cell thick (squamous epithelium) → very short diffusion distance
• Moist lining → gases dissolve before diffusing across
• Rich capillary network → blood constantly flowing past maintains a steep concentration gradient (O₂ high in alveolus, low in blood; CO₂ high in blood, low in alveolus)
• Good ventilation (breathing) → constantly refreshes air in alveoli, maintaining the concentration gradient
O₂ diffuses from alveolus → blood (combines with haemoglobin). CO₂ diffuses from blood → alveolus → exhaled.
What are non-communicable diseases and what are the main risk factors?

Non-communicable diseases (NCDs) cannot be spread from person to person. Main examples in the spec: cardiovascular disease (heart disease and stroke), Type 2 diabetes, cancers.
Lifestyle risk factors: poor diet (high fat/salt/sugar → obesity, high cholesterol), lack of exercise, smoking (lung cancer, CVD), excessive alcohol (liver disease, some cancers).
Other factors: age, sex, genetics (inherited predisposition).
Key exam point: risk factors increase the probability of disease but do not guarantee it. Correlation ≠ causation — a factor may be associated with a disease without directly causing it. Some people have multiple risk factors but never develop the disease.

Non-communicable diseases {{c1::cannot spread}} from person to person. Examples: {{c2::cardiovascular disease}}, {{c3::Type 2 diabetes}}, {{c4::cancer}}. Lifestyle risk factors include {{c5::smoking}}, {{c6::poor diet}}, {{c7::lack of exercise}} and {{c8::excessive alcohol}}. Other factors: {{c9::age}}, {{c10::genetics}}. A risk factor increases the {{c11::probability}} of disease — but correlation does not mean {{c12::causation}}.
Label the leaf cross-section — name each numbered part

① Upper epidermistransparent protective layer; covered by a waxy cuticle to reduce water loss
② Palisade mesophylltightly packed cells with many chloroplasts — main site of photosynthesis; near top of leaf to absorb maximum light
③ Spongy mesophyllloosely packed cells with large air spaces — CO₂, O₂ and water vapour diffuse through the leaf here
④ Lower epidermislower protective layer containing stomata — allows gas exchange and transpiration
⑤ Guard cells / stomaguard cells change shape to open/close the stoma (pore). Open in light for photosynthesis; close when water is scarce to reduce transpiration

In a leaf cross-section: the {{c1::palisade mesophyll}} layer is nearest the top surface and contains the most {{c2::chloroplasts}} — it is the main site of {{c3::photosynthesis}}. The {{c4::spongy mesophyll}} has large {{c5::air spaces}} that allow {{c6::CO₂, O₂ and water vapour}} to diffuse through the leaf. The {{c7::epidermis}} is covered by a waxy {{c8::cuticle}} which reduces {{c9::water loss}}. {{c10::Guard cells}} control the opening and closing of {{c11::stomata}}, regulating both {{c12::gas exchange}} and {{c13::transpiration}}.
Why are palisade cells positioned near the top of the leaf? What features make them suited for photosynthesis?

Palisade cells are close to the upper surface to receive the maximum light intensity — light is the main limiting factor for photosynthesis. Their features: tall, column-shaped cells packed tightly together (maximises the number of cells per unit area); large numbers of chloroplasts per cell (maximises light absorption); thin cell walls and large vacuole push chloroplasts to the edges (closer to light and CO₂). The spongy mesophyll below has fewer chloroplasts because less light reaches that depth.

Stomata are tiny {{c1::pores}} in the leaf epidermis, found mainly on the {{c2::lower}} surface. They are opened and closed by pairs of {{c3::guard cells}}. Guard cells become {{c4::turgid}} (absorb water by osmosis) to {{c5::open}} the stoma. They become {{c6::flaccid}} (lose water) to {{c7::close}} it. Stomata open in {{c8::light}} to allow CO₂ in for photosynthesis. They close when the plant is short of {{c9::water}}, reducing water loss by {{c10::transpiration}}.

Levels of ecological organisation: {{c1::individual}} → {{c2::population}} (same species in one area) → {{c3::community}} (all populations in one area) → {{c4::ecosystem}} (community + abiotic environment).
What are abiotic and biotic factors?

Abiotic (non-living): temperature, light intensity, moisture, pH, wind speed, CO₂ levels, oxygen levels. Biotic (living): food availability, predators, competitors, disease. Both affect distribution and abundance of organisms.
What is a food chain and what do the terms mean?

Shows feeding relationships. Producer → Primary consumer → Secondary consumer → Tertiary consumer. Arrows show direction of energy transfer. Producers are photosynthetic plants. Decomposers (bacteria/fungi) break down dead matter, returning nutrients to soil.

Biomass decreases at each trophic level because energy is lost through {{c1::respiration}}, {{c2::waste products}} (urine/faeces), and {{c3::heat}}. Typically only {{c4::10%}} of energy is transferred between levels.
What is the carbon cycle?

Carbon is recycled through: photosynthesis (CO₂ absorbed by plants). Respiration (CO₂ released by all organisms). Decomposition (CO₂ released by bacteria/fungi). Combustion (burning fossil fuels releases CO₂). Ocean absorption. Limestone/fossil fuels lock carbon away for millions of years.
What are the effects of human activities on ecosystems?

Deforestation (reduces CO₂ absorption, destroys habitats). Pollution (water: fertilisers/sewage causing eutrophication; air: acid rain, greenhouse gases). Peat bog destruction (releases stored CO₂). Overfishing. Intensive farming reduces biodiversity.

Eutrophication: excess {{c1::nitrates/phosphates}} from fertilisers enter water → algae bloom → blocks light → plants die → decomposers use up {{c2::oxygen}} → aquatic life dies. Called {{c3::eutrophication}}.
What methods are used to protect biodiversity?

Breeding programmes for endangered species. Seed banks. Conservation of habitats (nature reserves, marine protected areas). Reforestation. Reducing pollution. Sustainable fishing (quotas, mesh size). International agreements (e.g. CITES on endangered species trade).
What are quadrats and transects? How are they used to estimate population size?

Quadrat: a square frame placed randomly in a habitat. Count individuals of a species inside → calculate mean per m². Scale up to estimate total population: mean density × total area.
Random sampling: use random number table to choose coordinates — avoids bias.
Transect: a line across a habitat. Place quadrats at regular intervals along the line. Used to investigate how species distribution changes across a gradient (e.g. shore to inland, light to shade).
Mark-recapture method (mobile animals): capture → mark → release → recapture. Population = (1st catch × 2nd catch) ÷ number of marked individuals in 2nd catch.
Describe a predator-prey population graph and explain the relationship.

Pattern: both populations cycle. Prey population increases → predator population increases (more food). Predator population increases → prey population decreases (more predation). Prey population decreases → predator population decreases (food shortage). Predator decrease → prey population recovers → cycle repeats.
Key features of graph: predator numbers lag behind prey numbers (takes time for predators to respond). Both oscillate — never reach zero in a stable ecosystem. Lines are out of phase (prey peaks before predator).
What is the nitrogen cycle?

Nitrogen fixation: N₂ gas → nitrates/ammonia — done by bacteria in soil and root nodules of legumes (Rhizobium). Lightning also fixes nitrogen.
Nitrification: ammonium ions → nitrites → nitrates — done by nitrifying bacteria in soil. Nitrates absorbed by plants.
Decomposition: dead organisms → ammonium ions — done by decomposers (bacteria/fungi).
Denitrification: nitrates → N₂ gas — done by denitrifying bacteria in waterlogged/anaerobic soils.
Assimilation: plants absorb nitrates → make proteins/DNA → eaten by animals.
How does global warming affect ecosystems?

Rising temperatures: species distribution shifts poleward (tracking their climate niche). Timing of seasonal events changes — earlier flowering/breeding may not match food availability → mismatches in food chains.
Melting ice: loss of habitat for polar species (polar bears, Arctic foxes). Rising sea levels → loss of coastal habitats. Ocean acidification (CO₂ dissolved → carbonic acid) → damages coral reefs and shellfish (can't form shells).
Biodiversity loss: species unable to adapt fast enough may become extinct. Invasive species spread into newly suitable areas, outcompeting native species.
What is homeostasis and why is it important?

Homeostasis is the regulation of the internal conditions of a cell or organism to maintain optimum conditions for enzyme action and cell function. Examples: blood glucose concentration, body temperature (37°C), water levels.
The nervous system: stimulus → {{c1::receptor}} → {{c2::sensory neurone}} → {{c3::CNS (brain/spinal cord)}} → {{c4::motor neurone}} → {{c5::effector}} (muscle or gland) → response.

A synapse is a junction between neurones. The nerve impulse triggers release of {{c1::neurotransmitters}} which diffuse across the {{c2::synaptic gap}} and bind to receptors on the {{c3::next neurone}}, creating a new impulse.
What is a reflex arc and why are reflexes important?

A reflex arc: receptor → sensory neurone → relay neurone (in spinal cord) → motor neurone → effector. Reflexes are automatic and fast because they bypass the conscious brain — protects the body from harm (e.g. pulling hand away from heat).
How does the body control blood glucose levels?

Monitored by the pancreas. If too high: pancreas releases insulin → glucose taken up by cells and liver (converted to glycogen) → blood glucose falls. If too low: pancreas releases glucagon → glycogen converted back to glucose → blood glucose rises. Type 1 diabetes: pancreas produces no insulin. Type 2: cells stop responding to insulin.

Type 1 diabetes is treated by {{c1::insulin injections}} and monitoring diet. Type 2 diabetes is managed by {{c2::diet and exercise}} — losing weight can reverse it. Type 2 is linked to {{c3::obesity}}.
How does the body regulate temperature?

Thermoregulation centre in the hypothalamus detects blood temperature changes. Too hot: vasodilation (more blood to skin surface), sweating (evaporation cools). Too cold: vasoconstriction (less blood to skin), shivering (muscle contractions generate heat), hair erection (trap air layer).
What is the role of hormones in the menstrual cycle?

FSH (from pituitary): stimulates egg maturation and oestrogen production. Oestrogen: thickens uterus lining, inhibits FSH, stimulates LH. LH (from pituitary): triggers ovulation (day 14). Progesterone: maintains uterus lining. Falls if no fertilisation → lining shed (menstruation).

Menstrual cycle hormones: {{c1::FSH}} (from pituitary) — stimulates egg maturation and {{c2::oestrogen}} production. {{c3::Oestrogen}} — thickens uterus lining, inhibits FSH, stimulates {{c4::LH}}. {{c5::LH}} (from pituitary) — triggers {{c6::ovulation}} (~day 14). {{c7::Progesterone}} — maintains uterus lining after ovulation; falls if no fertilisation → {{c8::menstruation}}.
What are the methods of contraception and how do they work?

Hormonal methods (all prevent egg maturation by inhibiting FSH/LH):
• Combined pill (oestrogen + progesterone) — very effective, taken daily
• Progesterone-only pill (mini pill) — thickens cervical mucus, inhibits ovulation
• Injection/implant/patch/hormonal IUD (Mirena) — slow release of progesterone, long-acting
Non-hormonal methods:
• Condom (male/female) — barrier method, also protects against STIs
• Diaphragm — barrier, used with spermicide
• Copper IUD — toxic to sperm, also prevents implantation; non-hormonal, long-lasting
• Abstinence — no sexual intercourse
• Sterilisation (vasectomy/tubal ligation) — surgical, permanent
Key exam point: hormonal methods prevent egg maturation; barrier methods prevent sperm reaching egg; IUD prevents implantation or fertilisation.

Contraceptive pills contain {{c1::oestrogen and/or progesterone}} which {{c2::inhibit FSH production}}, preventing egg maturation. IVF uses {{c3::FSH}} to stimulate multiple egg production. The {{c4::copper IUD}} is non-hormonal and works by being {{c5::toxic to sperm}}. {{c6::Condoms}} are the only contraceptive that also protect against {{c7::STIs}}.
How do the kidneys control water balance (osmoregulation)? What is the role of ADH?

Kidneys filter blood under high pressure → produce urine. Useful substances (glucose, water, some ions) are selectively reabsorbed back into the blood.
ADH (antidiuretic hormone) — released by pituitary gland:
• If blood water potential is TOO LOW (dehydrated): ADH released → kidneys reabsorb MORE water → concentrated, small volume urine.
• If blood water potential is TOO HIGH (overhydrated): less ADH → kidneys reabsorb LESS water → dilute, large volume urine.
Negative feedback loop: hypothalamus detects → pituitary releases/stops ADH → kidney responds → blood water potential changes → hypothalamus detects again.
How does thyroxine illustrate the principle of negative feedback?

Thyroxine is produced by the thyroid gland and regulates metabolic rate.
Negative feedback:
1. Hypothalamus detects low thyroxine → releases TRH
2. Pituitary releases TSH (thyroid stimulating hormone)
3. Thyroid gland produces more thyroxine
4. High thyroxine level detected by hypothalamus → TRH stops → TSH stops → thyroxine production falls.
Key principle of negative feedback: the output (thyroxine) feeds back to inhibit the control system → maintains a stable concentration. All hormones in homeostasis use this mechanism.
What is adrenaline and what are its effects?

Adrenaline is a hormone produced by the adrenal glands (above the kidneys) in response to stress, danger, or excitement. It prepares the body for 'fight or flight': increases heart rate (more O₂ to muscles), increases blood glucose (glycogen converted to glucose), dilates pupils, diverts blood from digestive system to muscles. Unlike most hormones, adrenaline acts very quickly — released directly into the blood and triggers rapid physiological changes.
What are the plant hormones and their commercial uses?

Auxin (IAA): produced at shoot tip. Causes cell elongation. More in shaded side → shoot bends towards light (phototropism). Commercial uses: rooting powder (cuttings grow roots), selective weedkillers (causes broad-leaf weeds to grow uncontrollably while grass is unaffected).
Gibberellins: promote stem elongation and seed germination. Commercial use: seedless grapes, malting barley (beer production).
Ethene: promotes fruit ripening. Commercial use: ripen fruit during transport/storage (expose to ethene gas).

Required practical — reaction time: use a {{c1::ruler drop test}}. Person A holds ruler at 0cm mark; Person B's fingers are at bottom ready to catch. Person A drops — Person B catches. Distance fallen → converted to {{c2::reaction time}} using a table or formula. Test factors affecting reaction time: e.g. {{c3::caffeine}}, {{c4::tiredness}}, {{c5::distractions}}.

DNA is made of {{c1::nucleotides}}, each containing a {{c2::sugar}}, {{c3::phosphate}} and one of four {{c4::bases}} (A, T, C, G). It is a {{c5::double helix}} structure. A pairs with {{c6::T}}, C pairs with {{c7::G}}.

A gene is a {{c1::section of DNA}} that codes for a specific {{c2::protein}}. Humans have {{c3::46}} chromosomes in each cell, organised into {{c4::23 pairs}}. Sex chromosomes: {{c5::XX}} = female, {{c6::XY}} = male.
What is the difference between meiosis and mitosis?
