The EM spectrum in order of increasing frequency (lowest to highest): {{c1::radio waves}}, {{c2::microwaves}}, {{c3::infrared}}, {{c4::visible light}}, {{c5::ultraviolet}}, {{c6::X-rays}}, {{c7::gamma rays}}. All travel at {{c8::3×10⁸ m/s}} in a vacuum.
Going from radio to gamma: frequency {{c1::increases}}, wavelength {{c2::decreases}}, energy {{c3::increases}}. Higher frequency = more {{c4::ionising}} and more {{c5::dangerous}} to living tissue.
What are the properties, uses and hazards of radio waves?

Longest wavelength, lowest frequency and energy. Produced by oscillating electrons. Uses: TV and radio broadcasting, Bluetooth, WiFi. Hazards: generally considered safe at normal exposure levels — non-ionising.
What are the properties, uses and hazards of microwaves?

Between radio and infrared. Absorbed by water molecules — causes heating. Uses: cooking (microwave ovens), satellite communication, mobile phones, radar. Hazards: can heat body tissue internally — non-ionising but can cause heating of cells.
What are the properties, uses and hazards of infrared radiation?

Just below visible light. All objects emit infrared — hotter objects emit more. Detected by skin as heat. Uses: thermal imaging cameras, TV remotes, optical fibre communication, night vision, toasters/grills, physiotherapy. Hazards: can cause skin burns and eye damage at high intensities.
What are the properties, uses and hazards of visible light?

The only part of the EM spectrum detectable by the human eye. Range from red (lowest frequency) to violet (highest). Uses: sight, photography, optical fibres for communication, lasers (surgery, barcode scanners). Hazards: high intensity lasers or bright light can damage the retina.
What are the properties, uses and hazards of ultraviolet (UV) radiation?

Higher frequency than visible light — ionising. Produced by the Sun and UV lamps. Uses: detecting forged bank notes (security markings fluoresce), sterilising water/medical equipment, tanning beds. Hazards: damages DNA → skin cancer, damages eyes → cataracts. Ozone layer absorbs most UV from the Sun.
What are the properties, uses and hazards of X-rays?

High frequency, short wavelength — ionising. Produced by firing electrons at metal targets. Absorbed by dense materials (bone/metal), pass through soft tissue. Uses: medical imaging (bone fractures, dental checks), security scanning (airports), checking welds in industry. Hazards: ionises cells → can cause mutations and cancer. Radiographers move away or use lead screens.
What are the properties, uses and hazards of gamma rays?

Highest frequency, shortest wavelength — most ionising. Emitted by unstable atomic nuclei (radioactive decay). Uses: cancer treatment (radiotherapy — kills tumour cells), sterilising medical equipment, food irradiation (kills bacteria), PET scans. Hazards: highly ionising — damages/kills cells and DNA → cancer. Reduced by thick lead or concrete.

Non-ionising radiation ({{c1::radio, microwave, infrared, visible}}) does not have enough energy to remove electrons from atoms. Ionising radiation ({{c2::UV, X-rays, gamma}}) has enough energy to ionise atoms, potentially causing {{c3::cancer}} by damaging {{c4::DNA}}.
What is the difference between reflection, absorption and transmission of EM waves?

Reflection: wave bounces off a surface (angle of incidence = angle of reflection). Absorption: wave transfers energy to the material — causes heating. Transmission: wave passes through the material. Different materials reflect, absorb and transmit different parts of the EM spectrum — e.g. glass transmits visible light but absorbs UV.

Amplitude = {{c1::maximum displacement}} from rest position. Frequency = number of {{c2::complete waves per second}} (Hz). Wavelength = distance from one {{c3::crest to the next crest}} (or trough to trough). Period = {{c4::1 ÷ frequency}} (time for one complete wave).
What is the required practical for waves? How do you measure the speed of waves?

Ripple tank (water waves): measure wavelength (distance between wave crests on stroboscope or freeze-frame). Count frequency (oscillations per second). Speed = fλ.
Sound waves in a solid (slinky): send a wave pulse, measure time for it to travel known distance. Speed = distance/time.
Measuring speed of sound in air: stand far from a wall. Clap boards together and time the echo (echo = sound travels twice the distance). Speed ≈ 330 m/s. Or use two microphones connected to an oscilloscope — measure time delay for sound to travel between them.
What is refraction and how does it explain optical fibres and lenses?

Refraction: waves change speed when crossing a boundary between two different media — if entering at an angle, direction also changes.
Light into denser medium (e.g. glass): slows down → bends towards the normal.
Total internal reflection: if angle of incidence exceeds the critical angle → light reflects back into the denser medium. This is the basis of optical fibres — light signal bounces along inside the fibre with no signal loss through the glass walls.
Lenses: converging (convex) lens brings parallel rays to a focal point. Diverging (concave) lens spreads rays apart. Used in cameras, microscopes, spectacles.
